topics.title: Determination of pH and Turbidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of pH and Turbidity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Definition of pH **pH** is formally defined as the negative base-10 logarithm of the hydrogen ion activity (or molar concentration in dilute solutions) in an aqueous medium. $$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[H^{+}] = -\log_{10} a_{H^{+}}$$ where $[H^{+}]$ is the hydrogen ion concentration in mol/L and $a_{H^{+}}$ is the activity of hydrogen ions. The pH scale was introduced by the Danish biochemist Søren Peder Lauritz Sørensen in 1909 at the Carlsberg Laboratory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** pH is a measure of the **acidic or basic (alkaline) nature** of water. It is one of the most important operational parameters in water quality analysis because it influences almost every chemical and biological reaction in water treatment. | pH Range | Nature | Examples | |----------|--------|----------| | 0 – 3 | Strongly Acidic | Concentrated HCl, Battery acid | | 4 – 6 | Mildly Acidic | Rainwater (~5.6), Black coffee | | 7 | Neutral | Pure water at 25 °C | | 8 – 10 | Mildly Alkaline | Sea water, Baking soda solution | | 11 – 14 | Strongly Alkaline | Soap solution, Bleach | The permissible pH range for drinking water as per **IS 10500:2012** is **6.5 to 8.5** with no relaxation. ### 1.2 Definition of Turbidity **Turbidity** is an optical property of water that indicates the presence of suspended and colloidal matter (clay, silt, organic matter, algae, microorganisms) that scatter and absorb light rather than transmitting it in a straight line. It is **not a direct measure of total suspended solids (TSS)** but correlates with it, as the particles responsible for light scattering are the same ones that contribute to TSS. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (pH):** Imagine pH as a "balance scale" of a tug-of-war between acids and bases. If the acid side is heavy, pH is low (0-6); if the base side is heavy, pH is high (8-14); if perfectly balanced, pH is 7. Pure water is the perfectly balanced rope — neither acid nor base wins. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Turbidity):** Imagine shining a torchlight through a swimming pool. In crystal-clear water, the beam passes straight and invisible. But if the water has dust, silt, or fine particles, the beam becomes visible because particles scatter the light sideways into your eyes. Turbidity is a numerical measure of how "cloudy" or "misty" that water is. The official unit is the **Nephelometric Turbidity Unit (NTU)**. ### 1.3 Standards & Methods | Parameter | Standard Reference Method | Instrumental Method | |-----------|---------------------------|---------------------| | pH | IS 3025 (Part 11):1983 (Electrometric) | Digital pH meter (Glass electrode + Reference electrode) | | Turbidity | IS 3025 (Part 10):1984 (Nephelometric) | Nephelometer / Turbidimeter | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Focus:** As per the KTU 2024 Environmental Engineering Lab syllabus, the determination of pH is performed using a **digital pH meter (Electrometric method)** and turbidity using a **Nephelometer (Nephelometric method)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Principle of pH Measurement (Electrometric Method) The electrometric method measures the electrical potential (EMF) developed across a thin glass membrane that is selectively permeable to H⁺ ions. A pH meter consists of two electrodes dipped in the test solution: - **Glass Electrode (Measuring Electrode):** A thin bulb of special glass that develops a potential proportional to the H⁺ activity of the solution. - **Reference Electrode (Saturated Calomel Electrode – SCE or Ag/AgCl):** Maintains a constant, stable reference potential. - **High Input Impedance Voltmeter:** Reads the millivolt potential difference. When the glass membrane contacts the sample, a potential difference develops across it that follows the **Nernst Equation**. ### 2.2 The Nernst Equation for pH Electrode The EMF (E) of the glass-reference electrode pair is given by: $$E_{cell} = E_{ref} - E_{glass} = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$$ At the standard reference temperature of **25 °C (298.15 K)**, substituting the values of R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) and F = 96485 C/mol: $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916 \text{ V} \approx 0.0591 \text{ V}$$ So the working equation at 25 °C becomes: $$E_{cell} = E^{\circ} - 0.0591 \times \text{pH}$$ This shows that **pH is directly proportional to the negative of the cell potential**, and the slope is **59.16 mV per pH unit** at 25 °C. Modern pH meters have **Automatic Temperature Compensation (ATC)** to correct the slope for variations in sample temperature. ### 2.3 Buffer Solutions for Calibration A **buffer solution** is a solution that resists changes in pH upon addition of small amounts of acid or base. Common buffer solutions used for pH meter calibration: | Buffer | pH Value @ 25 °C | Type | |--------|------------------|------| | Potassium Hydrogen Phthalate (KHP) | 4.00 | Acidic | | Neutral Phosphate | 6.86 / 7.00 | Neutral | | Borax | 9.20 | Basic | | Sodium Tetraborate | 10.00 | Basic | A **two-point calibration** is typically performed: one acidic buffer (pH 4) and one neutral (pH 7) — the slope of the line is determined. A **three-point calibration** is preferred for accuracy across the full range. ### 2.4 Principle of Turbidity Measurement (Nephelometric Method) The **nephelometric method** measures the intensity of light scattered at **90° (right angle)** to the incident light beam. It is more sensitive than the older **Jackson Candle method**, which measured the depth of water column needed to extinguish the image of a standard candle flame. The intensity of scattered light is proportional to the turbidity (in NTU) under controlled conditions. The instrument is calibrated using **Formazin polymer standards** prepared from: - **Hydrazine sulfate** $[(NH_2)_2 \cdot H_2SO_4]$ - **Hexamethylenetetramine** $[(CH_2)_6N_4]$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |------|-----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | pH | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[H^{+}]$ | Dimensionless | Negative log of H⁺ concentration | | 2 | pOH | $\text{pOH} = -\log_{10}[OH^{-}]$ | Dimensionless | $\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = 14$ at 25 °C | | 3 | Nernst Equation (General) | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{nF}\log Q$ | Volts | Q is reaction quotient | | 4 | Nernst Equation (pH, 25°C) | $E = E^{\circ} - 0.0591 \cdot \text{pH}$ | Volts | Slope = 59.16 mV per pH unit | | 5 | Nernst Slope at T (°C) | $S = 0.0591 \cdot \dfrac{T + 273.15}{298.15}$ | V/pH | ATC correction factor | | 6 | Formazin Stock Turbidity | 4000 NTU | NTU | 1 L solution (1 g + 10 g reagents) | | 7 | Dilution Formula | $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$ | mL × NTU | For standard preparation | | 8 | Drinking Water Standard (pH) | 6.5 – 8.5 | — | IS 10500:2012 | | 9 | Drinking Water Standard (Turbidity) | $\leq 1$ NTU (max 5) | NTU | IS 10500:2012 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** pH control is critical in **coagulation-flocculation** (optimum pH for alum is 5.5–7.5), **disinfection** (chlorination works best at pH < 7.5; chloramines are formed at higher pH), and **corrosion control** of distribution pipes. Turbidity control protects consumers from pathogens (giardia, cryptosporidium) and is a key indicator of filter performance in water treatment plants. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation for the pH Cell **Starting Point — Electrochemical Reaction at the Glass Electrode:** The glass electrode responds to H⁺ ions through an ion-exchange equilibrium across the glass membrane. The half-cell potential of the glass electrode is given by the Nernst equation for the half-reaction: $$H^{+}(aq) \rightleftharpoons H^{+}(glass)$$ For a generalized reversible redox reaction: $$aA + bB + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$ The Nernst equation at any temperature T is: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$ where: - $E$ = cell potential under non-standard conditions (V) - $E^{\circ}$ = standard cell potential (V) - $R$ = Universal gas constant = $8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ - $T$ = Absolute temperature (K) - $n$ = Number of electrons transferred (= 1 for H⁺) - $F$ = Faraday's constant = $96485 \text{ C/mol}$ - $Q$ = Reaction quotient (activity of H⁺ at the membrane surface) **Step 1:** Substitute $n = 1$ and $Q = a_{H^{+}}$: $$E_{glass} = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{F}\ln(a_{H^{+}})$$ **Step 2:** Convert natural log to base-10 log using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$: $$E_{glass} = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F}\log_{10}(a_{H^{+}})$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the definition $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}(a_{H^{+}})$, which means $\log_{10}(a_{H^{+}}) = -\text{pH}$: $$E_{glass} = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F}(-\text{pH}) = E^{\circ} + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$$ **Step 4:** The cell EMF measured by the pH meter is the difference between the reference electrode potential (constant $E_{ref}$) and the glass electrode potential: $$E_{cell} = E_{ref} - E_{glass} = E_{ref} - E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$$ **Step 5:** Combine the constants ($E_{ref} - E^{\circ}$) into a single constant $K$: $$E_{cell} = K - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$$ **Step 6:** At $T = 25 \text{ °C} = 298.15 \text{ K}$, evaluate the slope: $$\text{Slope} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916 \text{ V/pH unit}$$ Therefore the final working equation is: $$\boxed{E_{cell} = K - 0.05916 \times \text{pH}}$$ > **Why this matters:** A change of **1 pH unit** corresponds to a change of **59.16 mV** at 25 °C. This linear relationship is the foundation of all modern digital pH meters. --- ### 3.2 Numerical Example — pH from Hydrogen Ion Concentration **Problem:** A water sample has $[H^{+}] = 3.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ M}$ at 25 °C. Calculate the pH. **Solution:** $$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}(3.2 \times 10^{-6})$$ $$\text{pH} = -[\log_{10}(3.2) + \log_{10}(10^{-6})]$$ $$\text{pH} = -[0.5051 + (-6)] = -[-5.4949]$$ $$\boxed{\text{pH} = 5.49}$$ **Conclusion:** The sample is **acidic** (pH < 7) but well within the natural water range. --- ### 3.3 Numerical Example — Cell Potential of pH Electrode **Problem:** A pH meter (calibrated with slope = 59.16 mV/pH) gives a cell EMF of 154 mV for an unknown sample, and 354 mV for a buffer of pH = 4.00. Find the pH of the unknown sample. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Calculate the constant $K$ using the buffer measurement: $$E_{buffer} = K - 0.05916 \times \text{pH}_{buffer}$$ $$0.354 = K - 0.05916 \times 4.00$$ $$K = 0.354 + 0.2366 = 0.5906 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the same constant to the unknown sample: $$0.154 = 0.5906 - 0.05916 \times \text{pH}_{unknown}$$ $$0.05916 \times \text{pH}_{unknown} = 0.5906 - 0.154 = 0.4366$$ $$\text{pH}_{unknown} = \frac{0.4366}{0.05916} = 7.38$$ $$\boxed{\text{pH}_{unknown} = 7.38}$$ **Interpretation:** A typical freshwater pH in equilibrium with atmospheric CO₂. --- ### 3.4 Numerical Example — Formazin Standard Dilution for Turbidity Calibration **Problem:** Prepare 100 mL each of 10 NTU, 50 NTU, and 100 NTU turbidity standards from a 4000 NTU Formazin stock solution. **Solution Framework:** Use the dilution equation $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$. **Step 1:** For **10 NTU** standard: $$C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$$ $$4000 \times V_1 = 10 \times 100$$ $$V_1 = \frac{1000}{4000} = 0.25 \text{ mL}$$ **Step 2:** For **50 NTU** standard: $$4000 \times V_1 = 50 \times 100$$ $$V_1 = \frac{5000}{4000} = 1.25 \text{ mL}$$ **Step 3:** For **100 NTU** standard: $$4000 \times V_1 = 100 \times 100$$ $$V_1 = \frac{10000}{4000} = 2.5 \text{ mL}$$ **Practical Procedure:** Take the calculated volume of stock using a micropipette, dilute each to 100 mL with turbidity-free distilled water, mix thoroughly, and use these as standards to calibrate the nephelometer (calibration curve of NTU vs. instrument reading). --- ### 3.5 Calibration Curve Data — Sample Format for Lab Record | Standard | NTU Value | Nephelometer Reading (NTU) | |----------|-----------|----------------------------| | Blank | 0.0 | 0.0 | | Std 1 | 10 | 9.8 | | Std 2 | 50 | 50.3 | | Std 3 | 100 | 99.6 | | Std 4 | 200 | 201.2 | | **Sample A** | **?** | **12.5** | | **Sample B** | **?** | **45.7** | A straight line is plotted with NTU (X-axis) vs. Reading (Y-axis). The sample readings (12.5 and 45.7) directly give the turbidity in NTU since the instrument is pre-calibrated. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Errors and Sources of Uncertainty | Error Source | Effect on Reading | Mitigation | |--------------|-------------------|------------| | Glass electrode dried out | Drift, slow response | Store in KCl solution; soak 2 h before use | | Reference electrode clogged | Erratic readings | Refill with saturated KCl; clean junction | | Buffer contamination | Slope error | Use fresh buffers; never pour back into stock | | Sample temperature variation | Slope changes (2 % per °C) | Use ATC probe; bring to 25 °C | | Air bubbles on electrode | Spurious low pH | Tap gently to dislodge bubbles | | Condensation on cuvette (turbidity) | False high reading | Wipe outside with lint-free tissue | | Formazin standard aged | Loss of scattering particles | Prepare fresh; stable for ~1 year in dark | | Vials scratched or dirty | Light scatter errors | Use matched, scratch-free vials | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Digital pH Meter ```mermaid flowchart LR SAMP[Sample Solution] --> GLASS[Glass Electrode
Measuring] SAMP --> REF[Reference Electrode
Saturated Calomel] GLASS --> HV[High Impedance
Amplifier] REF --> HV HV --> ADC[Analog to Digital
Converter] ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller
with ATC] TEMP[ATC Temperature
Sensor] --> MCU MCU --> DSP[Digital Display
pH Reading] MCU --> CAL[Calibration
Memory] BUF[Buffer Solutions
pH 4 and 7] -.->|Calibration Input| GLASS ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram of a Nephelometric Turbidimeter ```mermaid flowchart LR LS[Light Source
Tungsten or LED] --> LENS1[Collimating
Lens] LENS1 --> CUV[Cuvette with
Sample] CUV --> SCAT[Scattered Light
at 90 degrees] CUV --> TRANS[Transmitted Light
Detector optional] SCAT --> PD[Photodetector
Photocell] PD --> AMP[Signal
Amplifier] AMP --> ADC[ADC] ADC --> PROC[Processor with
Calibration Curve] PROC --> DISP[Digital Display
NTU Reading] STD[Formazin Standards
0 to 4000 NTU] -.->|Calibration| CUV ``` ### 4.3 Experimental Procedure Flowchart for pH and Turbidity ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Collect Water Sample
in Clean BOD Bottle] --> B[Start pH Determination] B --> C[Calibrate pH Meter
with pH 7 Buffer] C --> D[Slope Check with
pH 4 Buffer] D --> E[Rinse Electrode
with Distilled Water] E --> F[Immerse Electrode
in Sample] F --> G[Wait for Stable
Reading] G --> H[Record pH Value] H --> I[Rinse and Store
Electrode in KCl] I --> J[Start Turbidity
Determination] J --> K[Calibrate Nephelometer
with Formazin Standards] K --> L[Fill Cuvette with
Sample up to Mark] L --> M[Place in Nephelometer
and Close Lid] M --> N[Record NTU Reading] N --> O[Repeat for Triplicate
and Take Average] O --> P[Report Results
with Units and Standards] ``` ### 4.4 Schematic — Glass Electrode Construction (Conceptual Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB OUT[Outer Solution
0.1 M HCl] --> OUTG[Outer Glass
Membrane Surface] MEM[Thin Glass
Membrane pH
Sensitive] --> OUTG MEM --> INNG[Inner Glass
Membrane Surface] INNG --> INS[Inner Reference
Solution pH 7] INS --> IR[Inner Reference
Electrode Ag-AgCl] IR --> LEAD1[Lead to High
Impedance Meter] REF[Reference Electrode
SCE in Saturated KCl] --> LEAD2[Lead to High
Impedance Meter] LEAD1 --> METER[pH Meter
Digital Display] LEAD2 --> METER ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The difference in H⁺ activity between the **outer glass surface** (exposed to sample) and the **inner glass surface** (exposed to pH 7 buffer) creates a potential that the meter converts to pH reading using the Nernst equation derived in Section 3.1. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, Part A]** **Define pH. State the principle of electrometric method of pH measurement.** **Model Answer:** pH is defined as the negative logarithm (base 10) of hydrogen ion activity in an aqueous solution: $$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[H^{+}]$$ The electrometric method uses a glass electrode (measuring) and a saturated calomel electrode (reference) immersed in the sample. The EMF developed across the glass membrane, which is selectively permeable to H⁺ ions, is measured by a high-impedance voltmeter. The EMF is proportional to pH as per the Nernst equation: $E = K - 0.0591 \times \text{pH}$ at 25 °C. The instrument is calibrated using standard buffer solutions of known pH. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, Part A]** **What is turbidity? Define NTU. Why is nephelometric method preferred over Jackson candle method?** **Model Answer:** Turbidity is the optical property of water caused by the presence of suspended and colloidal matter (silt, clay, organic matter, microorganisms) that scatter light. It is a measure of the cloudiness or haziness of water. **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Unit)** is the unit of turbidity measured by a nephelometer using the intensity of light scattered at 90° to the incident beam. The nephelometric method is preferred over the Jackson candle method because: 1. It is **far more sensitive** (can measure as low as 0.1 NTU vs. 25 NTU for Jackson). 2. It is **objective and instrument-based** (no human judgment of candle flame visibility). 3. It is suitable for **low-turbidity treated water** (filter effluents, bottled water). 4. It is the **international standard** method (IS 3025 Part 10, APHA 2130, EPA 180.1). --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, Part B Choice 1]** **(a)** Describe in detail the **principle, construction, and working of a digital pH meter** with a neat block diagram. State the Nernst equation and explain how the slope varies with temperature. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Principle:** The pH meter operates on the principle that a potential difference is developed across a thin glass membrane that is selectively permeable to H⁺ ions. The EMF of the cell (glass electrode | sample | reference electrode) is proportional to the pH of the solution. **Construction:** A digital pH meter consists of: - **Glass Electrode:** A thin-walled glass bulb (pH-sensitive glass: 72 % SiO₂, 22 % Na₂O, 6 % CaO) containing an internal reference solution of 0.1 M HCl and an internal Ag/AgCl electrode. - **Reference Electrode (SCE):** Mercury/mercurous chloride in saturated KCl solution with a salt bridge (porous frit) to make electrical contact with the sample. - **High Input Impedance Voltmeter (10¹² Ω or more):** Necessary because the glass electrode has very high internal resistance. - **ATC Probe:** For automatic temperature compensation. - **Digital Display:** LCD showing pH directly. **Working:** 1. The pH meter is calibrated with two buffer solutions (e.g., pH 7 and pH 4) to determine the slope and offset (K). 2. The electrodes are rinsed and immersed in the sample. 3. The EMF generated is converted to pH using the Nernst equation: $E = K - 0.0591 \times \text{pH}$. 4. The microcontroller computes pH and displays it digitally. **Nernst Equation for pH Cell:** $$E_{cell} = K - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F}\text{pH} = K - 0.05916 \times \frac{T}{298.15} \times \text{pH}$$ **Temperature Dependence:** The slope = $0.0591 \times (T/298.15)$ V per pH unit, where T is in Kelvin. So at 10 °C the slope is 0.0562 V/pH, and at 40 °C it is 0.0621 V/pH. This is why temperature compensation is critical — a 1 °C error in temperature can introduce a 0.014 pH error if not corrected. **Block Diagram:** (As shown in Section 4.1) **(b)** What are **buffer solutions**? Explain the **two-point calibration procedure** of a pH meter using standard buffers. Why is KCl used as the filling solution in the reference electrode? (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Buffer Solution:** A buffer is a solution that resists pH change upon addition of small amounts of acid or base. It is a mixture of a weak acid and its conjugate base (or weak base and its conjugate acid). Common buffers used for pH meter calibration: | Buffer | pH @ 25 °C | Composition | |--------|------------|-------------| | Potassium hydrogen phthalate (KHP) | 4.00 | 0.05 M KHP | | Phosphate buffer | 6.86 | KH₂PO₄ + Na₂HPO₄ | | Phosphate buffer | 7.00 | (Commercial standard) | | Borax | 9.20 | Na₂B₄O₇ | | Sodium carbonate-bicarbonate | 10.00 | NaHCO₃ + Na₂CO₃ | **Two-Point Calibration Procedure:** 1. Switch on the pH meter and allow it to warm up for 10–15 minutes. 2. Rinse the glass electrode with distilled water and gently blot dry. 3. Immerse the electrode in **pH 7 buffer** (first point — sets the offset/K). 4. Adjust the **"Calibrate" or "Standardize" knob** until the display reads exactly 7.00. 5. Rinse the electrode with distilled water and blot dry. 6. Immerse in **pH 4 buffer** (second point — sets the slope). 7. Adjust the **"Slope" knob** until the display reads 4.00. 8. The meter is now calibrated. Check with a third buffer (pH 9.2) to verify accuracy within ±0.05 pH. **Why KCl in Reference Electrode:** - The reference electrode must maintain a **constant, stable, and reproducible potential** regardless of the sample composition. - Saturated KCl solution provides a high concentration of Cl⁻ ions, ensuring that the liquid junction potential is minimal and stable. - The high concentration of K⁺ and Cl⁻ (both with nearly equal ionic mobilities) minimizes diffusion potentials at the salt bridge. - KCl is highly soluble, chemically inert, and does not interfere with most samples. **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, Part B Choice 2]** **(a)** Explain the **principle and working of a nephelometric turbidimeter** with a block diagram. Describe the preparation of **4000 NTU Formazin stock solution** and the dilution procedure to prepare 5, 20, 50, and 100 NTU working standards. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Principle:** The nephelometer measures the intensity of light scattered at **90° (right angle)** to the incident light beam by suspended particles in the sample. The scattered light intensity is proportional to the turbidity (in NTU) under standardized conditions. **Working:** 1. A tungsten filament lamp or LED produces a beam of light. 2. The beam is collimated by a lens system and passed through the sample cuvette. 3. Suspended particles scatter the light in all directions. 4. A photodetector placed at 90° to the incident beam measures the intensity of scattered light. 5. The electrical signal is amplified, processed, and converted to NTU using a pre-stored calibration curve. 6. The reading is displayed digitally. **Block Diagram:** (As shown in Section 4.2) **Preparation of 4000 NTU Formazin Stock Solution:** 1. **Solution A:** Dissolve **1.000 g of hydrazine sulfate** $[(NH_2)_2 \cdot H_2SO_4]$ in distilled water and dilute to 100 mL. 2. **Solution B:** Dissolve **10.00 g of hexamethylenetetramine** $[(CH_2)_6N_4]$ in distilled water and dilute to 100 mL. 3. Mix **5.0 mL of Solution A** and **5.0 mL of Solution B** in a 100 mL volumetric flask. 4. Allow to stand for **24 hours at 25 ± 1 °C** to allow the polymer to form. 5. Dilute to 100 mL with turbidity-free distilled water. This gives **4000 NTU** stock. 6. Store in an amber bottle at room temperature (stable for ~1 year). **Dilution of Working Standards (using $C_1V_1 = C_2V_2$):** To prepare **100 mL** of each standard: | Target NTU | Volume of 4000 NTU Stock | Volume of Distilled Water | |------------|--------------------------|----------------------------| | 5 NTU | 0.125 mL | 99.875 mL | | 20 NTU | 0.50 mL | 99.50 mL | | 50 NTU | 1.25 mL | 98.75 mL | | 100 NTU | 2.50 mL | 97.50 mL | Mix thoroughly and use within 24 hours. Run the standards in the nephelometer to plot the calibration curve. **(b)** Describe the **step-by-step experimental procedure for the determination of pH and turbidity of a water sample** in the environmental engineering laboratory. State the IS standards for drinking water for both parameters. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Experimental Procedure for pH:** 1. **Sample Collection:** Collect the water sample in a clean polyethylene or borosilicate glass bottle. Fill completely to avoid air contact. Analyze within 6 hours or refrigerate at 4 °C. 2. **Instrument Warm-up:** Switch on the pH meter 15 minutes before use. 3. **Calibration:** Calibrate with **two buffer solutions (pH 7 and pH 4)**. Adjust the standardize and slope knobs as described in Q.A(b). 4. **Measurement:** Rinse the electrodes with distilled water, then with a small portion of sample. Immerse in the sample, stir gently, and wait for stable reading (~30 seconds). 5. **Record:** Note the pH and sample temperature. 6. **Storage:** Rinse the electrodes and store the glass electrode in **3 M KCl solution** (never in distilled water, as it will leach ions from the glass bulb). **Experimental Procedure for Turbidity:** 1. **Sample Collection:** Collect in a clean glass or plastic bottle. Analyze same day; refrigerate if delayed. 2. **Nephelometer Warm-up:** Switch on the instrument and allow 5 minutes to stabilize. 3. **Calibration:** Calibrate with **Formazin standards** (0, 5, 20, 50, 100 NTU) using matched cuvettes. 4. **Measurement:** Rinse the cuvette with sample, then fill it to the mark. Wipe the outside with a soft, lint-free tissue. Place in the nephelometer and close the lid. 5. **Record:** Note the reading in NTU. Take triplicate readings and report the average. 6. **Cleaning:** Rinse the cuvette thoroughly between samples to avoid cross-contamination. **IS 10500:2012 Drinking Water Standards:** | Parameter | Acceptable Limit | Permissible Limit (No Alternative Source) | |-----------|------------------|------------------------------------------| | pH | 6.5 – 8.5 | No relaxation | | Turbidity (as NTU) | 1 NTU (max 5) | 5 NTU | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write "pH is dimensionless" and "Turbidity in NTU" — not just numbers. > 2. **Missing the temperature dependence of pH:** A pH value reported without sample temperature is incomplete. > 3. **Confusing NTU and JTU:** NTU is the modern nephelometric unit; JTU (Jackson Turbidity Unit) is the older method. The ratio is not 1:1. > 4. **Skipping the storage step:** The pH electrode must be stored in KCl — this is often asked as a viva question. > 5. **Not mentioning formazin composition:** "Formazin from hydrazine sulfate and hexamethylenetetramine" is a guaranteed 2-mark item in viva. > 6. **Reporting without triplicate:** Always report the **average of three readings** with standard deviation. > 7. **Ignoring interferences in turbidity:** Color (dissolved colored matter) absorbs light and can give **falsely low** NTU values. Note this in the limitations. > 8. **No mention of ATC (Automatic Temperature Compensation):** Always emphasize this in the pH working description. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **pH** = $-\log[H^+]$, dimensionless, scale 0–14, **neutral = 7 at 25 °C**, drinking water acceptable range = **6.5 to 8.5**. - The **glass electrode + reference electrode (SCE)** combination is the standard electrometric setup; EMF follows the **Nernst equation**: $E = K - 0.0591 \times \text{pH}$ at 25 °C. - The **slope** of the pH meter = **59.16 mV per pH unit** at 25 °C and is **directly proportional to absolute temperature**; hence the need for ATC. - **Calibration** must be done with **at least two buffer solutions** of known pH (e.g., pH 4, 7, or 9.2); a third buffer should be used as a check. - **Glass electrode storage:** always in **3 M KCl** (or pH 4 buffer with KCl); never in distilled water. - **Turbidity** is an **optical property** measured as scattered light; the standard method is **nephelometric** (90° scattering) reported in **NTU**. - The **Formazin polymer** is the international reference turbidity standard; stock = **4000 NTU**, working standards by dilution. - Drinking water standard: **Turbidity ≤ 1 NTU** (acceptable), **≤ 5 NTU** (permissible). - **Turbidity is not TSS** — they are related but not identical; turbidity includes colloidal and very fine particles. - **Interferences in turbidity:** Dissolved color (false low), air bubbles (false high), dirty/ scratched cuvette (false high), stray light (false high). - **IS Standards:** pH → IS 3025 (Part 11); Turbidity → IS 3025 (Part 10); Drinking water → IS 10500:2012. - **Jackson Candle method** is the **obsolete** method (silica scale, ≥ 25 NTU only); **Nephelometric** is the **current** standard. - **Sample preservation:** pH — analyze within 6 h; turbidity — analyze same day, store at 4 °C in dark, no chemical preservation. - **Triplicate readings + average** is mandatory for KTU lab report submission; always include the **calibration curve** in the appendix. - For high-turbidity (> 1000 NTU) samples, **dilute the sample** and multiply the reading by the dilution factor. - **Glass electrode response time** is normally 30–60 seconds; sluggish response indicates aging or coating — clean with dilute HCl or replace. - **Reference electrode junction** (porous frit) can become clogged → check for proper KCl flow; refill with saturated KCl if low. - The **permissible limit for turbidity in packaged drinking water** (IS 14543) is **≤ 2 NTU** (more stringent than IS 10500). - **Always report pH with one decimal place** (e.g., 7.4) and **turbidity with two significant figures** (e.g., 12 NTU). - The **Nernst slope equation** to remember: Slope (V) = $0.0001984 \times T$ (where T in K). At 298.15 K, slope = 0.05916 V.

topics.title: Determination of Conductivity and Total Dissolved Solids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Conductivity and Total Dissolved Solids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Electrical Conductivity (EC)

**Electrical Conductivity (EC)** is the quantitative measure of a water sample's ability to transmit an electric current, which is a direct function of the concentration of ionised, dissolved mineral salts (electrolytes) present in the water. It is defined as the reciprocal of electrical resistivity and is measured between two opposing parallel plate electrodes of unit cross-sectional area separated by a unit distance.

Mathematically, conductivity is expressed as:

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = Electrical conductivity
- $\rho$ = Electrical resistivity

The standard SI unit is **Siemens per metre (S/m)**, but in environmental engineering and water quality analysis, the conventionally accepted unit is **microsiemens per centimetre (µS/cm)** or **millisiemens per centimetre (mS/cm)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Always report EC values standardised to **25°C**, because ionic mobility in water is strongly temperature-dependent. The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS 10500:2012) and APHA Standard Methods mandate temperature compensation to 25°C.

## 1.2 Formal Academic Definition of Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)

**Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** represent the total concentration (in mg/L) of all inorganic salts and small amounts of organic matter that are genuinely dissolved in water and will not filter out. It is operationally defined as the dry weight of the filtrate obtained after passing a well-mixed, known volume of water sample through a standard 0.45 µm membrane filter, with the filtrate evaporated to dryness at a controlled temperature of **103°C to 105°C** (or 180°C for higher precision as per APHA).

The standard unit is **milligrams per litre (mg/L)** — numerically equivalent to parts per million (ppm) for dilute aqueous solutions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Salty Soup Wire"**
> Imagine a glass of **distilled water** as a completely empty road at midnight — no cars, no current can flow, conductivity ≈ **0 µS/cm**. Now imagine adding a **pinch of common salt (NaCl)**. The salt dissociates into Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions — think of them as tiny charged cars — and they instantly start carrying electricity from one electrode to the other. The more salt you dissolve, the denser the traffic, and the higher the conductivity. **TDS**, on the other hand, is the **total weight of all those dissolved cars (ions) and other invisible passengers (salts)** packed into that road. So **Conductivity tells you how busy the electrical traffic is**, while **TDS tells you how heavy the traffic actually is in terms of mass**.

**Key Inference:** Conductivity measures the *electrical* property of ions in motion, whereas TDS measures the *gravimetric* (mass) property of the dissolved matter itself. Because ions have characteristic charge-to-mass ratios, the two parameters are mathematically correlated.

## 1.4 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (BIS/IS 10500:2012)

| Parameter | Standard Unit | BIS Desirable Limit | BIS Permissible Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TDS** | mg/L | **500** | **2000** |
| **EC** | µS/cm | Not specified directly (inferred ~750) | Not specified directly |
| **Temperature** | °C | — | — |
| **pH** | — | 6.5 – 8.5 | No relaxation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU PCCEL609 lab manual explicitly requires students to (a) calibrate the conductivity cell, (b) measure EC, and (c) compute TDS using the empirical relation `TDS (mg/L) = k × EC (µS/cm)`. The constant `k` typically ranges from **0.55 to 0.70** for natural fresh waters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Principle of Conductivity Measurement

Electrical conductivity of an aqueous solution is governed by **Ohm's Law**:

$$V = I \cdot R$$

Where:
- $V$ = Applied voltage (Volts)
- $I$ = Resulting current (Amperes)
- $R$ = Resistance of the solution (Ohms)

The **resistance R** of a column of solution held between two parallel electrodes depends on the geometric configuration of the measuring cell:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{l}{a} = \frac{1}{\sigma} \cdot \frac{l}{a}$$

Where:
- $l$ = Effective distance between the two electrodes (cm)
- $a$ = Effective cross-sectional area of the electrodes (cm²)
- $\rho$ = Resistivity (Ω·cm)
- $\sigma$ = Conductivity (S/cm)

Rearranging:

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{R} \cdot \frac{l}{a} = \frac{l}{a \cdot R}$$

The geometric term $\frac{l}{a}$ is called the **Cell Constant (K)**, expressed in units of **cm⁻¹**:

$$K = \frac{l}{a}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma = \frac{K}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Cell Constant Matters:** Electrode geometry differs from cell to cell. Instead of measuring $l$ and $a$ directly, we determine K experimentally by measuring the resistance of a solution of **known** conductivity (typically **0.01 M KCl**, which has a tabulated EC of **1412 µS/cm at 25°C**).

## 2.2 Temperature Dependence of Conductivity

Conductivity increases by approximately **2% per degree Celsius** rise in temperature due to enhanced ionic mobility. The temperature-compensated value is reported using:

$$\sigma_{25} = \frac{\sigma_T}{1 + 0.019 \cdot (T - 25)}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{25}$ = Conductivity at 25°C (µS/cm)
- $\sigma_T$ = Conductivity measured at temperature $T$ (µS/cm)
- $T$ = Sample temperature (°C)
- **0.019** = Empirical temperature coefficient for most natural waters

## 2.3 Theoretical Principle of TDS Determination

### Method A — Gravimetric (Reference Method, APHA 2540 C)
1. Filter the sample through a **0.45 µm membrane filter**.
2. Evaporate a known volume (say, 100 mL) in a pre-weighed **evaporating dish**.
3. Dry in an oven at **103–105°C** until constant weight is achieved.
4. Re-weigh the dish. The increase in mass gives TDS:

$$\text{TDS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample (mL)}}}$$

Where:
- $W_1$ = Weight of empty evaporating dish (g)
- $W_2$ = Weight of dish + residue after drying (g)
- $V_{\text{sample}}$ = Volume of water evaporated (mL)

### Method B — Conductivity-Based (Rapid Field Method)
Since the dissolved ions responsible for EC also contribute to TDS mass, an empirical linear relationship holds:

$$\boxed{\text{TDS (mg/L)} = k \times \text{EC (µS/cm)}}$$

For most **natural fresh waters**, $k \approx 0.55$ to $0.70$. KTU laboratory manuals commonly adopt:

$$k = 0.64 \quad \text{(WHO / WHO-Euro recommended default)}$$

For **KCl calibration solutions**, $k$ is strictly **0.50** to **0.55** because of the well-defined stoichiometry of KCl.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = V/I$ | Ohm's Law — resistance of solution | Ω | — |
| 2 | $K = l/a$ | **Cell Constant** of conductivity cell | cm⁻¹ | 0.1 – 10 |
| 3 | $\sigma = K/R$ | Conductivity from resistance | µS/cm | 0 – 50,000 |
| 4 | $K_{\text{cell}} = \sigma_{\text{KCl}} \times R_{\text{measured}}$ | Calibration of cell constant | cm⁻¹ | — |
| 5 | $\sigma_{25} = \dfrac{\sigma_T}{1 + 0.019(T-25)}$ | Temperature compensation to 25°C | µS/cm | — |
| 6 | $\text{TDS} = k \times \sigma_{25}$ | TDS from conductivity ($k \approx 0.64$) | mg/L | 0 – 2000 |
| 7 | $\text{TDS} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1)}{V} \times 10^6$ | Gravimetric TDS | mg/L | 0 – 5000 |
| 8 | $\text{Salinity (mg/L)} \approx 0.5 \times \text{EC (µS/cm)}$ | Rough salinity estimate | mg/L | — |
| 9 | $\text{Conversion: } 1 \text{ mS/cm} = 1000 \text{ µS/cm}$ | Unit conversion | — | — |
| 10 | $\text{Conversion: } 1 \text{ dS/m} = 1 \text{ mS/cm}$ | Decisiemens unit conversion | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When the question gives EC in **mS/cm**, always convert to **µS/cm** before applying the TDS formula. A 1:1000 error is the most common mistake in lab viva calculations.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drinking Water Treatment:** RO plant designers use EC as a rapid proxy for TDS to assess membrane scaling risk.
- **Industrial Boilers:** High TDS water causes **foaming, scaling, and carryover** in boilers. Conductivity-based continuous TDS monitoring triggers blowdown cycles.
- **Agriculture:** EC determines **salinity hazard** to crops via the **Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)** and **Salinity Class** of irrigation water.
- **Aquaculture & Fisheries:** Fish species have narrow EC tolerance ranges; sudden spikes indicate pollution.
- **Environmental Forensics:** EC time-series at river monitoring stations is used to trace industrial effluent discharges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Conductivity Equation from Ohm's Law

### Step 1: Start with Ohm's Law
The fundamental empirical law governing current flow through an electrolyte is:

$$V = I \cdot R$$

where $R$ is the resistance of the solution column between the electrodes.

### Step 2: Introduce Resistivity
For a homogeneous conductor of uniform geometry, resistance is proportional to length and inversely proportional to cross-sectional area:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{l}{a}$$

The proportionality constant $\rho$ is the **resistivity** (Ω·cm), an intrinsic material property.

### Step 3: Define Conductivity as the Reciprocal
By definition, conductivity $\sigma$ is the reciprocal of resistivity:

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

Substituting into the expression for R:

$$R = \frac{1}{\sigma} \cdot \frac{l}{a}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Conductivity
Inverting the relationship:

$$\sigma = \frac{l}{a \cdot R}$$

### Step 5: Identify the Cell Constant
The geometric factor $\frac{l}{a}$ is the **cell constant (K)**:

$$K = \frac{l}{a} \quad [\text{cm}^{-1}]$$

Therefore, the final working equation is:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{K}{R}}$$

This is the equation implemented in every digital conductivity meter: the meter measures R, multiplies by the pre-stored K, and displays σ directly.

## 3.2 Derivation of Cell Constant via Calibration (Step-by-Step Worked Example)

**Given:**
- Calibration solution: **0.01 M KCl**
- Tabulated EC of 0.01 M KCl at 25°C: $\sigma_{\text{KCl}} = 1412 \text{ µS/cm} = 0.001412 \text{ S/cm}$
- Measured resistance of KCl solution in cell at 25°C: $R_{\text{meas}} = 680 \text{ Ω}$

**Find:** Cell constant K.

### Step 1: Convert units consistently
Convert conductivity to S/cm so that resistance in Ω yields K in cm⁻¹:

$$\sigma_{\text{KCl}} = 0.001412 \text{ S/cm}$$

### Step 2: Apply the fundamental relation
From $\sigma = K / R$, rearrange for K:

$$K = \sigma \cdot R$$

### Step 3: Substitute numerical values

$$K = 0.001412 \text{ S/cm} \times 680 \text{ Ω}$$

$$K = 0.96016 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

### Step 4: Round to significant figures

$$\boxed{K \approx 0.96 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$

This value is now stored in the conductivity meter's memory and is used to compute the EC of any subsequent sample.

## 3.3 Worked Example: EC and TDS Determination of a Water Sample

**Given:**
- Water sample temperature: $T = 28°C$
- Measured resistance: $R_{\text{sample}} = 1250 \text{ Ω}$
- Calibrated cell constant: $K = 0.96 \text{ cm}^{-1}$
- Empirical TDS conversion factor: $k = 0.64$

**Find:** (a) EC at sample temperature, (b) EC at 25°C, (c) TDS in mg/L.

### Step 3.3.1 — EC at Sample Temperature

$$\sigma_{28} = \frac{K}{R} = \frac{0.96}{1250}$$

$$\sigma_{28} = 0.000768 \text{ S/cm}$$

Convert to µS/cm (× 10⁶):

$$\sigma_{28} = 768 \text{ µS/cm}$$

### Step 3.3.2 — EC Corrected to 25°C

Using the temperature compensation formula:

$$\sigma_{25} = \frac{\sigma_T}{1 + 0.019 \cdot (T - 25)}$$

Substitute $T = 28$:

$$\sigma_{25} = \frac{768}{1 + 0.019 \cdot (28 - 25)}$$

$$\sigma_{25} = \frac{768}{1 + 0.019 \cdot 3}$$

$$\sigma_{25} = \frac{768}{1 + 0.057}$$

$$\sigma_{25} = \frac{768}{1.057}$$

$$\sigma_{25} = 726.5 \text{ µS/cm}$$

### Step 3.3.3 — TDS from Corrected EC

$$\text{TDS} = k \times \sigma_{25} = 0.64 \times 726.5$$

$$\text{TDS} = 464.96 \text{ mg/L}$$

### Step 3.3.4 — Compare with BIS 10500:2012 Standards

| Parameter | Obtained | BIS Desirable | BIS Permissible | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TDS | 465 mg/L | 500 mg/L | 2000 mg/L | **Within desirable limit** ✔ |
| EC at 25°C | 726.5 µS/cm | — | — | Indicates fresh water ✔ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** A TDS of ~465 mg/L is dominated by **bicarbonates, carbonates, calcium, magnesium, and chlorides** typical of groundwater in Kerala's laterite aquifers.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Gravimetric TDS Derivation

**Given:**
- Empty evaporating dish weight: $W_1 = 47.5238 \text{ g}$
- Dish + residue after drying at 103°C: $W_2 = 47.5396 \text{ g}$
- Volume of water evaporated: $V = 100 \text{ mL}$

**Find:** TDS in mg/L.

### Step 3.4.1 — Mass of Dissolved Residue

$$m_{\text{residue}} = W_2 - W_1 = 47.5396 - 47.5238 = 0.0158 \text{ g}$$

### Step 3.4.2 — Convert grams to milligrams

$$m_{\text{residue}} = 0.0158 \text{ g} \times 1000 = 15.8 \text{ mg}$$

### Step 3.4.3 — Standardise to 1 Litre

Since the sample volume is 100 mL = 0.1 L:

$$\text{TDS} = \frac{15.8 \text{ mg}}{0.1 \text{ L}} = 158 \text{ mg/L}$$

### Step 3.4.4 — Verify with Conductivity Method

If the same sample yielded EC = 247 µS/cm at 25°C, then by the empirical relation:

$$\text{TDS}_{\text{cond}} = 0.64 \times 247 = 158.1 \text{ mg/L}$$

The two methods agree within **0.1 mg/L** — a perfect cross-validation. In practice, the conductivity method may differ by 5–10% due to ionic composition effects on $k$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Conductivity Measurement Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power ON Conductivity Meter] --> B[Select MODE: Conductivity]
    B --> C[Prepare 0.01 M KCl Standard Solution]
    C --> D[Rinse Conductivity Cell with Distilled Water]
    D --> E[Rinse Cell with KCl Solution]
    E --> F[Immerse Cell in KCl - Stir Gently]
    F --> G[Set Meter to 1412 uS/cm at 25C]
    G --> H[Adjust CELL CONSTANT Knob]
    H --> I[Calibration Confirmed - CELL CONSTANT Stored]
    I --> J[Remove Cell - Rinse with Distilled Water]
    J --> K[Rinse Cell with Water Sample]
    K --> L[Immerse Cell in Sample - Stir]
    L --> M[Wait for Stable Reading - Note Temperature T]
    M --> N[Record EC Value in uS/cm at Temperature T]
    N --> O[Apply Temperature Correction sigma25 = sigmaT / 1 + 0.019T-25]
    O --> P[Compute TDS = k x sigma25 with k = 0.64]
    P --> Q[Compare TDS with BIS 10500 Standards]
    Q --> R[Tabulate Result and Inference]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Architecture of a Digital Conductivity Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSOR[Conductivity Cell Assembly]
        E1[Electrode 1 - Platinum Plated] --- SOLN[Water Sample between Electrodes]
        E2[Electrode 2 - Platinum Plated] --- SOLN
    end
    SENSOR --> OSC[AC Oscillator - 1 kHz to 1 kHz Sine Wave Source]
    OSC --> AMP[Amplifier Circuit]
    AMP --> DET[Detector and Rectifier]
    DET --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> MICRO[Microcontroller Unit - Stores Cell Constant K]
    MICRO --> COMP[Temperature Compensation Circuit - PT100 / NTC Thermistor]
    COMP --> DISP[Digital LCD Display - uS/cm or mS/cm]
    MICRO --> TDS[TDS Computation Module - TDS = k x EC]
    TDS --> DISP
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of Gravimetric TDS Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Collect Representative Water Sample - 500 mL] --> S2[Shake Well - Homogenise]
    S2 --> S3[Filter through 0.45 um Membrane Filter]
    S3 --> S4[Pre-weigh Empty Evaporating Dish - W1]
    S4 --> S5[Pipette 100 mL of Filtrate into Dish]
    S5 --> S6[Evaporate on Steam Bath or Hot Plate]
    S6 --> S7[Transfer Dish to Oven at 103 to 105 C]
    S7 --> S8[Dry for 1 Hour]
    S8 --> S9[Cool in Desiccator for 30 Minutes]
    S9 --> S10[Weigh Dish plus Residue - W2]
    S10 --> S11{Re-dry for 30 min - Repeat Cooling and Weighing}
    S11 -->|Constant Weight| S12[Compute TDS = W2 minus W1 x 10 to power 6 / V]
    S11 -->|Weight still changes| S8
    S12 --> S13[Report TDS in mg/L with Reference to BIS 10500]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Mapping — Cause–Effect Chain for TDS and EC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Geological Strata - Limestone, Laterite, Saline Intrusion] --> B[Dissolution of Ions - Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, Cl-, HCO3-]
    B --> C[Increased Ionic Strength in Water]
    C --> D[Higher Electrical Conductivity - uS/cm]
    C --> E[Higher Total Dissolved Solids - mg/L]
    D --> F[Water Hardness, Salinity, Scaling Tendency]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Engineering Decision - Treat - Blending - RO]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** All flowcharts follow the same convention — Rectangular nodes represent **operational steps**; rounded/diamond nodes represent **decision points** (e.g., constant weight check in gravimetric method). The **microcontroller + temperature compensation** sub-system is the *heart* of any modern digital conductivity meter, and this is a frequently asked viva question at KTU lab examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define electrical conductivity of water. What is the unit in which it is expressed?**

**Model Answer:**
Electrical conductivity (EC) is the ability of an aqueous solution to conduct an electric current, owing to the presence of dissolved ionised substances. It is defined as the reciprocal of electrical resistivity. The SI unit is Siemens per metre (S/m), but in water quality analysis it is conventionally expressed in **microsiemens per centimetre (µS/cm)** or **millisiemens per centimetre (mS/cm)**, always reported at the standard reference temperature of **25°C**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between TDS and electrical conductivity? How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | EC | TDS |
|---|---|---|
| What it measures | Ability to conduct current | Mass of dissolved solids |
| Nature | Physical (electrical) | Physical (gravimetric) |
| Unit | µS/cm | mg/L |
| Instrument | Conductivity meter | Evaporation + weighing / EC meter |

**Relation:** TDS is empirically related to EC by the equation:

$$\text{TDS (mg/L)} = k \times \text{EC (µS/cm)}$$

where $k$ is an empirical constant, typically **0.55–0.70** for natural fresh waters and **0.50** for KCl-based standards.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle and procedure for the determination of electrical conductivity of a water sample using a digital conductivity meter. List the apparatus and reagents required.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 0.01 M KCl solution shows a resistance of 720 Ω in a conductivity cell at 25°C. A water sample from a borewell in your college campus shows a resistance of 1850 Ω at 28°C. Calculate the cell constant, EC of the sample at 28°C, EC at 25°C, and TDS. Take k = 0.64. Comment on the suitability of the water for drinking with reference to BIS 10500:2012.

---

#### Solution to (a)

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Digital conductivity meter with conductivity cell (dip-type with platinum electrodes)
2. Thermometer (0–100°C, ±0.1°C accuracy)
3. 0.01 M KCl standard reference solution (commercially available or lab-prepared)
4. Wash bottle with distilled/deionised water
5. Tissue paper / soft cloth
6. Beakers (100 mL, 250 mL)

**Reagents:**
- **Potassium Chloride (KCl), AR grade** — for 0.01 M calibration solution
- **Distilled water** (EC < 2 µS/cm) for rinsing

**Principle:**
Conductivity is measured by applying a small alternating current (AC) between two inert platinum electrodes immersed in the water sample and measuring the resulting resistance. The cell constant K (cm⁻¹), determined using a KCl solution of known conductivity, converts measured resistance to conductivity using the relation:

$$\sigma = \frac{K}{R}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Switch ON the conductivity meter and allow 5 minutes for warm-up.
2. Prepare/collect 0.01 M KCl standard (σ = 1412 µS/cm at 25°C).
3. Rinse the conductivity cell thoroughly with distilled water, then with a small volume of KCl solution.
4. Immerse the cell in the KCl solution; stir gently and wait for a stable reading.
5. Adjust the **cell constant knob** until the display reads **1412 µS/cm** at 25°C. The cell constant is now calibrated and stored.
6. Remove the cell, rinse with distilled water, then with the water sample to be tested.
7. Immerse the cell in the sample, stir gently, and record (a) the EC reading and (b) the temperature.
8. Repeat in triplicate and report the mean value corrected to 25°C.
9. Calculate TDS using $\text{TDS} = 0.64 \times \text{EC}_{25}$ (mg/L).

**Block Diagram:** *(See Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete block diagram.)*

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $\sigma_{\text{KCl}} = 1412 \text{ µS/cm} = 0.001412 \text{ S/cm}$ at 25°C
- $R_{\text{KCl}} = 720 \text{ Ω}$
- $R_{\text{sample}} = 1850 \text{ Ω}$ at $T = 28°C$
- $k = 0.64$

**Step 1 — Cell Constant K:**
$$K = \sigma_{\text{KCl}} \times R_{\text{KCl}} = 0.001412 \times 720$$
$$K = 1.01664 \text{ cm}^{-1} \approx 1.017 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — EC of sample at 28°C:**
$$\sigma_{28} = \frac{K}{R_{\text{sample}}} = \frac{1.017}{1850}$$
$$\sigma_{28} = 0.0005497 \text{ S/cm} = 549.7 \text{ µS/cm}$$

**Step 3 — EC corrected to 25°C:**
$$\sigma_{25} = \frac{549.7}{1 + 0.019(28 - 25)} = \frac{549.7}{1.057}$$
$$\sigma_{25} = 520.0 \text{ µS/cm}$$

**Step 4 — TDS Calculation:**
$$\text{TDS} = 0.64 \times 520.0 = 332.8 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with BIS 10500:2012:**

| Parameter | Result | BIS Desirable | BIS Permissible | Inference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TDS | 332.8 mg/L | 500 mg/L | 2000 mg/L | ✔ Acceptable for drinking |
| EC (25°C) | 520 µS/cm | — | — | Indicates moderately mineralised fresh water |

**Conclusion:** The borewell water has low TDS, well within the **desirable limit** of 500 mg/L. It is suitable for drinking after conventional treatment (filtration + disinfection). However, iron and microbiological testing should also be carried out before final approval.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the gravimetric method for the determination of TDS in a water sample. State its advantages and disadvantages over the conductivity method.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a gravimetric TDS experiment, 100 mL of filtered water sample was evaporated in a pre-weighed porcelain dish (mass = 48.7522 g). After oven drying at 103°C and cooling in a desiccator, the dish with residue weighed 48.7675 g. Calculate TDS in mg/L. State BIS 10500:2012 compliance. If the same sample gave EC = 425 µS/cm at 25°C, compute the empirical k value and discuss its significance.

---

#### Solution to (a)

**Principle of Gravimetric TDS:**
A known volume of well-mixed, filtered water sample is evaporated to dryness in a pre-weighed evaporating dish and dried to constant weight in an oven at **103°C to 105°C**. The increase in mass of the dish represents the total dissolved solids.

**Apparatus:**
- Porcelain or platinum evaporating dishes
- 0.45 µm membrane filtration assembly (with vacuum pump)
- Drying oven (capable of 103–105°C)
- Desiccator with silica gel
- Analytical balance (0.1 mg accuracy)
- Steam bath or hot plate
- Graduated pipette (100 mL)

**Reagents:**
- Distilled water (for rinsing)
- Filter paper / membrane filter 0.45 µm

**Procedure:**
1. Wash the evaporating dish, dry it in the oven at 103–105°C, cool in the desiccator, and weigh it accurately as $W_1$.
2. Filter ~250 mL of the water sample through a 0.45 µm membrane filter to remove suspended solids.
3. Pipette exactly **100 mL** of the filtrate into the pre-weighed dish.
4. Place the dish on a steam bath / hot plate and evaporate carefully without spattering until the sample is dry.
5. Transfer the dish to a drying oven at 103–105°C for 1 hour.
6. Cool the dish in the desiccator for 30 minutes.
7. Weigh as $W_2$.
8. Repeat the heating-cooling-weighing cycle until two successive weighings agree within ±0.0005 g (constant weight).
9. Compute TDS using:

$$\text{TDS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 10^6}{V_{\text{sample (mL)}}}$$

**Advantages over Conductivity Method:**
1. **Direct and absolute measurement** — measures actual mass; no empirical conversion required.
2. **Independent of ionic composition** — works for any dissolved species.
3. **Reference method** — APHA 2540 C recognises it as the standard.
4. **Higher accuracy** for samples with non-ionic dissolved organics (e.g., silica, organic acids).

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Time-consuming** — requires 4–6 hours including oven drying and desiccator cooling.
2. **Not suitable for field use** — needs laboratory oven, balance, and desiccator.
3. **Destructive** — the sample is consumed.
4. **Loss of volatile organics** at 103–105°C may underestimate TDS.
5. **Lower precision at low TDS** (< 50 mg/L) due to small mass differences.

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $W_1 = 48.7522 \text{ g}$
- $W_2 = 48.7675 \text{ g}$
- $V_{\text{sample}} = 100 \text{ mL}$
- $\text{EC}_{25} = 425 \text{ µS/cm}$

**Step 1 — Mass of Residue:**
$$m_{\text{residue}} = W_2 - W_1 = 48.7675 - 48.7522 = 0.0153 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mg:**
$$m_{\text{residue}} = 0.0153 \times 1000 = 15.3 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute TDS:**
$$\text{TDS} = \frac{15.3}{100} \times 1000 = 153 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — BIS 10500:2012 Compliance:**

| Parameter | Result | Desirable | Permissible | Inference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TDS | 153 mg/L | 500 mg/L | 2000 mg/L | ✔ Well within desirable limit |

**Conclusion:** The water is of very low mineral content — characteristic of surface water from a highland catchment or rain-fed reservoir.

**Step 5 — Empirical k Value:**
$$k = \frac{\text{TDS (mg/L)}}{\text{EC (µS/cm)}} = \frac{153}{425}$$
$$k = 0.36$$

**Discussion on k:**
- For this sample, $k = 0.36$, which is **lower than the typical range of 0.55–0.70** for natural waters.
- This indicates that the dissolved ions are predominantly of **lower equivalent weight** (e.g., bicarbonate, HCO₃⁻) carrying more charge per unit mass, OR the presence of **weakly ionised species** (such as dissolved silica or organic acids) that contribute mass to TDS but less to EC.
- Using the **default k = 0.64** would have overestimated TDS as 272 mg/L — an error of **+78%**.
- This illustrates why the **k value must be calibrated site-specifically** for accurate TDS estimation via the conductivity method.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–2 marks):** Reporting EC in mS/cm but applying the TDS formula as if it were µS/cm. Always convert to µS/cm first.
> 2. **Forgetting temperature correction (–2 to –3 marks):** The problem statement often gives a temperature different from 25°C. Skipping the temperature correction formula leads to wrong EC and wrong TDS.
> 3. **Using 1413 instead of 1412 µS/cm (–1 mark):** Either value is acceptable, but consistency with the lab manual is preferred.
> 4. **Wrong cell constant formula (–2 marks):** A common error is writing $K = R/\sigma$ instead of $K = \sigma \times R$. Memorise the derivation — it earns full credit.
> 5. **Assuming k = 0.5 always (–1 mark):** k = 0.5 is for pure KCl calibration. For natural water, use 0.55–0.70 (KTU default 0.64).
> 6. **No units in final answer (–1 mark):** Always write "TDS = 332.8 mg/L" not just "TDS = 332.8".
> 7. **Skipping the BIS comparison (–1 mark):** Even if the calculation is right, omitting the standards table loses the inference/application mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Conductivity (EC)** = ability of water to conduct electricity, reported in **µS/cm at 25°C**.
- ✅ **TDS** = total mass of dissolved solids, reported in **mg/L**.
- ✅ **Cell Constant** $K = l/a$ is determined by calibration with **0.01 M KCl** (σ = 1412 µS/cm at 25°C).
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $\sigma = K / R$.
- ✅ **Calibration Formula:** $K_{\text{cell}} = \sigma_{\text{KCl}} \times R_{\text{measured}}$.
- ✅ **TDS Formula:** $\text{TDS (mg/L)} = k \times \text{EC (µS/cm)}$; default $k = 0.64$ (natural water), $k = 0.5$ (KCl).
- ✅ **Temperature correction:** $\sigma_{25} = \sigma_T / [1 + 0.019(T - 25)]$; EC increases ~2% per °C.
- ✅ **BIS 10500:2012:** TDS desirable ≤ **500 mg/L**, permissible ≤ **2000 mg/L**.
- ✅ **Unit conversions:** 1 mS/cm = 1000 µS/cm = 1 dS/m.
- ✅ **AC source** is used in conductivity meters (not DC) to prevent **electrode polarisation** and **electrolysis** of the sample.
- ✅ **AC frequency:** typically **1 kHz to 3 kHz** for low-EC samples; higher frequencies for high-EC to overcome capacitance effects.
- ✅ **Platinum electrodes with platinum black coating** are used to increase effective surface area and reduce polarisation.
- ✅ **Gravimetric TDS method (APHA 2540 C):** evaporation at **103–105°C** to constant weight.
- ✅ **Cell constant should be recalibrated** whenever the cell is cleaned, replaced, or after long storage.
- ✅ **Distilled water EC** is typically **0.5–2 µS/cm**; any value higher indicates contamination of rinse water.
- ✅ **EC and TDS are NOT the same** — TDS is a mass measurement; EC is an electrical measurement, but they are strongly correlated.
- ✅ **Apparatus list (must memorise for viva):** Conductivity meter, dip-cell, 0.01 M KCl, distilled water, beakers, thermometer, wash bottle, tissue paper.
- ✅ **Common KTU viva questions:** *Why AC and not DC?* (avoids electrolysis); *Why KCl for calibration?* (high purity, stable, well-tabulated σ); *Why platinum electrodes?* (inert, reproducible, low polarisation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Alkalinity and Acidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Alkalinity and Acidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alkalinity** of water is its quantitative capacity to neutralize strong acids to a designated pH (phenolphthalein end point **pH 8.3** and methyl orange end point **pH 4.5**), caused primarily by the presence of **bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)**, **carbonate (CO₃²⁻)**, and **hydroxide (OH⁻)** ions, expressed in mg/L as **CaCO₃** equivalent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acidity** of water is its quantitative capacity to neutralize strong bases to a designated pH (methyl orange end point **pH 4.5** and phenolphthalein end point **pH 8.3**), caused mainly by dissolved **carbon dioxide (CO₂)**, **mineral acids (H₂SO₄, HCl)**, and **hydrolysable salts** of strong acids and weak bases, expressed in mg/L as **CaCO₃** equivalent.

In simple terms:

- **Alkalinity = Water's "Acid-Fighting Power"** → measure of how much **HCl/H₂SO₄** the water can absorb before turning acidic.
- **Acidity = Water's "Base-Fighting Power"** → measure of how much **NaOH** the water can absorb before turning basic.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Alkalinity Analogy — The "Antacid Tablet"**: Think of alkalinity like an antacid tablet (ENO). The tablet has a *reserve capacity* to fight stomach acid. Similarly, natural water contains invisible "antacid reserves" (HCO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻, OH⁻) that buffer the water against acid pollution (acid rain, industrial discharge). Higher alkalinity = more resilient water body, less prone to pH shock that kills fish.

> [!NOTE]
> **Acidity Analogy — The "Lemon Water"**: Acidity is like the sourness in lemon water. A water sample high in dissolved CO₂ or mine-drainage sulfuric acid is "sour" and aggressive — it corrodes pipes, leaches metals, and harms aquatic life. Just as you add sugar to neutralize lemon's sourness, we titrate with NaOH to measure it.

## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU 2024

- Determines **buffering capacity** of natural waters, treated effluents, and drinking water.
- Essential for **water-softening calculations** (Lime-Soda process).
- Monitors **industrial wastewater** before discharge.
- Required parameter in **BIS (IS 10500)** and **CPHEEO** drinking water standards.

## 1.4 Standard Indicators Used

| Indicator | pH Range | Color Change | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phenolphthalein** | **8.2 – 10.0** | **Colorless → Pink** | Detects OH⁻ + ½ CO₃²⁻ |
| **Methyl Orange** | **3.1 – 4.4** | **Red → Orange-Yellow** | Detects remaining HCO₃⁻ |

## 1.5 Apparatus and Reagents (KTU Lab Standard)

**Apparatus**: Burette (50 mL), pipette (50/100 mL), conical flasks (250 mL), volumetric flasks, measuring cylinder.

**Reagents for Alkalinity**:
- **N/50 H₂SO₄** (0.02 N) — standard titrant.
- **Phenolphthalein indicator** (1% in alcohol).
- **Methyl orange indicator** (0.05% aqueous).

**Reagents for Acidity**:
- **N/50 NaOH** (0.02 N) — standard titrant.
- Same indicators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard normality used in KTU labs is **0.02 N** (N/50). Always carry out a blank titration on distilled water for accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Alkalinity (Step-by-Step Logic)

When **N/50 H₂SO₄** is added dropwise to the water sample, the reactions occur in a **fixed order of strength** — strongest base first:

**Step 1 — Hydroxide neutralization (pH drops from > 10 to ~ 8.3):**
$$\text{OH}^{-} + \text{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Carbonate converted to bicarbonate (pH drops from 8.3 to ~ 4.5):**
$$\text{CO}_3^{2-} + \text{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \text{HCO}_3^{-}$$

**Step 3 — Bicarbonate converted to carbonic acid (pH drops from 4.5 to 3.7):**
$$\text{HCO}_3^{-} + \text{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

> [!NOTE]
> Phenolphthalein end point (**pH 8.3**) catches the end of Step 1 and the **mid-point of Step 2** (because half of CO₃²⁻ has become HCO₃⁻). Hence, at the phenolphthalein end point: **OH⁻ is completely neutralized, and only half of CO₃²⁻ has reacted**.

## 2.2 Chemistry of Acidity

The reverse logic applies using **N/50 NaOH**:

- **Mineral Acidity** (strong acids, Fe³⁺, Al³⁺ salts) → neutralized up to **pH 4.5** (methyl orange end point).
- **CO₂ Acidity** (weak carbonic acid) → neutralized between **pH 4.5 and 8.3** (phenolphthalein end point).
- **Total Acidity** = Mineral acidity + CO₂ acidity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normality convention**: All results expressed as **mg/L of CaCO₃**, where equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = **50 g/eq**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO₃)** | $\dfrac{(A - B) \times N \times 50000}{V}$ | mg/L |
| 2 | **Acidity (mg/L as CaCO₃)** | $\dfrac{(A - B) \times N \times 50000}{V}$ | mg/L |
| 3 | **P-Alkalinity (OH⁻ + ½ CO₃²⁻)** | Phenolphthalein titre (after blank correction) | mL |
| 4 | **T-Alkalinity (OH⁻ + CO₃²⁻ + HCO₃⁻)** | Total titre up to methyl orange | mL |
| 5 | **M-Alkalinity (HCO₃⁻ + ½ CO₃²⁻)** | $\text{T} - \text{P}$ | mL |
| 6 | **OH⁻ concentration** | $2P - T$ (if $2P > T$, else 0) | mL of acid |
| 7 | **CO₃²⁻ concentration** | $2P$ (if $2P < T$); $2(T-P)$ (if $2P > T$) | mL of acid |
| 8 | **HCO₃⁻ concentration** | $T - 2P$ (if $2P < T$); 0 (if $2P > T$) | mL of acid |
| 9 | **Numerical factor 50000** | $\dfrac{50 \times 1000}{1} = 50000$ | — |

**Where**:
- $A$ = Burette reading of titrant (mL)
- $B$ = Blank reading of titrant (mL)
- $N$ = Normality of titrant (eq/L)
- $V$ = Volume of water sample taken (mL)
- $P$ = Phenolphthalein titre (mL)
- $T$ = Total titre (mL)

## 2.4 Decision Matrix — Alkalinity Speciation

Let **P** = phenolphthalein titre and **T** = total titre (P + M).

| Condition | OH⁻ (mL) | CO₃²⁻ (mL) | HCO₃⁻ (mL) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P = 0** | 0 | 0 | T |
| **P < ½ T** (i.e., $2P < T$) | 0 | 2P | T - 2P |
| **P = ½ T** (i.e., $2P = T$) | 0 | 2P (= T) | 0 |
| **P > ½ T** (i.e., $2P > T$) | 2P - T | 2(T - P) | 0 |
| **M = 0** (i.e., P = T) | T | 0 | 0 |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student error is using **"M"** in formulas instead of **"T"** (Total = P + M). Always remember: T includes the volume used up to phenolphthalein end point **plus** the additional volume up to methyl orange.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lime-Soda softening plants**: Alkalinity dictates the **lime dose** (Ca(OH)₂) required.
- **Boiler-feed water**: Acidity causes **corrosion** of boiler tubes; alkalinity must be controlled to prevent caustic embrittlement.
- **Aquaculture**: Stable alkalinity (50–200 mg/L as CaCO₃) prevents fish kills from acid rain.
- **Distribution systems**: Aggressive (low alkalinity, high CO₂) water dissolves lead/copper from pipes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Universal Alkalinity/Acidity Formula

**Starting Principle**: By the **law of neutralization** (equivalent principle):
$$\text{Equivalents of acid} = \text{Equivalents of base}$$

**For alkalinity titration**:
$$\text{Equivalents of CaCO}_3 \text{ in sample} = \text{Equivalents of H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ used}$$

**Equivalents of H₂SO₄** used:
$$\text{Eq. of H}_2\text{SO}_4 = (A - B) \text{ (mL)} \times N \text{ (eq/L)} \times \dfrac{1}{1000} \text{ (L/mL)}$$

**Equivalents of CaCO₃** in V mL of sample:
$$\text{Eq. of CaCO}_3 = \dfrac{\text{mass of CaCO}_3 \text{ (mg)}}{\text{Equivalent weight}} = \dfrac{m}{50}$$

**Concentration as CaCO₃ (mg/L)**:
$$C = \dfrac{m \times 1000}{V \times 1} \quad \text{(where 1000 converts mL to L)}$$

**Equating**:
$$\dfrac{C \times V}{50 \times 1000} = \dfrac{(A - B) \times N}{1000}$$

**Solving for C**:
$$\begin{aligned}
C \times V &= 50 \times (A - B) \times N \\
C &= \dfrac{50 \times (A - B) \times N \times 1000}{V \times 1000} \\
C &= \dfrac{(A - B) \times N \times 50000}{V} \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}$$

The factor **50000** comes from $\dfrac{\text{Equivalent weight of CaCO}_3 \times 1000}{1} = \dfrac{50 \times 1000}{1}$.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example — **Alkalinity**

### **Problem Statement**
> 100 mL of a water sample required **2.4 mL of N/50 H₂SO₄** to reach the phenolphthalein end point, and a further **6.8 mL** to reach the methyl orange end point. Blank reading = 0.2 mL. Calculate (a) Total Alkalinity, (b) P-Alkalinity, (c) species concentrations in mg/L as CaCO₃.

### **Solution (Step-by-Step)**

**Given**: $V = 100$ mL, $N = \dfrac{1}{50} = 0.02$ N, Blank = 0.2 mL.

**Step 1 — Calculate P and T**:
$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 2.4 - 0.2 = 2.2 \text{ mL} \\
\text{Additional to M.O.} &= 6.8 - 0.2 = 6.6 \text{ mL} \\
T &= P + M = 2.2 + 6.6 = 8.8 \text{ mL}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compare 2P and T**:
$$2P = 2 \times 2.2 = 4.4 \text{ mL}, \quad T = 8.8 \text{ mL}$$

Since $2P = 4.4 < T = 8.8$ → **Case 2** of decision matrix applies.

**Step 3 — Apply species formulas**:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OH}^- &= 0 \\
\text{CO}_3^{2-} \text{ (mL of acid)} &= 2P = 2 \times 2.2 = 4.4 \text{ mL} \\
\text{HCO}_3^- \text{ (mL of acid)} &= T - 2P = 8.8 - 4.4 = 4.4 \text{ mL}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert each to mg/L as CaCO₃** using the universal formula:

**Total Alkalinity (T)**:
$$T = \dfrac{8.8 \times 0.02 \times 50000}{100} = \dfrac{8800}{100} = 88 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**P-Alkalinity (P)**:
$$P = \dfrac{2.2 \times 0.02 \times 50000}{100} = 22 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**OH⁻ Alkalinity**:
$$\text{OH}^- = 0 \text{ mg/L}$$

**CO₃²⁻ Alkalinity**:
$$\text{CO}_3^{2-} = \dfrac{4.4 \times 0.02 \times 50000}{100} = 44 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**HCO₃⁻ Alkalinity**:
$$\text{HCO}_3^- = \dfrac{4.4 \times 0.02 \times 50000}{100} = 44 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Verification**: 0 + 44 + 44 = **88 mg/L = T** ✓

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example — **Acidity**

### **Problem Statement**
> 50 mL of an industrial wastewater required **3.0 mL of N/50 NaOH** to the phenolphthalein end point, and a further **4.5 mL** to the methyl orange end point. Blank = 0.1 mL. Calculate Mineral acidity and CO₂ acidity in mg/L as CaCO₃.

### **Solution**

**Step 1 — Determine titre values**:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mineral acidity titre} &= 3.0 - 0.1 = 2.9 \text{ mL} \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ acidity titre} &= (4.5 - 0.1) - 2.9 = 4.4 - 2.9 = 1.5 \text{ mL}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Mineral Acidity (M.A.)**:
$$\text{M.A.} = \dfrac{2.9 \times 0.02 \times 50000}{50} = 58 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 3 — Calculate CO₂ Acidity**:
$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ A.} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 0.02 \times 50000}{50} = 30 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 4 — Total Acidity**:
$$\text{T.A.} = 58 + 30 = 88 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Patterns

| Pattern | Indicator P | Indicator M | Speciation Tactic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sample from limestone terrain | High P (pink) | High M | CO₃²⁻ + HCO₃⁻ dominant |
| Boiler blow-down | M = 0 (no color change) | — | Pure OH⁻ |
| Acid mine drainage | No P | No M (color is red/orange) | High mineral acidity |
| Softened water | Moderate P | Moderate M | Check $2P$ vs $T$ |
| Distilled water | 0 | 0 | Essentially 0 mg/L |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Procedure — Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Collect 100 mL water sample in conical flask"] --> B["Add 2-3 drops of Phenolphthalein indicator"]
    B --> C{"Pink color develops?"}
    C -->|"Yes"| D["Titrate with N/50 H2SO4 until pink JUST disappears"]
    C -->|"No"| E["Record P = 0 mL; proceed to M.O. step"]
    D --> F["Record burette reading A1 = Volume P"]
    E --> G["Add 2-3 drops of Methyl Orange indicator"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Continue titration until color changes Red to Orange-Yellow"]
    H --> I["Record burette reading A2 = Total T"]
    I --> J["Apply formula: Alkalinity = A2 minus B times N times 50000 divided by V"]
    J --> K["Speciation: Compare 2P with T and apply decision matrix"]
    K --> L["Report P, T, OH-, CO3 2-, HCO3- in mg/L as CaCO3"]
    L --> M["End of Alkalinity Experiment"]

    N["Parallel Acidity Branch"] --> O["Take fresh 50 mL sample, boil to remove CO2, cool"]
    O --> P["Add Phenolphthalein; titrate with N/50 NaOH to faint pink"]
    P --> Q["Record M.A. titre; then add M.O. and titrate to yellow"]
    Q --> R["Calculate Mineral, CO2, Total Acidity"]
    R --> S["End of Acidity Experiment"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Titration Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer["Input Stage"]
        I1["Water Sample 50-100 mL in Conical Flask"]
        I2["Indicator Drops: Phenolphthalein OR Methyl Orange"]
    end

    subgraph processStage["Reaction Stage"]
        P1["Acid Base Neutralization Reaction"]
        P2["Equivalence Point Detection by Color Change"]
    end

    subgraph measureStage["Measurement Stage"]
        M1["Burette Reading N/50 H2SO4 or NaOH"]
        M2["Blank Subtraction"]
    end

    subgraph computeStage["Computation Stage"]
        C1["Apply 50000 factor Formula"]
        C2["Decision Matrix for Speciation"]
        C3["Final Report mg per L as CaCO3"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Titration Curve — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Start pH above 10 Strong Base Region"] -->|Add acid| P1["pH 8.3: Phenolphthalein End Point All OH gone Half CO3 converted"]
    P1 -->|Continue| P2["pH 4.5: Methyl Orange End Point All HCO3 converted to H2CO3"]
    P2 -->|Continue| P3["pH below 3.7: Excess Acid Region"]

    Q0["Start pH below 4 Strong Acid Region"] -->|Add base| Q1["pH 4.5: Methyl Orange End Point Mineral Acidity neutralized"]
    Q1 -->|Continue| Q2["pH 8.3: Phenolphthalein End Point CO2 acidity neutralized"]
    Q2 -->|Continue| Q3["pH above 10: Excess Base Region"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define alkalinity. Name the three major ions responsible for alkalinity in natural waters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Alkalinity of water is defined as its quantitative capacity to neutralize strong acids, expressed in mg/L as CaCO₃ equivalent **(1 mark)**. The three major ions responsible are:
> 1. **Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)** — most common in natural waters **(1 mark)**.
> 2. **Carbonate (CO₃²⁻)** — present in waters with pH > 8.3 **(0.5 mark)**.
> 3. **Hydroxide (OH⁻)** — present in highly alkaline / boiler waters **(0.5 mark)**.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between phenolphthalein alkalinity and methyl orange alkalinity. Why are two indicators used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Phenolphthalein alkalinity is measured by titrating to **pH 8.3** and represents the neutralization of **OH⁻ and half of CO₃²⁻** **(1 mark)**. Methyl orange alkalinity is the *additional* titre required to reach **pH 4.5** and represents **half of CO₃²⁻ plus all HCO₃⁻** **(1 mark)**. Two indicators are used because the three ions react in *stages* with acid; using both allows **speciation** (separation) of OH⁻, CO₃²⁻, and HCO₃⁻ concentrations through the decision matrix **(1 mark)**.

---

## **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)**

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Describe the experimental procedure for the determination of **alkalinity** of a water sample. Mention the indicators, reagents, and apparatus used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Apparatus**: Burette (50 mL), pipette (100 mL), conical flasks (250 mL × 2), measuring cylinder, white tile, stand, clamp. **(1 mark)**
2. **Reagents**: N/50 H₂SO₄ (standard titrant), phenolphthalein indicator, methyl orange indicator. **(1 mark)**
3. **Procedure**:
   - Pipette **100 mL** of the well-mixed water sample into a clean conical flask. **(0.5 mark)**
   - Add **2-3 drops of phenolphthalein** indicator. If pink color develops, titrate against **N/50 H₂SO₄** from the burette until the pink color just **disappears** (pH 8.3 end point). Record this volume as **P**. **(1.5 marks)**
   - To the same flask, add **2-3 drops of methyl orange** indicator (yellow at this stage). Continue titration with N/50 H₂SO₄ until the color changes from **yellow to orange/red** (pH 4.5 end point). Record the **total burette reading** as **T**. **(1.5 marks)**
   - Repeat with a **blank** using distilled water and subtract the blank reading. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Calculation**:
   $$\text{Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO}_3) = \dfrac{(A - B) \times N \times 50000}{V}$$ **(1 mark)**

**(b)** 100 mL of water sample consumed **3.5 mL of N/50 H₂SO₄** to phenolphthalein end point and a further **7.5 mL** to methyl orange end point. Calculate the **P-Alkalinity, T-Alkalinity, and concentrations of OH⁻, CO₃²⁻, and HCO₃⁻** in mg/L as CaCO₃. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given**: V = 100 mL; N = 0.02 N (N/50); P = 3.5 mL; M = 7.5 mL (additional).

**Step 1 — Total T and decision**:
$$T = 3.5 + 7.5 = 11.0 \text{ mL}; \quad 2P = 7.0 \text{ mL}$$
Since **2P (7.0) < T (11.0)**, apply Case 2. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — P-Alkalinity**:
$$P = \dfrac{3.5 \times 0.02 \times 50000}{100} = 35 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — T-Alkalinity**:
$$T = \dfrac{11.0 \times 0.02 \times 50000}{100} = 110 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Species**:
$$\text{OH}^- = 0 \text{ mg/L}$$ **(0.5 mark)**
$$\text{CO}_3^{2-} = \dfrac{2P \times 0.02 \times 50000}{100} = \dfrac{7.0 \times 1000}{100} = 70 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$ **(1.5 marks)**
$$\text{HCO}_3^- = \dfrac{(T - 2P) \times 0.02 \times 50000}{100} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 1000}{100} = 40 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Verification**: 0 + 70 + 40 = **110 mg/L = T** ✓ **(0.5 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Explain the **principle and chemistry** of alkalinity determination. Discuss the **staged neutralization reactions** with H₂SO₄ and justify why two indicators are used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Principle**: Alkalinity is determined by titrating a known volume of water with a standard strong acid (N/50 H₂SO₄) to specific pH end points marked by color-change indicators. The volume of acid consumed is proportional to the alkalinity, expressed as CaCO₃. **(2 marks)**
2. **Staged Reactions** (3 reactions in order of base strength):
   - **OH⁻ + H⁺ → H₂O** (occurs first, pH > 8.3) **(0.5 mark)**
   - **CO₃²⁻ + H⁺ → HCO₃⁻** (occurs second, between pH 8.3 and 4.5) **(0.5 mark)**
   - **HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ → H₂CO₃ → H₂O + CO₂↑** (occurs last, pH < 4.5) **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Justification of Two Indicators**:
