3. **Complexed iron** — bound to organic ligands (humic/fulvic acids); colloidal, difficult to remove.
4. **Suspended iron** — iron oxide/hydroxide flocs and corrosion scales in distribution systems.
5. **Bacterial iron** — encrustations by iron bacteria (*Gallionella ferruginea*, *Crenothrix polyspora*).

**Environmental Significance:**
- **Aesthetic:** Staining of laundry, sanitary ware, and cooked food (> 0.3 mg/L).
- **Health:** Chronic ingestion linked to *haemochromatosis*; promotes growth of iron bacteria in distribution mains.
- **Industrial:** Fouling of ion-exchange resins, boiler tubes, RO membranes.
- **Ecological:** Excess iron damages aquatic ecosystems by clogging fish gills.

**BIS / WHO Standards:**

| Authority | Limit (mg/L, as Fe) |
|---|---|
| **IS 10500:2012 (BIS, India)** | **0.3** (no relaxation) |
| WHO Guidelines (4th Ed.) | 0.3 (aesthetic), no health-based guideline |
| EU Drinking Water Directive | 0.2 |
| US EPA Secondary Standard | 0.3 |

**Comparison of Methods:**

| Parameter | 1,10-Phenanthroline | Thiocyanate |
|---|---|---|
| Species determined | Fe²⁺ (total Fe after reduction) | Fe³⁺ (ferric thiocyanate) |
| Colour | Orange-red | Blood red |
| Wavelength | **510 nm** | **480 nm** |
| pH | 3.0 – 3.5 | < 1 (acidic) |
| Stability | Stable > 24 h | Fades rapidly (≤ 30 min) |
| Sensitivity (ε) | 11,100 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ | Lower (~7,000) |
| Interferences | CN⁻, NO₂⁻, Cr | Cl⁻, F⁻, PO₄³⁻, organic acids |
| APHA Reference | 3500-Fe B | 3500-Fe D |
| KTU Preference | **Recommended** | Rarely used |

**(b) [7 Marks]** A water sample gave an absorbance of **0.165** at 510 nm using the phenanthroline method. The calibration equation obtained was $A = 0.182\,C + 0.003$, where $C$ is in mg/L. A 50 mL aliquot of the sample was taken and diluted to 100 mL before analysis. Calculate (i) the iron concentration in the **diluted** sample, (ii) the iron concentration in the **original** sample, and (iii) comment on potability.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $A_{sample} = 0.165$
- $A = 0.182\,C + 0.003$
- Dilution: 50 mL → 100 mL (dilution factor = 2)

**(i) Iron concentration in the diluted sample:**

$$
C_{diluted} = \dfrac{A - 0.003}{0.182} = \dfrac{0.165 - 0.003}{0.182} = \dfrac{0.162}{0.182} = 0.890\ \text{mg/L}
$$

**(ii) Iron concentration in the original (undiluted) sample:**

Multiplication by dilution factor:

$$
C_{original} = 0.890 \times \dfrac{100}{50} = 0.890 \times 2 = 1.780\ \text{mg/L}
$$

**(iii) Potability check:**

$$
C_{original} = 1.780\ \text{mg/L} \gg 0.3\ \text{mg/L}\ (\text{BIS limit})
$$

> **Conclusion:** The original water sample is **highly unsuitable for drinking** — exceeding the BIS limit by **~5.9×**. The very high iron content indicates either **severe corrosion of the distribution main** or **contamination from lateritic/iron-rich geology**. The water would appear distinctly yellow-brown and would stain utensils and laundry. **Recommended action:** Source-water characterisation, mains replacement, and treatment via **aeration → sedimentation → sand filtration → disinfection** before supply.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Avoid these in the KTU exam!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the blank** — Always subtract reagent-blank absorbance from sample readings before applying the calibration equation.
> 2. **Ignoring dilution factor** — If sample is diluted (e.g., 50 → 100 mL), the final answer must be **multiplied by the dilution factor**. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Reporting wrong units** — Iron concentration in water is reported in **mg/L (= ppm)**. Do not report in μg/L unless the value is < 0.01 mg/L.
> 4. **Confusing "total" vs "ferrous" iron** — Without hydroxylamine reduction, the method measures only Fe²⁺. The full procedure including hydroxylamine gives **total iron**.
> 5. **Reporting $R^2$ < 0.99** — A weak calibration curve yields unreliable results; re-prepare fresh standards.
> 6. **Wrong wavelength** — The orange-red complex is measured at **510 nm** (NOT 480 nm, which is for thiocyanate).
> 7. **Skipping the 10–15 min colour-development time** — Incomplete development → low absorbance → underestimation.
> 8. **Stating pH incorrectly** — Always quote **pH 3.0 – 3.5**, not "slightly acidic" or "neutral".
> 9. **Forgetting to mention BIS limit (0.3 mg/L)** — When asked about potability, ALWAYS compare with **IS 10500:2012**.
> 10. **Glassware contamination** — Iron is ubiquitous; soak all glassware in **1:1 HNO₃** overnight and rinse with iron-free distilled water.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Method:** 1,10-Phenanthroline (APHA 3500-Fe B / IS 3025 Pt 53)
- **Wavelength:** **510 nm** (orange-red complex)
- **Complex formula:** **[Fe(phen)₃]²⁺** (molar ratio 1:3)
- **pH range:** **2.9 – 3.5** (ammonium acetate buffer)
- **Reducing agent:** Hydroxylamine hydrochloride (converts Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺)
- **Beer-Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon \, c \, l$
- **Calibration equation:** $A = mC + b$; $C_{sample} = (A_{sample} - b) / m$
- **Linear range:** 0.02 – 5.0 mg/L Fe
- **Detection limit:** 0.02 mg/L
- **Colour development time:** 10 – 15 minutes
- **Stock standard preparation:** 0.7022 g ferrous ammonium sulfate per litre (acidified) → 100 mg/L Fe
- **Working standard:** Dilute stock 1:10 → 10 mg/L
- **Cuvette path length:** 1 cm (4 cm for low concentrations)
- **Acceptance R²:** ≥ 0.999
- **BIS permissible limit (IS 10500:2012):** **0.3 mg/L** (Fe, total)
- **WHO guideline:** 0.3 mg/L (aesthetic quality)
- **Interferences:** Oxidants, cyanide, nitrite, high Co²⁺/Cu²⁺/Ni²⁺/Zn²⁺
- **Sample preservation:** Acidify to pH < 2 with HNO₃; store at 4 °C; analyse within 6 months
- **Glassware protocol:** Soak in 1:1 HNO₃ overnight, rinse with iron-free water
- **Blank subtraction:** Always subtract reagent-blank absorbance from all readings
- **Dilution correction:** Multiply by dilution factor (V_final / V_aliquot)
- **Potability comment:** Compare with BIS 0.3 mg/L; recommend aeration-filtration if exceeding
- **Common error:** Confusing 510 nm (phenanthroline) with 480 nm (thiocyanate)
- **Alternative method:** Thiocyanate method measures Fe³⁺ at 480 nm; less stable, more interferences
- **Real-world Kerala context:** Iron in groundwater of lateritic regions (Kozhikode, Kannur, Kasaragod, Palakkad) frequently exceeds BIS limits; aeration-cum-filtration units are deployed in rural piped-water schemes (JJM)
- **Mnemonic for procedure:** **"HAP-B"** = Hydroxylamine → Acetate buffer → Phenanthroline → Bring to mark
- **Mnemonic for roles:** **"H" = Hide nothing** (reduces ALL iron), **"A" = Adjust** pH, **"P" = Paint** (forms colour)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** If you forget the wavelength in the exam, remember the colour of the complex — **orange-red** (like sunset) → blue-green light (510 nm) is complementary and is maximally absorbed. The **green filter** of a colorimeter also works as a backup!

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Dissolved Oxygen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Dissolved Oxygen**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** is the amount of free (non-compound) oxygen dissolved in water, expressed in **mg/L** (or **ppm**). It represents the volume of oxygen available to sustain aquatic life and drive aerobic biochemical oxidation processes.

According to **IS 3025 (Part 38) : 1989 (Reaffirmed 2019)** and **Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater (APHA, 23rd Edition, Method 4500-O)**, DO is the most critical indicator of the **health of a water body** and the **efficiency of aerobic biological treatment** units (e.g., activated sludge, aeration tanks, bio-filters).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, the **Winkler Iodometric Method** (and its **Azide Modification**) is the prescribed experiment. Students are expected to determine DO, calculate **BOD**, and interpret the data in the context of pollution load.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a fish swimming in a pond. Just like humans breathe oxygen from the air using lungs, fish "breathe" oxygen dissolved in water using their gills. Now think of **Dissolved Oxygen as the "lung capacity" of a water body** — it tells us how much breathable oxygen is available for fish, microorganisms, and chemical reactions.

- **High DO (8–14 mg/L)** → Clean, cold, fast-flowing water (think mountain streams) → Healthy ecosystem.
- **Moderate DO (4–7 mg/L)** → Tolerable for some species.
- **Low DO (< 4 mg/L)** → Polluted water; fish struggle to survive.
- **Zero DO (Anaerobic)** → Septic, foul-smelling water; only anaerobic bacteria survive.

**Real-world implication:** A municipal sewage treatment plant uses **DO measurement as a control parameter** to ensure that bacteria are getting enough oxygen to break down organic pollutants. If DO drops, treatment fails.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Equivalent weight of Oxygen (O₂)** = **8** (since molecular weight = 32 and valency = 4 in iodometric reaction).
> - **Standard temperature for DO reporting** = **20 °C** (or the temperature at sampling).
> - **Saturated DO at 20 °C and 1 atm in pure water** ≈ **9.17 mg/L**.
> - **Normality of standard Sodium Thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃)** used = **0.025 N**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Winkler Iodometric Method — Underlying Principle

The **Winkler Method** (developed by Lajos Winkler in 1888) is a **redox titration** that indirectly measures DO. The logic is elegant: oxygen in the sample oxidizes manganese(II) to manganese(IV) in alkaline conditions; on acidification, the manganese(IV) releases free iodine equivalent to the original oxygen; the iodine is then back-titrated with a standard reducing agent (sodium thiosulfate).

**Step-by-step Logic:**

1. **Fixation of DO (Alkaline Phase):**
   - Manganous sulfate reacts with alkali to form white manganous hydroxide precipitate.
   - This precipitate is highly reactive and immediately reacts with dissolved O₂ to form brown manganic hydroxide.

2. **Acidification (Liberation of Iodine):**
   - Concentrated H₂SO₄ dissolves the precipitate.
   - Manganese(IV) oxidizes iodide (I⁻) to free iodine (I₂).
   - The amount of I₂ liberated is **stoichiometrically equivalent** to the original DO.

3. **Titration (Quantitative Step):**
   - The liberated I₂ is titrated against **standard sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃·5H₂O)**.
   - **Starch indicator** turns the solution blue-black with I₂; the end point is the disappearance of the blue color (sharp, repeatable).

### 2.2 Governing Chemical Reactions

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1 (Alkaline):} \quad \text{MnSO}_4 + 2\text{KOH} \longrightarrow \text{Mn(OH)}_2 \downarrow + \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \\[4pt]
&\text{Step 2 (DO fixation):} \quad 2\text{Mn(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{MnO(OH)}_2 \;\; (\text{brown precipitate}) \\[4pt]
&\text{Step 3 (Acidification):} \quad \text{MnO(OH)}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{Mn(SO}_4)_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \\[4pt]
&\text{Step 4 (Iodine release):} \quad \text{Mn(SO}_4)_2 + 2\text{KI} \longrightarrow \text{MnSO}_4 + \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{I}_2 \\[4pt]
&\text{Step 5 (Titration):} \quad \text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \longrightarrow 2\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Net Stoichiometry:** $1 \text{ mole O}_2 \equiv 2 \text{ moles I}_2 \equiv 4 \text{ moles Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3$

This is the **critical relationship** that links the volume of thiosulfate consumed directly to the mass of oxygen present.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all the formulas** a KTU student must memorize for the ESE and lab viva. **No pipes `|` are used inside math expressions** to preserve table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Dissolved Oxygen (uncorrected) | $\text{DO} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s}$ | mg/L | $V$ = titre volume (mL), $N$ = normality of thiosulfate, $V_s$ = sample aliquot volume (mL) |
| 2 | Dissolved Oxygen (with blank) | $\text{DO} = \dfrac{(V - V_b) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s}$ | mg/L | $V_b$ = blank titre (mL) |
| 3 | DO using 0.025 N thiosulfate (standard) | $\text{DO} = \dfrac{(V - V_b) \times 0.025 \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s} = \dfrac{(V - V_b) \times 200}{V_s}$ | mg/L | Simplified for routine lab use |
| 4 | DO when $V_s = 200$ mL (common) | $\text{DO} = (V - V_b) \times 1.0$ | mg/L | Direct reading shortcut |
| 5 | BOD (5-day, 20 °C) | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f}{P}$ | mg/L | $D_1, D_2$ = DO of diluted sample; $B_1, B_2$ = DO of dilution water blank; $P$ = decimal volumetric fraction of sample; $f$ = ratio of seed in dilution water to seed in diluted sample |
| 6 | BOD (unseeded, simplified) | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{P}$ | mg/L | When seed correction is negligible |
| 7 | Dilution Factor (DF) | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{V_b}{V_m} = \dfrac{1}{P}$ | — | $V_b$ = BOD bottle volume (mL), $V_m$ = sample volume (mL) |
| 8 | Saturation DO at temperature T (approx.) | $\text{DO}_{\text{sat}} \approx 14.652 - 0.41022\,T + 0.007991\,T^2 - 0.000077774\,T^3$ | mg/L | $T$ in °C, valid for 0–30 °C, fresh water, 1 atm |
| 9 | Percent Saturation | $\% \text{ Saturation} = \dfrac{\text{DO}_{\text{measured}}}{\text{DO}_{\text{sat}}} \times 100$ | % | Used to assess water quality |

### 2.4 Why "8" in the Formula? — The Engineering Logic

The factor **8** is the **equivalent weight of O₂** in this reaction. It arises because:
- Molecular weight of O₂ = 32 g/mol.
- In the Winkler reaction, **4 electrons** are transferred per O₂ molecule (oxidation state changes from 0 to −2 in two atoms).
- Equivalent weight = 32 / 4 = **8 g/eq**.

This is **engineering rigor**: every constant in the formula has a stoichiometric and physical meaning. Memorize the *origin* of 8, not just the number — examiners reward conceptual clarity.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs):** Operators measure DO in aeration tanks to maintain **2–4 mg/L** for activated sludge.
- **Aquaculture:** Fish ponds require DO > **5 mg/L**; aerators are switched on/off based on real-time DO probes.
- **River Health Monitoring:** Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) classifies rivers based on DO (Class A > 6 mg/L, Class E < 1 mg/L).
- **Industrial Effluents:** High BOD effluents from distilleries, tanneries must be checked for residual DO before discharge.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Winkler DO Formula

**Start with the net reaction stoichiometry:**

$$
1 \text{ mole O}_2 \equiv 4 \text{ moles Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3
$$

**Step 1 — Moles of thiosulfate consumed:**

If $V$ mL of $N$ normal Na₂S₂O₃ is consumed, then by definition of normality:

$$
\text{moles of Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 = \frac{V \times N}{1000}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to moles of O₂ using the 1:4 stoichiometry:**

$$
\text{moles of O}_2 = \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{V \times N}{1000} = \frac{V \times N}{4000}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of O₂ in grams (using molecular weight = 32 g/mol):**

$$
\text{mass of O}_2 = \frac{V \times N}{4000} \times 32 = \frac{V \times N \times 8}{1000} \;\; \text{grams}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mg (multiply by 1000):**

$$
\text{mass of O}_2 = V \times N \times 8 \;\; \text{mg}
$$

**Step 5 — Concentration in the sample aliquot of volume $V_s$ mL:**

The sample volume $V_s$ mL = $V_s / 1000$ L. Concentration in mg/L:

$$
\text{DO} = \frac{\text{mass of O}_2 \text{ (mg)}}{\text{volume of sample (L)}} = \frac{V \times N \times 8}{\left(\dfrac{V_s}{1000}\right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{DO} = \frac{V \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s} \;\; \text{mg/L}}
$$

**Including blank correction:**

$$
\boxed{\text{DO} = \frac{(V - V_b) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s} \;\; \text{mg/L}}
$$

This is the **master equation** the KTU examiner expects every student to reproduce on the answer sheet.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem Statement (Board Exam Standard):**
> *In a Winkler titration, 200 mL of the acidified sample required 4.8 mL of 0.025 N sodium thiosulfate solution. The blank reading was 0.2 mL. Calculate the Dissolved Oxygen of the sample in mg/L. If the saturation DO at the sampling temperature is 9.0 mg/L, find the percent saturation.*

**Given Data:**
- Volume of sample aliquot, $V_s = 200$ mL
- Volume of thiosulfate for sample, $V = 4.8$ mL
- Volume of thiosulfate for blank, $V_b = 0.2$ mL
- Normality of thiosulfate, $N = 0.025$ N
- Saturation DO, $\text{DO}_{\text{sat}} = 9.0$ mg/L

**Step 1 — Apply the corrected formula:**

$$
\text{DO} = \frac{(V - V_b) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\text{DO} = \frac{(4.8 - 0.2) \times 0.025 \times 8 \times 1000}{200}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(4.8 - 0.2) &= 4.6 \text{ mL} \\
4.6 \times 0.025 &= 0.115 \\
0.115 \times 8 &= 0.92 \\
0.92 \times 1000 &= 920
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by sample volume:**

$$
\text{DO} = \frac{920}{200} = 4.6 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute percent saturation:**

$$
\% \text{ Saturation} = \frac{\text{DO}_{\text{measured}}}{\text{DO}_{\text{sat}}} \times 100 = \frac{4.6}{9.0} \times 100 = 51.11\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{DO} = 4.6 \text{ mg/L}$, $\% \text{ Saturation} \approx 51.1\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The sample is at **51% saturation** — moderately polluted. Fish diversity is reduced; cold-water species (salmon, trout) cannot survive. The water body is at risk of **eutrophication** if organic loading increases.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Modifications for Interferences

| Interfering Substance | Effect on DO | Modification Used | KTU Mark Tip |
|----------------------|-------------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Nitrite (NO₂⁻)** | Oxidizes I⁻ to I₂ falsely (positive error) | **Azide Modification** — Sodium azide (NaN₃) destroys NO₂⁻ | Mentioned in 90% of board questions |
| **Ferrous Iron (Fe²⁺)** | Reduces I₂ to I⁻ (negative error) | **Permanganate Modification** — KMnO₄ oxidizes Fe²⁺ first | Often asked as "Why is azide added?" |
| **Ferric Iron (Fe³⁺)** | Oxidizes I⁻ to I₂ (positive error) | **Fluoride (KF) Modification** — complexes Fe³⁺ | Rare but high-reward in viva |
| **Suspended Solids / Organic Matter** | I₂ adsorption; side reactions | Pre-treatment with alum flocculation | Practical lab note |
| **Sulfide (S²⁻)** | Reduces I₂ | Oxidation with iodate in acid medium | Not in routine syllabus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Azide Modification is the KTU default.** When a question says "determine DO by modified Winkler method," it means the **Azide Modification** unless otherwise specified.

---

### 3.4 BOD Calculation — Linking DO to Pollution Load

**Worked BOD Problem:**

> *A 5-day BOD test was performed on a domestic wastewater sample. 100 mL of wastewater was diluted in a 300 mL BOD bottle. Initial DO = 8.5 mg/L, Final DO = 3.2 mg/L. The blank's DO depletion was 0.3 mg/L over 5 days. Calculate BOD₅.*

**Step 1 — Decimal fraction of sample $P$:**

$$
P = \frac{V_m}{V_b} = \frac{100}{300} = 0.333
$$

**Step 2 — DO depletion of diluted sample:**

$$
D_1 - D_2 = 8.5 - 3.2 = 5.3 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 3 — Blank correction (if any seed used; here unseeded case for simplicity):**

$$
\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{D_1 - D_2}{P} = \frac{5.3}{0.333} = 15.9 \text{ mg/L}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Forgetting to apply the dilution factor. DO depletion of 5.3 mg/L is **not** the BOD of undiluted wastewater; the wastewater is diluted 3×, so the actual BOD is 3× higher (≈ 15.9 mg/L).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Step-by-Step Winkler Procedure

The following **block-level functional architecture flow** maps every operation in the Azide Modification of the Winkler Method. Each node uses a purely alphanumeric ID and contains clean uppercase text (no markdown formatting inside labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[STEP 1 COLLECT SAMPLE IN 300 ML BOD BOTTLE NO AIR BUBBLES]
    n2[STEP 2 ADD 1 ML MANGANOUS SULFATE SOLUTION BELOW LIQUID SURFACE]
    n3[STEP 3 ADD 1 ML ALKALINE IODIDE AZIDE REAGENT]
    n4[STEP 4 STOPPER AND INVERT SEVERAL TIMES BROWN PPT FORMS]
    n5[STEP 5 ALLOW PPT TO SETTLE FOR 5 MINUTES]
    n6[STEP 6 ADD 1 ML CONCENTRATED SULFURIC ACID]
    n7[STEP 7 STOPPER AND INVERT UNTIL PPT DISSOLVES SOLUTION TURNS YELLOW]
    n8[STEP 8 PIPETTE OUT 200 ML INTO CONICAL FLASK]
    n9[STEP 9 ADD 1 ML STARCH INDICATOR BLUE COLOR APPEARS]
    n10[STEP 10 TITRATE WITH 0.025 N SODIUM THIOSULFATE UNTIL BLUE DISAPPEARS]
    n11[STEP 11 RECORD VOLUME V OF THIOSULFATE]
    n12[STEP 12 RUN BLANK WITH DILUTION WATER RECORD Vb]
    n13[STEP 13 CALCULATE DO USING FORMULA DO EQUALS V MINUS Vb TIMES N TIMES 8 TIMES 1000 DIVIDED BY 200]

    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n4 --> n5
    n5 --> n6
    n6 --> n7
    n7 --> n8
    n8 --> n9
    n9 --> n10
    n10 --> n11
    n11 --> n12
    n12 --> n13

    style n13 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** Each "STEP X" is a sequential operation. The **gold-highlighted final node** represents the analytical endpoint — the calculation step where all the chemistry translates into an engineering number (DO in mg/L).

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Reaction Cascade (Chemistry-to-Calculation Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    chem1[MnSO4 PLUS KOH GIVES Mn OH 2 WHITE PPT]
    chem2[O2 DISSOLVED OXIDIZES Mn OH2 TO MnO OH 2 BROWN PPT]
    chem3[H2SO4 DISSOLVES PPT RELEASES MN4 PLUS]
    chem4[KI OXIDIZED TO I2 YELLOW COLOR]
    chem5[Na2S2O3 REDUCES I2 COLORLESS]
    chem6[STARCH INDICATOR SHARP BLUE TO COLORLESS ENDPOINT]
    calc[VOLUME OF THIOSULFATE TO MG PER L DO]

    chem1 --> chem2
    chem2 --> chem3
    chem3 --> chem4
    chem4 --> chem5
    chem5 --> chem6
    chem6 --> calc

    style chem2 fill:#8B4513,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style calc fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **brown precipitate (MnO(OH)₂)** is the visible signature that DO has been successfully "fixed." If the precipitate is white, the sample had **no DO** (anaerobic). This is a quick visual diagnostic the KTU lab examiner will ask about.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define Dissolved Oxygen. Why is it considered the most important parameter in water quality analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dissolved Oxygen (DO) is the amount of free, non-compound oxygen dissolved in water, expressed in mg/L (IS 3025 Part 38). It is the most important parameter because:
1. **(1 mark)** It is essential for the survival of aquatic life (fish, macro-invertebrates); a minimum of **4 mg/L** is required for sustaining a diverse aquatic ecosystem.
2. **(1 mark)** It is the primary indicator of the **aerobic capacity** of a water body — high BOD wastes deplete DO, signaling pollution.
3. **(1 mark)** It directly controls the operation efficiency of aerobic biological treatment units in STPs (e.g., activated sludge process operates optimally at **2–4 mg/L** DO in aeration tanks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**What is the role of starch indicator in the Winkler titration? Why is it added near the end-point?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Starch forms a deep **blue-black complex** with free iodine (I₂) in the presence of iodide ions, allowing the analyst to visually detect the titration end-point.
- **(1 mark)** The blue color disappears sharply when all the I₂ is reduced to I⁻ by sodium thiosulfate, marking the end-point.
- **(1 mark)** Starch is added **near the end-point (when the yellow color of I₂ starts fading to pale yellow)** because adding it earlier would lead to irreversible adsorption of I₂ by the starch-starch complex, causing a **faded, indistinct end-point and negative error** in DO estimation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question is split into sub-part (a) for **7 marks** and sub-part (b) for **7 marks**. Internal choice is provided (Question A OR Question B).

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **principle, procedure, and chemistry** involved in the determination of Dissolved Oxygen by the **Azide Modification of the Winkler Method**. Mention the role of each reagent added. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The Azide Modification of the Winkler Method is an **iodometric titration** based on the principle that dissolved oxygen oxidizes manganous hydroxide to manganic hydroxide in alkaline medium, which on acidification liberates an equivalent amount of iodine. The iodine is then titrated against standard sodium thiosulfate. Sodium azide is added to destroy nitrite interference (which would otherwise cause positive error by oxidizing I⁻ to I₂).

**Procedure with Reagent Roles (3 marks):**
| Step | Reagent Added | Role |
|------|---------------|------|
| 1 | **Manganous sulfate (MnSO₄)** | Source of Mn²⁺ ions |
| 2 | **Alkaline KI + NaN₃** | Provides OH⁻ (forms Mn(OH)₂) and destroys NO₂⁻ interference |
| 3 | **Conc. H₂SO₄** | Dissolves the brown MnO(OH)₂ precipitate; releases I₂ |
| 4 | **Starch indicator** | Visual end-point indicator (blue → colorless) |
| 5 | **0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃** | Standard titrant; reduces I₂ to I⁻ |

**Key Chemistry (2 marks):**
- Fixation: $2\text{Mn(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MnO(OH)}_2$ (brown ppt)
- Acidification: $\text{MnO(OH)}_2 + 2\text{I}^- + 4\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+} + \text{I}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Titration: $\text{I}_2 + 2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3^{2-} \rightarrow 2\text{I}^- + \text{S}_4\text{O}_6^{2-}$

---

**(b)** In a DO determination, **200 mL** of the acidified sample required **4.6 mL** of **0.025 N** sodium thiosulfate. The blank reading was **0.2 mL**. Calculate the Dissolved Oxygen of the sample in **mg/L** and express it as **% saturation** if the saturation DO at sampling temperature is **9.0 mg/L**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — State the given data:**
- $V_s = 200$ mL, $V = 4.6$ mL, $V_b = 0.2$ mL, $N = 0.025$ N, $\text{DO}_{\text{sat}} = 9.0$ mg/L

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{DO} = \frac{(V - V_b) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Substitute and compute:**

$$
\text{DO} = \frac{(4.6 - 0.2) \times 0.025 \times 8 \times 1000}{200}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \frac{4.4 \times 0.025 \times 8000}{200} \\
&= \frac{4.4 \times 200}{200} \\
&= 4.4 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Compute % saturation:**

$$
\% \text{ Saturation} = \frac{4.4}{9.0} \times 100 = 48.89\%
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Interpretation:**

The sample is at **≈ 48.9% saturation**, indicating **moderate organic pollution**. The water body can support only hardy fish species; sensitive species will be absent. Aeration is recommended for any downstream use.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **various modifications of the Winkler Method** to eliminate interferences. Why is the **Azide Modification** the most commonly used in municipal wastewater analysis? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The original Winkler Method suffers from interferences by nitrite, ferrous/ferric iron, and other oxidizing/reducing agents. Several modifications have been developed to overcome these.

**Modifications (4 marks):**

1. **Azide Modification (Alsterberg Method):**
   - **Interference removed:** Nitrite (NO₂⁻)
   - **Reagent:** Sodium azide (NaN₃) added with alkaline iodide
   - **Reaction:** $\text{NaN}_3 + \text{NO}_2^- + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + \text{N}_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$
   - **Used for:** Most municipal wastewaters (low to moderate NO₂⁻)

2. **Permanganate Modification:**
   - **Interference removed:** Ferrous iron (Fe²⁺) and other reducing agents
   - **Reagent:** KMnO₄ added first to oxidize Fe²⁺; excess KMnO₄ destroyed by oxalate
   - **Used for:** Industrial effluents with high iron content

3. **Fluoride (KF) Modification:**
   - **Interference removed:** Ferric iron (Fe³⁺) — complexes Fe³⁺ as [FeF₆]³⁻
   - **Used for:** Acid mine drainage and metal-finishing effluents

4. **Copper Sulfate–Sulfamic Acid Modification:**
   - **Interference removed:** Nitrite at very high concentrations (> 5 mg/L)
   - **Used for:** Industrial wastewater and biological treatment effluents

**Why Azide is Most Common (2 marks):**
- **(1)** Handles the **most common interference** (nitrite) in municipal and biological wastewater.
- **(1)** Simpler procedure, less prone to error, single-step addition, and approved by **APHA Standard Methods (Method 4500-O C)** as the default for routine analysis.

---

**(b)** A **5-day BOD test** was conducted on a domestic wastewater sample. **75 mL** of sample was added to a **300 mL BOD bottle** and made up with **nutrient buffer dilution water**. The **initial DO** of the diluted sample was **8.2 mg/L** and the **final DO after 5 days** was **3.4 mg/L**. The **dilution water blank** had an initial DO of **9.0 mg/L** and final DO of **8.6 mg/L**. Calculate the **BOD₅** of the wastewater in mg/L. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute decimal fraction $P$:**

$$
P = \frac{V_m}{V_b} = \frac{75}{300} = 0.25
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — DO depletion of diluted sample:**

$$
D_1 - D_2 = 8.2 - 3.4 = 4.8 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — DO depletion of blank:**

$$
B_1 - B_2 = 9.0 - 8.6 = 0.4 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — State the seed-corrected BOD formula:**

$$
\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f}{P}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Assume unseeded ($f = 1$):**

$$
\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{4.8 - 0.4 \times 1}{0.25} = \frac{4.4}{0.25} = 17.6 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Interpretation:**

The BOD₅ of the wastewater is **17.6 mg/L**. This is a **moderately strong domestic wastewater** (typical raw domestic sewage has BOD₅ of 200–300 mg/L, so this is partially treated or a diluted sample). Effluent discharge norms (CPCB: BOD < 30 mg/L for discharge into inland surface water) are **met**.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Check dilution validity:**
The DO depletion of the sample (4.8 mg/L) must be ≥ **2 mg/L** and the residual DO ≥ **1 mg/L** for the result to be valid. Here, 3.4 mg/L > 1 mg/L ✓ and 4.8 > 2 ✓ → Result is **valid**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **(−1 mark)** Forgetting to subtract the **blank reading ($V_b$)** in the DO formula.
> 2. **(−2 marks)** Not applying the **decimal fraction ($P$)** in BOD calculation, leading to DO depletion being reported as BOD (a classic blunder).
> 3. **(−1 mark)** Stating "**8**" in the formula without explaining it is the **equivalent weight of O₂**.
> 4. **(−1 mark)** Failing to mention the **role of azide** when the term "Azide Modification" appears in the question.
> 5. **(−1 mark)** Reporting DO in **ppm** without specifying the basis (1 ppm ≈ 1 mg/L for dilute aqueous solutions, but examiners want the explicit **mg/L**).
> 6. **(−1 mark)** No interpretation / environmental significance of the numerical result — a fatal omission in KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 emphasizes **higher-order thinking**).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 12-Point Rapid Recall List for DO Determination (Print and Keep):**

- ✅ **DO** = mg/L of free O₂ in water; critical for aquatic life and aerobic treatment.
- ✅ **Winkler Method** = iodometric redox titration; the gold standard (APHA 4500-O).
- ✅ **Azide Modification** = the **default choice** for routine wastewater analysis.
- ✅ **Key Reagents:** MnSO₄, Alkaline KI + NaN₃, Conc. H₂SO₄, Starch, 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $\text{DO} = \dfrac{(V - V_b) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s}$ mg/L.
- ✅ **The number "8"** = equivalent weight of O₂ (molecular weight 32 ÷ 4 electrons transferred).
- ✅ **Net Stoichiometry:** 1 mole O₂ = 4 moles Na₂S₂O₃ (memorize this!).
- ✅ **BOD₅ Formula:** $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2)}{P}$; **always apply dilution factor $P$**.
- ✅ **BOD Bottle:** Standard 300 mL glass-stoppered bottle; **no air bubbles** during sampling.
- ✅ **BOD Validity Check:** DO depletion ≥ 2 mg/L AND residual DO ≥ 1 mg/L.
- ✅ **Saturated DO at 20 °C** ≈ **9.17 mg/L** in pure freshwater; decreases with temperature, salinity, and altitude.
- ✅ **Common interferences and their fixes:** Nitrite → Azide; Fe²⁺ → KMnO₄; Fe³⁺ → KF; S²⁻ → Iodate oxidation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Nitrogen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Nitrogen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Nitrogen in Water and Wastewater**
> Nitrogen occurs in water predominantly in five (5) oxidation states, ranging from the most reduced form (ammonia, $\text{NH}_3$, oxidation state **$-3$**) to the most oxidized form (nitrate, $\text{NO}_3^-$, oxidation state **$+5$**). The laboratory "Determination of Nitrogen" experiment quantifies one or more of these species — namely **Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN)**, **Ammonia Nitrogen**, **Nitrite Nitrogen**, and **Nitrate Nitrogen** — to assess the **pollution load, biodegradability, and treatment efficiency** of a water/wastewater sample.

The **KTU PCCEL609 Module 2** syllabus specifically mandates the **Kjeldahl Method** (for TKN and Ammonia) and the **colorimetric (phenate) / Nessler / distillation-titration method** for ammonia determination.

### 1.1.1 The Five Oxidation States of Nitrogen in Water

| Oxidation State | Chemical Species | Common Name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $-3$ | $\text{NH}_3$ / $\text{NH}_4^+$ | Ammonia (reduced) |
| $-1$ | $\text{NH}_2\text{OH}$ | Hydroxylamine |
| $0$ | $\text{N}_2$ | Molecular Nitrogen |
| $+3$ | $\text{NO}_2^-$ | Nitrite |
| $+5$ | $\text{NO}_3^-$ | Nitrate (oxidized) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Exact Wording Expected):**
> *Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN) is the sum of organic nitrogen and ammonia nitrogen present in a water sample, determined by digestion with concentrated sulphuric acid followed by distillation and titration.*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: "The Nitrogen Family Tree"**
> Imagine nitrogen in water as a **family of five siblings** sitting on a staircase, where each step represents a different oxidation level. The eldest sibling, **Nitrate ($\text{NO}_3^-$)**, is at the top — fully "grown up" and stable. The youngest, **Ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$)**, is at the bottom — restless and reactive. In nature (and sewage treatment plants), bacteria act as the **"parents"** that push the children up or down the staircase. This biological process is called **Nitrification** (pushing upward: $\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^- \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^-$) and **Denitrification** (pushing downward: $\text{NO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow$).

When a sanitation engineer measures nitrogen, they are essentially **"taking attendance"** of this family. The total headcount is the **TKN + Nitrate + Nitrite**, which tells the engineer how "nutrient-rich" (eutrophic potential) or "polluted" the water is.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why measure nitrogen?**
> 1. **Public Health:** Excess ammonia is toxic to aquatic life and indicates recent sewage contamination.
> 2. **Eutrophication Control:** Nitrate/nitrite cause algal blooms in receiving water bodies.
> 3. **Treatment Plant Performance:** BOD/N ratio determines biodegradability. Optimum $\text{C:N:P} = 100:5:1$.
> 4. **Drinking Water Standard (IS 10500):** Nitrate $\le 45\ \text{mg/L}$ (as $\text{NO}_3^-$).

---

## 1.3 Forms of Nitrogen — Detailed Classification

### (a) Total Nitrogen (TN)
$$\text{TN} = \text{TKN} + \text{Nitrite-N} + \text{Nitrate-N}$$

### (b) Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN)
$$\text{TKN} = \text{Organic-N} + \text{Ammonia-N}\ (\text{NH}_3\text{-N} + \text{NH}_4^+\text{-N})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The Kjeldahl method does **NOT** measure nitrite or nitrate nitrogen. They must be determined separately and added to obtain Total Nitrogen.

### (c) Organic Nitrogen
Nitrogen bound in organic molecules such as proteins, amino acids, urea, and nucleic acids. Computed by:
$$\text{Organic-N} = \text{TKN} - \text{Ammonia-N}$$

### (d) Ammonia Nitrogen ($\text{NH}_3$-N)
Exists in equilibrium:
$$\text{NH}_4^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}^+$$
The proportion of $\text{NH}_3$ (toxic, un-ionized form) increases with **pH** and **temperature**.

### (e) Nitrite Nitrogen ($\text{NO}_2^-$-N)
Unstable intermediate; its presence indicates **incomplete nitrification** or **recent biological activity**.

### (f) Nitrate Nitrogen ($\text{NO}_3^-$-N)
End product of nitrification; the most stable oxidized form in aerobic waters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Kjeldahl Method — Operational Logic

The classical Kjeldahl method involves **three sequential stages**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3-Stage Logic of Kjeldahl Method**
> 1. **Digestion** — Organic-N + $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ → Ammonium Sulphate (releases N as $\text{NH}_4^+$)
> 2. **Distillation** — $\text{NH}_4^+$ + $\text{NaOH}$ → $\text{NH}_3 \uparrow$ (liberated as gas, trapped in boric acid)
> 3. **Titration** — Boric acid solution (containing absorbed $\text{NH}_3$) titrated against standard $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ or $\text{HCl}$

### 2.1.1 Stage 1 — Digestion (The "Breaking" Step)

The organic nitrogen compounds are broken down using **concentrated sulphuric acid ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $98\%$)** at high temperature (${\sim}370^\circ\text{C}$) in the presence of a **catalyst** (typically a mixture of $\text{CuSO}_4$ + $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$ + $\text{Se}$ or $\text{HgO}$).

$$\text{Organic-N} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\ \text{catalyst}} (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$$

- $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$ **raises the boiling point** of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (from $\sim337^\circ\text{C}$ to $\sim370^\circ\text{C}$).
- $\text{CuSO}_4$ / $\text{HgO}$ / $\text{Se}$ acts as a **catalyst** to speed up oxidation.
- The carbon and hydrogen in organic matter are oxidized to $\text{CO}_2 \uparrow$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O} \uparrow$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Many students write that $\text{HNO}_3$ is used in digestion. **This is wrong** — nitric acid is **NOT** used because it adds exogenous nitrogen, destroying the measurement.

### 2.1.2 Stage 2 — Distillation (The "Releasing" Step)

After digestion, the cooled solution is diluted and made **strongly alkaline** with $\text{NaOH}$. This converts the ammonium ion back to free ammonia gas, which is then **steam-distilled**.

$$(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{NH}_3 \uparrow + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The liberated $\text{NH}_3$ is absorbed into a known volume of **boric acid solution (4%)** containing a mixed indicator (bromocresol green + methyl red).

$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_3\text{BO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{H}_2\text{BO}_3^-$$

The solution turns from **pink/red → green/blue** (or vice versa, depending on the indicator system) at the absorption point, indicating successful capture.

### 2.1.3 Stage 3 — Titration (The "Counting" Step)

The ammonium borate formed is titrated against a **standard acid** (typically $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ of $0.02\text{ N}$ or $\text{HCl}$ of $0.1\text{ N}$).

$$\text{H}_2\text{BO}_3^- + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{H}_3\text{BO}_3$$

The **equivalence point** is detected by the color change of the mixed indicator.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (All-In-One)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 90% of KTU numerical problems.**

### 2.2.1 Formula 1 — TKN by Kjeldahl Method (Distillation-Titration)

$$\boxed{\text{TKN (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 14 \times 1000}{V}}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $V_s$ | Volume of standard acid used for **sample** titration | mL |
| $V_b$ | Volume of standard acid used for **blank** titration | mL |
| $N$ | Normality of standard acid ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ or $\text{HCl}$) | eq/L |
| $14$ | Equivalent weight of Nitrogen ($\text{N}$) | g/eq |
| $1000$ | Conversion factor (mg/g) | — |
| $V$ | Volume of sample taken for analysis | mL |

### 2.2.2 Formula 2 — Ammonia Nitrogen ($\text{NH}_3$-N)

$$\boxed{\text{NH}_3\text{-N (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 14 \times 1000}{V}}$$

*(Identical formula, but sample is **NOT** digested; only direct distillation after adding buffer.)*

### 2.2.3 Formula 3 — Organic Nitrogen

$$\boxed{\text{Organic-N (mg/L)} = \text{TKN} - \text{NH}_3\text{-N}}$$

### 2.2.4 Formula 4 — Total Nitrogen

$$\boxed{\text{Total-N (mg/L)} = \text{TKN} + \text{NO}_2^-\text{-N} + \text{NO}_3^-\text{-N}}$$

### 2.2.5 Formula 5 — Per-Cent Recovery (QC Check)

$$\boxed{\%\ \text{Recovery} = \frac{\text{Concentration Found}}{\text{Concentration Spiked}} \times 100}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Acceptable recovery range: 90%–110%**. If outside, repeat the analysis.

### 2.2.6 Formula 6 — Free Ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$, un-ionized) from Total Ammonia

$$\boxed{[\text{NH}_3] = \frac{[\text{TAN}]}{1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)}}}$$

where $pK_a$ depends on temperature:

| Temperature ($^\circ\text{C}$) | $pK_a$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| $0$ | $9.86$ |
| $10$ | $9.34$ |
| $20$ | $8.96$ |
| $25$ | $8.81$ |
| $30$ | $8.66$ |

---

## 2.3 Summary of Reagents Used (KTU Practical Exam Quick-Recall)

| Stage | Reagent | Role |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Digestion | $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (conc.) | Oxidizing acid |
| Digestion | $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$ | Boiling-point elevator |
| Digestion | $\text{CuSO}_4$ (or Se / $\text{HgO}$) | Catalyst |
| Distillation | $\text{NaOH}$ (40% or 50%) | Alkalization |
| Absorption | Boric acid (4%) + mixed indicator | Absorbent |
| Titration | $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (0.02 N) or $\text{HCl}$ (0.1 N) | Standard acid |
| Indicator | Bromocresol green + Methyl red | Endpoint detection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why boric acid and not HCl/H₂SO₄ for absorption?**
> Boric acid is a **weak acid**; it does not interfere with subsequent titration. Using a strong acid (like $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$) would require a **back-titration** with NaOH, introducing additional error.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Procedure with Numerical Demonstration

### 3.1.1 Stage 1 — Digestion (TKN Determination)

**Procedure:**
1. Pipette **$V = 50\ \text{mL}$** of well-mixed sample into a **$800\ \text{mL}$ Kjeldahl flask**.
2. Add **$10\ \text{mL}$ concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$**.
3. Add **$5\ \text{g}$ $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$** and **$0.5\ \text{g}$ $\text{CuSO}_4$** (catalyst mixture).
4. Place in a **Kjeldahl digestion rack** inside a **fume hood**.
5. Heat gently, then increase to full heat. Digest until the solution becomes **clear pale blue / greenish** and white fumes cease.
6. Cool to room temperature. Dilute cautiously with **$200\ \text{mL}$ distilled water**. Cool again.

### 3.1.2 Stage 2 — Distillation

1. Transfer digested sample to the **distillation flask** of a Kjeldahl distillation apparatus.
2. Add **$3$–$4$ glass beads** (anti-bumping).
3. Add **$40$–$50\ \text{mL}$ of $\text{NaOH}$** (40% w/v) carefully along the side so that it forms a **separate alkaline layer** below the acid.
4. Connect immediately to the distillation unit.
5. Place a **$250\ \text{mL}$ conical flask** containing **$50\ \text{mL}$ of 4% boric acid** (with $3$–$4$ drops of mixed indicator) at the condenser tip. The tip must be **immersed** in the boric acid.
6. Distill and collect **$\sim 200\ \text{mL}$** of distillate.

### 3.1.3 Stage 3 — Titration

1. Remove the receiving flask. Lower the tip out of the liquid.
2. Titrate the distillate against **standard $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ ($0.02\ \text{N}$)** until the color changes from **green → pink/violet** (or red, per indicator system).
3. Record titre volume as $V_s$.
4. Repeat with a **blank** (distilled water processed identically). Record as $V_b$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Style)

### **Problem:**
A $50\ \text{mL}$ sample of municipal wastewater is analyzed for TKN by the Kjeldahl method. After digestion and distillation, the liberated ammonia is absorbed in $50\ \text{mL}$ of 4% boric acid and titrated against $0.02\ \text{N}\ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$. The titre value is $24.5\ \text{mL}$. A blank titration consumed $0.3\ \text{mL}$ of the same acid. Calculate the **TKN concentration** in mg/L.

### **Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:**

**Given Data:**
- $V$ (sample volume) $= 50\ \text{mL}$
- $N$ (normality of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$) $= 0.02\ \text{N}$
- $V_s$ (sample titre) $= 24.5\ \text{mL}$
- $V_b$ (blank titre) $= 0.3\ \text{mL}$
- Equivalent weight of $\text{N} = 14\ \text{g/eq}$

**Step 1: Compute the net volume of acid consumed**

$$V_{\text{net}} = V_s - V_b$$

$$V_{\text{net}} = 24.5 - 0.3 = 24.2\ \text{mL}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Kjeldahl formula**

$$\text{TKN (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 14 \times 1000}{V}$$

$$\text{TKN} = \frac{24.2 \times 0.02 \times 14 \times 1000}{50}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the numerator**

$$24.2 \times 0.02 = 0.484$$

$$0.484 \times 14 = 6.776$$

$$6.776 \times 1000 = 6776$$

**Step 4: Divide by sample volume**

$$\text{TKN} = \frac{6776}{50} = 135.52\ \text{mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TKN} = 135.52\ \text{mg/L} \ (\text{as N})}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Interferences & Troubleshooting

| Interference | Effect on Result | Remedy |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Nitrate / Nitrite present | Underestimated (not measured by Kjeldahl) | Measure separately, add to get TN |
| Mercury catalyst residue | Forms $\text{NH}_2\text{-Hg}$ complex (low result) | Add **Na₂S** or **Zn** to decompose |
| Chloride ($> 2\ \text{g/L}$) | High $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ consumption, low boiling | Reduce sample volume or dilute |
| Incomplete digestion | Low TKN | Add more $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$; digest longer until clear |
| Loss of $\text{NH}_3$ during digestion (overheating) | Low TKN | Digest at controlled $\sim 370^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Sample with high $\text{NH}_3$ | Foam in distillation | Add antifoam (silicone) or stearic acid |
| Pyridine, azo, nitro compounds | **NOT** recovered by Kjeldahl | Use **Dumas** or **persulfate** method instead |

---

## 3.4 Method Comparison Matrix (For Theory Exam)

| Parameter | Kjeldahl (TKN) | Nessler Colorimetric | Phenate (Indophenol) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Range | $1$–$1000\ \text{mg/L}$ | $0.05$–$5\ \text{mg/L}$ | $0.01$–$5\ \text{mg/L}$ |
| Principle | Distillation + titration | Direct color (yellow-brown) | Direct color (blue, indophenol) |
| Apparatus | Kjeldahl apparatus | Spectrophotometer at $425\ \text{nm}$ | Spectrophotometer at $640\ \text{nm}$ |
| Time | $3$–$4\ \text{hrs}$ | $30\ \text{min}$ | $45\ \text{min}$ |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | High | High |
| Interference | Hg, Cl⁻ | Ca, Mg, Fe (hardness) | Phenol contamination |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva-Voce Point:** The Nessler reagent is an **alkaline solution of potassium tetraiodomercurate(II)**, $\text{K}_2[\text{HgI}_4]$, which reacts with $\text{NH}_3$ to form a yellow-brown colloid.

---

## 3.5 Concentration Units — Critical Conversion (Often Tested!)

$$1\ \text{mg/L as NH}_3\text{-N} = 1.216\ \text{mg/L as NH}_3$$

$$1\ \text{mg/L as NH}_3\text{-N} = 1.286\ \text{mg/L as NH}_4^+$$

$$1\ \text{mg/L as NO}_3^-\text{-N} = 4.427\ \text{mg/L as NO}_3^-$$

$$1\ \text{mg/L as NO}_2^-\text{-N} = 3.285\ \text{mg/L as NO}_2^-$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion factor formula:**
> $$\text{Concentration as Compound} = \text{Concentration as N} \times \frac{\text{Molecular weight of compound}}{14}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Nitrogen Cycle in Wastewater Treatment (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DOMESTIC SEWAGE INLET"] --> nodeB["ORGANIC NITROGEN Proteins Amino Acids Urea"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["AMMONIFICATION NH3 / NH4+"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["NITROSOMONAS BACTERIA Aerobic Tank 1"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["NITRITE NO2-"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["NITROBACTER BACTERIA Aerobic Tank 2"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["NITRATE NO3-"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["DENITRIFICATION Anoxic Zone"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["NITROGEN GAS N2 ATMOSPHERE"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ["LAB KJELDAHL TKN NH3-N"]
    nodeG --> nodeK["LAB SPECTROPHOTO NO3-N"]
    nodeE --> nodeL["LAB COLORIMETRY NO2-N"]
    nodeJ --> nodeM["WASTEWATER DISCHARGE REPORT"]
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Domestic sewage enters a treatment plant. Organic-N is first **ammonified** to $\text{NH}_3/\text{NH}_4^+$ by heterotrophic bacteria. Then **Nitrosomonas** oxidize it to $\text{NO}_2^-$, and **Nitrobacter** oxidize it to $\text{NO}_3^-$. Finally, **denitrifying bacteria** in an anoxic zone convert $\text{NO}_3^-$ to harmless $\text{N}_2$ gas. The three lab methods (Kjeldahl, spectrophotometric for nitrate, colorimetric for nitrite) provide the analytical data for the discharge report.

---

## 4.2 Kjeldahl Apparatus — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Kjeldahl Flask 800 mL with Digested Sample"] -->|NH3 Liberated by NaOH| nodeB["Distillation Head Splash Guard"]
    nodeB -->|Steam + NH3 vapor| nodeC["Condenser Liebig Type"]
    nodeC -->|Condensed NH3 distillate| nodeD["Conical Flask 250 mL with 50 mL 4 percent Boric Acid and Indicator"]
    nodeD -->|NH3 absorbed color changes| nodeE["Burette 0.02 N H2SO4"]
    nodeE -->|Titration to Endpoint| nodeF["Titre Volume Recorded Vs Vb Blank"]
    nodeF -->|Plug into Formula| nodeG["TKN mg per L Calculation"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Function Summary:**
> - **Kjeldahl Flask:** Holds the acid-digested sample.
> - **Distillation Head:** Prevents carryover of $\text{NaOH}$ splash into the distillate.
> - **Liebig Condenser:** Cools the $\text{NH}_3$ vapor back to liquid.
> - **Boric Acid Trap:** Captures $\text{NH}_3$ in a stable, titratable form.
> - **Burette:** Delivers standard acid for the back-titration.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Which Nitrogen Method to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Water Sample Received"] --> nodeB{"Sample Type?"}
    nodeB -->|"Raw Sewage"| nodeC["TKN via Kjeldahl"]
    nodeB -->|"Treated Effluent"| nodeD{"Concentration Level?"}
    nodeD -->|"Greater than 5 mg per L"| nodeE["Distillation plus Titration"]
    nodeD -->|"Less than 5 mg per L"| nodeF["Nessler or Phenate Colorimetric at 425 or 640 nm"]
    nodeB -->|"Drinking Water"| nodeG["NO3-N via UV Spectrophotometry at 220 nm"]
    nodeB -->|"Surface Water"| nodeH["All forms plus TN by Persulfate Digestion"]
    nodeC --> nodeI["Report as TKN in mg per L as N"]
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** When asked *"Which method would you use for a low-concentration drinking water sample?"* — answer **Nessler or Phenate colorimetric** because Kjeldahl loses precision below $1\ \text{mg/L}$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN). What forms of nitrogen does it include and exclude?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — key points only):**

TKN is defined as the **sum of organic nitrogen and ammonia nitrogen** present in a water sample, determined by digestion with concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ followed by distillation and titration with a standard acid.

- **Included:** Organic-N (proteins, amino acids, urea) + Ammonia-N ($\text{NH}_3$ / $\text{NH}_4^+$).
- **Excluded:** Nitrite-N ($\text{NO}_2^-$) and Nitrate-N ($\text{NO}_3^-$). These are determined separately and added to obtain **Total Nitrogen (TN)**.
- **Reaction basis:** $\text{Organic-N} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4,\ \Delta,\ \text{cat.}} (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} \text{NH}_3 \uparrow$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly say Kjeldahl measures "all nitrogen." Deduct **0.5 marks** if nitrite/nitrate exclusion is not stated.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is boric acid (4%) used as the absorbing medium for ammonia in the Kjeldahl distillation step, instead of a standard solution of $\text{HCl}$ or $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — structured):**

1. **Weak Acid Property:** Boric acid ($\text{H}_3\text{BO}_3$) is a **weak monoprotic acid** with $pK_a \approx 9.24$. It does not appreciably dissociate, hence it does **not interfere** with the subsequent titration. The trapped $\text{NH}_3$ simply converts the boric acid to ammonium borate.
2. **No Back-Titration Required:** With $\text{HCl}$ or $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, the amount of acid neutralized by $\text{NH}_3$ would have to be determined by **back-titration** with a standard base (e.g., $\text{NaOH}$), introducing an additional step and more error.
3. **Direct Titration of the Absorbent:** In the boric acid method, the **entire absorbent solution** is titrated directly with the standard acid. The acid consumed corresponds **stoichiometrically** to the ammonia absorbed — a single, clean titration step.
4. **Color Indicator Compatible:** The boric acid + mixed indicator system gives a sharp endpoint (green → pink), making the result highly reproducible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students who write "because boric acid is cheap" get **0 marks**. The correct justification is the **weak acid / no back-titration** logic.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the principle, procedure, and chemistry involved in the determination of **Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN)** in a wastewater sample. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** $50\ \text{mL}$ of a wastewater sample was analyzed for TKN. The ammonia liberated was absorbed in $50\ \text{mL}$ of 4% boric acid and titrated with $0.02\ \text{N}\ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$. The titre value was $18.6\ \text{mL}$. A blank consumed $0.2\ \text{mL}$. Calculate the **TKN in mg/L**. If the Ammonia-N in the same sample (without digestion) consumed $14.0\ \text{mL}$ of the same acid, calculate the **Organic Nitrogen** and comment on the pollution strength. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 Marks)**

**Principle (2 Marks):**
TKN is the sum of organic-N and ammonia-N. Organic nitrogen compounds are first converted to ammonium sulphate by digestion with concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ in the presence of a catalyst. The ammonium ion is then liberated as free ammonia by the addition of $\text{NaOH}$ and distilled into a boric acid solution. The ammonia is then quantified by titration with a standard acid.

**Reactions (2 Marks):**
$$\text{Organic-N} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\ \text{CuSO}_4} (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$$
$$(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{NH}_3 \uparrow + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_3\text{BO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{BO}_3$$
$$\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{BO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_3\text{BO}_3$$

**Procedure (3 Marks):**
1. **Digestion:** Take $V = 50\ \text{mL}$ of sample in an $800\ \text{mL}$ Kjeldahl flask. Add $10\ \text{mL}$ conc. $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $5\ \text{g}$ $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$, $0.5\ \text{g}$ $\text{CuSO}_4$. Digest in a fume hood until a clear greenish-blue solution is obtained.
2. **Cooling & Dilution:** Cool, add $200\ \text{mL}$ distilled water slowly.
3. **Distillation:** Transfer to distillation unit. Add $40\text{–}50\ \text{mL}$ $\text{NaOH}$ (40%) along the side. Distill $\sim 200\ \text{mL}$ into a flask containing $50\ \text{mL}$ of 4% boric acid with mixed indicator.
4. **Titration:** Titrate the distillate against $0.02\ \text{N}\ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ from green to pink endpoint. Record $V_s$. Repeat blank to get $V_b$.
5. **Calculation:** Use the standard Kjeldahl formula.

**Precautions:** (Bonus half-mark for mentioning fume hood, anti-bumping beads, and indicator immersion).

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $V = 50\ \text{mL}$
- $N = 0.02\ \text{N}\ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$
- TKN titre: $V_s = 18.6\ \text{mL}$; blank $V_b = 0.2\ \text{mL}$
- $\text{NH}_3$-N titre: $V_{s,\text{NH}_3} = 14.0\ \text{mL}$; (same blank $0.2\ \text{mL}$)

**Step 1: TKN Calculation**

$$\text{TKN} = \frac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 14 \times 1000}{V}$$

$$\text{TKN} = \frac{(18.6 - 0.2) \times 0.02 \times 14 \times 1000}{50}$$

$$\text{TKN} = \frac{18.4 \times 0.02 \times 14 \times 1000}{50} = \frac{5152}{50} = 103.04\ \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 2: Ammonia-N Calculation**

$$\text{NH}_3\text{-N} = \frac{(14.0 - 0.2) \times 0.02 \times 14 \times 1000}{50}$$

$$\text{NH}_3\text{-N} = \frac{13.8 \times 0.02 \times 14 \times 1000}{50} = \frac{3864}{50} = 77.28\ \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Organic Nitrogen**

$$\text{Organic-N} = \text{TKN} - \text{NH}_3\text{-N} = 103.04 - 77.28 = 25.76\ \text{mg/L}$$

**Comment on Pollution Strength (1 Mark):**
- For domestic sewage, typical TKN = $20$–$85\ \text{mg/L}$.
- The calculated TKN ($103.04\ \text{mg/L}$) is **above the upper domestic range**, indicating **strong to very strong pollution** — possibly industrial contamination or high-strength septic sewage.
- The high $\text{NH}_3$-N fraction ($77.28$ of $103.04 \approx 75\%$) suggests the organic nitrogen has already been substantially **ammonified**, typical of **septic or old sewage**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** What are the different forms of nitrogen found in water and wastewater? Discuss their significance in environmental engineering. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Nessler method** for determination of ammonia nitrogen. A water sample gave an absorbance of $0.345$ at $425\ \text{nm}$. The calibration data: $0.5\ \text{ppm} \rightarrow 0.080$; $1.0\ \text{ppm} \rightarrow 0.165$; $2.0\ \text{ppm} \rightarrow 0.330$; $4.0\ \text{ppm} \rightarrow 0.665$. Find the ammonia-N concentration in the sample. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 Marks)**

**Forms of Nitrogen (3 Marks):**
1. **Organic Nitrogen** — bound in proteins, amino acids, urea, etc.
2. **Ammonia Nitrogen** — $\text{NH}_3 / \text{NH}_4^+$ (reduced form).
3. **Nitrite Nitrogen** — $\text{NO}_2^-$ (intermediate, unstable).
4. **Nitrate Nitrogen** — $\text{NO}_3^-$ (oxidized, end product).
5. **Molecular Nitrogen** — $\text{N}_2$ (dissolved atmospheric).

**Significance (4 Marks):**
- **Public Health:** $\text{NH}_3$ is toxic to fish at $0.02\ \text{mg/L}$ (un-ionized). Nitrate in drinking water causes **methemoglobinemia ("blue baby syndrome")** in infants.
- **Eutrophication:** Nitrate/Phosphate enrichment leads to algal blooms → dissolved oxygen depletion → fish kill.
- **Wastewater Treatment:** BOD/N ratio determines whether nitrogen removal (nitrification-denitrification) is needed.
- **Self-Purification:** Indicator of the natural stream recovery capacity. Ammonia-rich water is "fresh pollution"; nitrate-rich is "old/oxidized pollution."
- **Drinking Water Standard (IS 10500:2012):** $\text{NO}_3^-\le 45\ \text{mg/L}$; $\text{NO}_2^-\le 0.5\ \text{mg/L}$.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 Marks)**

**Nessler Method Principle (3 Marks):**
- The Nessler reagent is an **alkaline solution of potassium tetraiodomercurate(II), $\text{K}_2[\text{HgI}_4]$**, mixed with $\text{KOH}$.
- It reacts with ammonia (after distillation) to form a **yellow-brown colloidal complex** — *iodo-mercurate-ammonia*, $\text{NH}_2\text{Hg}_2\text{I}_3$ (sometimes written as $\text{NH}_2\text{HgO}\cdot\text{HgI}$).
- Intensity of color is **proportional to ammonia concentration** and measured spectrophotometrically at **$\lambda = 425\ \text{nm}$**.
- Procedure: Distill $\text{NH}_3$ from sample (with buffer, pH 9.5) → collect in boric acid → add Nessler reagent → wait $10$ min → read absorbance.
- **Interferences:** Hardness (Ca, Mg), residual chlorine, iron — removed by EDTA / Rochelle salt / sodium thiosulphate.

**Calibration & Calculation (4 Marks):**

Using the calibration data, perform linear regression. Let $A = m \cdot C + b$:

| $C$ (ppm) | $A$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0.5 | 0.080 |
| 1.0 | 0.165 |
| 2.0 | 0.330 |
| 4.0 | 0.665 |

**Step 1: Check Linearity** — Ratios:
- $0.080/0.5 = 0.160$
- $0.165/1.0 = 0.165$
- $0.330/2.0 = 0.165$
- $0.665/4.0 = 0.1663$

Essentially constant $\approx 0.165$ → linear with **slope $m = 0.165\ \text{ppm}^{-1}$**, intercept $b \approx 0$.

**Step 2: Apply to Sample Absorbance ($A = 0.345$):**

$$C = \frac{A - b}{m} = \frac{0.345 - 0}{0.165}$$

$$C = 2.09\ \text{ppm (mg/L)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{NH}_3\text{-N in sample} \approx 2.09\ \text{mg/L}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning (Common Loss-of-Marks):**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing Nessler as $\text{K}_2[\text{HgI}_2]$ — wrong formula. It is $\text{K}_2[\text{HgI}_4]$ (tetra-iodo).
> - **Mistake 2:** Reporting units as "ppm/L" or "absorbance" — always convert to **mg/L (as N)**.
> - **Mistake 3:** Forgetting to subtract blank absorbance before plugging into the formula.
> - **Mistake 4:** Reporting result with more than 2 decimal places without justification.
> - **Mistake 5:** Using the wrong wavelength ($540\ \text{nm}$ is for some other methods; Nessler is **$425\ \text{nm}$**).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Determination of Nitrogen (KTU Module 2)**

- ✅ **TKN = Organic-N + Ammonia-N** (NOT nitrite or nitrate).
- ✅ **Total-N = TKN + $\text{NO}_2^-$ + $\text{NO}_3^-$**.
- ✅ **Kjeldahl Method = Digestion ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ + catalyst) → Distillation ($\text{NaOH}$) → Absorption (Boric acid 4%) → Titration (Standard acid).**
- ✅ **Catalyst** in digestion = $\text{CuSO}_4$ / $\text{HgO}$ / $\text{Se}$; **Boiling-point elevator** = $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $\text{TKN (mg/L)} = \dfrac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 14 \times 1000}{V}$.
- ✅ **Equivalent weight of Nitrogen = 14 g/eq** (always; never 14.01 unless specified).
- ✅ **Boric acid is weak** → direct titration of absorbent, no back-titration.
- ✅ **Nessler** → yellow-brown colloid, **425 nm**; **Phenate** → indophenol blue, **640 nm**.
- ✅ **Nitrate in drinking water** $\le 45\ \text{mg/L}$ (as $\text{NO}_3^-$) per IS 10500.
- ✅ **Conversion:** $1\ \text{mg/L as N} \times 4.427 = \text{mg/L as NO}_3^-$.
- ✅ **Free $\text{NH}_3$ toxicity** increases with **pH** and **temperature**.
- ✅ **Free $\text{NH}_3$ fraction formula:** $[\text{NH}_3] = \dfrac{[\text{TAN}]}{1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)}}$.
- ✅ **Nitrification** = $\text{NH}_3 \to \text{NO}_2^- \to \text{NO}_3^-$ (aerobic, *Nitrosomonas* then *Nitrobacter*).
- ✅ **Denitrification** = $\text{NO}_3^- \to \text{N}_2 \uparrow$ (anoxic, *Pseudomonas*, etc.).
- ✅ **Don't use $\text{HNO}_3$** in digestion — it adds exogenous nitrogen.
- ✅ **Don't forget the blank** — subtract $V_b$ from $V_s$ **before** plugging into the formula.

topics.title: Determination of Total Phosphorous
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Total Phosphorous

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Total Phosphorus (TP)** is defined as the sum of all forms of phosphorus present in a water or wastewater sample, including **orthophosphates**, **condensed (poly)phosphates**, and **organic phosphorus** bound in particulate and dissolved organic matter. The determination involves an oxidative acid digestion step that hydrolyzes polyphosphates and mineralizes organic phosphorus, converting the entire pool into dissolved **orthophosphate (PO₄³⁻)**, which is then quantified colorimetrically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must understand that *Total Phosphorus is NOT measured directly*. The lab procedure first converts all P species into orthophosphate, which is the only analytically measurable form. The official reference is **APHA Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater, Method 4500-P** (Persulfate Digestion / Ascorbic Acid Method).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **chocolate box** containing three compartments:
1. **White chocolate squares** (orthophosphate — already in the usable form)
2. **Wrapped chocolate balls** (polyphosphates — need to be unwrapped/hydrolyzed)
3. **Chocolate chips inside cookies** (organically bound phosphorus — need to be crushed/digested out)

To know the *total amount of chocolate in the box*, you cannot just count the white squares. You must:
- **Melt everything** (acid-persulfate digestion at high temperature),
- **Unwrap** the balls,
- **Crush** the cookies,
- And then measure the unified molten chocolate using a color reaction (the molybdenum blue color).

That single color reading is your **Total Phosphorus**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forms of Phosphorus in Water**
> - **Dissolved Orthophosphate (PO₄³⁻, HPO₄²⁻, H₂PO₄⁻):** Directly bioavailable.
> - **Condensed Phosphates (polyphosphates like Na₅P₃O₁₀):** Used in detergents, hydrolyze slowly.
> - **Organic Phosphorus (DNA, phospholipids, pesticides):** Bound in biomass and humic substances.
> - **Particulate Phosphorus:** Adsorbed onto sediments and biomass.

### Standard Metric Used
The result is conventionally reported in **milligrams per litre as Phosphorus (mg/L as P)** — *not* as phosphate (PO₄). This convention avoids ambiguity because molecular weights differ.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Reporting the result "as PO₄" instead of "as P" costs marks. Always state the units. Conversion factor: $\text{mg/L as PO}_4 = \text{mg/L as P} \times 2.29$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The standard procedure is a **two-stage chemical process**:

**Stage 1 — Oxidative Acid Digestion (Persulfate Method)**
- Sample is acidified with **sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)** and boiled with **ammonium persulfate ((NH₄)₂S₂O₈)** in an autoclave (or hot plate at 100 °C for 30–40 min).
- The strong oxidizer breaks P–O–P bonds (polyphosphates) and P–C bonds (organophosphates).
- The condensed P–O–P network hydrolyses: $\text{P}_3\text{O}_{10}^{5-} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 3\text{PO}_4^{3-} + 4\text{H}^+$
- All phosphorus is now present as **orthophosphate (PO₄³⁻)**.

**Stage 2 — Ascorbic Acid Color Development**
- The digested, neutralized sample reacts with a **combined reagent** containing:
  - **Ammonium molybdate** — source of Mo(VI)
  - **Antimony potassium tartrate** — catalyst
  - **Ascorbic acid** — reducing agent
- In acidic medium, orthophosphate reacts with molybdate to form **molybdophosphoric acid (12-molybdophosphate)**.
- Ascorbic acid reduces Mo(VI) → Mo(V), giving the intensely colored **heteropoly molybdenum blue complex**.
- Absorbance is measured at **880 nm** (preferred) or **690 nm** using a **UV-Vis Spectrophotometer**.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Is Done |
|---|---|
| Acid-persulfate digestion | Releases P from biomass and hydrolyzes polyphosphates — only route to "total" P |
| Autoclaving at 121 °C (1.1 kg/cm²) | Provides controlled, uniform digestion temperature |
| Antimony tartrate catalyst | Accelerates Mo-blue formation, sharpens color |
| Ascorbic acid as reductant | Stable, non-toxic, replaces stannous chloride (older method) |
| Measurement at 880 nm | Higher sensitivity; lower interference from humic color than 690 nm |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas — Memorize These for ESE**

$$
\text{Total P (mg/L as P)} = \frac{C \times V_f}{V_s}
$$

where:
- $C$ = concentration read from calibration curve (mg/L)
- $V_f$ = final volume of digested/color-developed sample (mL)
- $V_s$ = volume of original sample taken (mL)

$$
\text{As PO}_4 \; (\text{mg/L}) = \text{As P} \; (\text{mg/L}) \times 2.29
$$

$$
\text{As P}_2\text{O}_5 \; (\text{mg/L}) = \text{As P} \; (\text{mg/L}) \times 2.29
$$

**Calibration curve** is built using standard **KH₂PO₄ (potassium dihydrogen phosphate)** solutions (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/L as P). The relationship follows the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$
A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell
$$

where $A$ is absorbance, $\varepsilon$ is molar absorptivity, $c$ is concentration, and $\ell$ is path length (typically 1 cm cuvette).

**Standard reference values (KTU board favourite):**

| Water Type | Typical TP Range (mg/L as P) |
|---|---|
| Unpolluted river water | 0.01 – 0.05 |
| Lake (mesotrophic) | 0.01 – 0.1 |
| Eutrophic lake | 0.1 – 1.0 |
| Municipal sewage (raw) | 4 – 15 |
| Industrial wastewater | 10 – 100 (variable) |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Eutrophication control:** TP > 0.02 mg/L in lakes triggers algal blooms → dissolved oxygen crash → fish kills.
- **Effluent discharge compliance:** CPCB/SPCB norms fix TP < 2 mg/L (inland surface discharge) for STPs.
- **Biological Nutrient Removal (BNR):** Design of anaerobic–anoxic–oxic tanks depends on influent TP/COD ratio.
- **Industrial process control:** Fertilizer, pulp/paper, and food industries monitor TP for product quality and waste recovery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### A. Derivation of the Standard Stock Solution Concentration
The primary standard is **anhydrous KH₂PO₄** (M = 136.09 g/mol, contains 30.97 g P/mol).

**Question context (typical KTU problem):** "Prepare 1 L of 50 mg/L phosphorus stock solution from KH₂PO₄."

**Step 1 — Find the mass of KH₂PO₄ needed to deliver 50 mg of P in 1 L.**

$$
\text{Mass of P required} = 50 \; \text{mg} = 0.050 \; \text{g}
$$

Since 1 mole of KH₂PO₄ (136.09 g) contains 1 mole of P (30.97 g):

$$
\frac{\text{Mass of KH}_2\text{PO}_4}{\text{Mass of P}} = \frac{136.09}{30.97} = 4.394
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{Mass of KH}_2\text{PO}_4 = 0.050 \times 4.394 = 0.2197 \; \text{g}
$$

**Step 2 — Dissolve 0.2197 g of KH₂PO₄ in distilled water, add 1 mL H₂SO₄ (1+1), and dilute to 1000 mL.** This gives a 50 mg/L P stock.

**Step 3 — Working standards:** Dilute 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10 mL of stock into 50 mL volumetric flasks → 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10 mg/L standards.

### B. Worked Numerical Problem (Calibration + Sample Calculation)

**Given:**
- Five standards: 0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0 mg/L P
- Absorbance (880 nm): 0.000, 0.092, 0.184, 0.371, 0.928
- Sample: 25 mL of raw sewage digested, made up to 50 mL
- Sample absorbance: 0.456
- Cuvette path length: 1 cm

**Step 1 — Establish calibration equation using least-squares linear regression.**

For Beer-Lambert linearity: $A = m \cdot c + b$

Calculate slope $m$:

$$
m = \frac{\sum (c_i - \bar{c})(A_i - \bar{A})}{\sum (c_i - \bar{c})^2}
$$

Computing:

$$
\bar{c} = \frac{0 + 0.5 + 1.0 + 2.0 + 5.0}{5} = 1.7 \; \text{mg/L}
$$

$$
\bar{A} = \frac{0.000 + 0.092 + 0.184 + 0.371 + 0.928}{5} = 0.315
$$

Sum of $(c_i - \bar{c})(A_i - \bar{A})$:

$$
(0 - 1.7)(0.000 - 0.315) + (0.5 - 1.7)(0.092 - 0.315) + (1.0 - 1.7)(0.184 - 0.315) + (2.0 - 1.7)(0.371 - 0.315) + (5.0 - 1.7)(0.928 - 0.315)
$$

Evaluating each term:

$$
(−1.7)(−0.315) = 0.5355
$$

$$
(−1.2)(−0.223) = 0.2676
$$

$$
(−0.7)(−0.131) = 0.0917
$$

$$
(0.3)(0.056) = 0.0168
$$

$$
(3.3)(0.613) = 2.0229
$$

$$
\sum = 0.5355 + 0.2676 + 0.0917 + 0.0168 + 2.0229 = 2.9345
$$

Sum of $(c_i - \bar{c})^2$:

$$
2.89 + 1.44 + 0.49 + 0.09 + 10.89 = 15.80
$$

$$
m = \frac{2.9345}{15.80} = 0.1857
$$

Intercept $b = \bar{A} - m \bar{c}$:

$$
b = 0.315 - (0.1857 \times 1.7) = 0.315 - 0.3157 = -0.0007 \approx 0
$$

**Calibration equation:**

$$
A = 0.1857 \, c
$$

**Step 2 — Read sample concentration from calibration:**

$$
c = \frac{A}{m} = \frac{0.456}{0.1857} = 2.455 \; \text{mg/L}
$$

This is the concentration in the **digested, diluted** sample.

**Step 3 — Apply the dilution correction formula** $\text{TP} = \dfrac{C \times V_f}{V_s}$:

$$
\text{TP} = \frac{2.455 \times 50}{25} = 4.91 \; \text{mg/L as P}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to as PO₄ (only if asked):**

$$
\text{TP as PO}_4 = 4.91 \times 2.29 = 11.24 \; \text{mg/L}
$$

### C. Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error and Safeguards

| Source of Error | Mechanism | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Glassware contamination | Trace P from detergents | Rinse with 1:1 HCl, then P-free water |
| Incomplete digestion | Residual polyphosphates | Maintain 121 °C for 30 min; use fresh persulfate |
| Interfering ions (As, F⁻, SiO₂) | Form competing heteropoly blues | Add NaF or use 880 nm where As/Si interference is low |
| Turbidity in final solution | Scatters light → false high A | Filter through 0.45 µm membrane after digestion |
| Color fading | Mo-blue fades in >30 min | Read absorbance within 10–30 min strictly |
| Reagent blank not zero | Impure reagents | Subtract blank absorbance from all readings |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Process Flow of Total Phosphorus Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Water or Wastewater Sample] --> B[Measure 25-50 mL Aliquot]
    B --> C[Add 1 mL H2SO4 and 0.4 g Ammonium Persulfate]
    C --> D[Autoclave at 121 deg C for 30 min]
    D --> E[Cool and Add NaOH to Neutralize pH to 7]
    E --> F[Add 1 mL Combined Reagent - Molybdate Antimony Ascorbic Acid]
    F --> G[Wait 10 to 30 min for Color Development]
    G --> H[Measure Absorbance at 880 nm using UV-Vis Spectrophotometer]
    H --> I{Compare with Calibration Curve}
    I --> J[Calculate Total Phosphorus in mg per L as P]
    J --> K[Report Result with Sample Identification]
```

### Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Analytical System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIG[Digestion Stage]
        D1[Sample In] --> D2[Acid Persulfate Addition]
        D2 --> D3[Autoclave 121 deg C]
        D3 --> D4[Cool and Neutralize]
        D4 --> D5[Orthophosphate Solution Out]
    end
    subgraph COL[Color Development Stage]
        C1[Orthophosphate Solution] --> C2[Add Molybdate Antimony Mixture]
        C2 --> C3[Add Ascorbic Acid]
        C3 --> C4[Incubate 10 to 30 min]
        C4 --> C5[Molybdenum Blue Complex]
    end
    subgraph DET[Detection Stage]
        T1[Molybdenum Blue Complex] --> T2[Cuvette in Spectrophotometer]
        T2 --> T3[Light Source 880 nm]
        T3 --> T4[Detector]
        T4 --> T5[Absorbance Reading]
    end
    subgraph CAL[Calibration and Computation]
        Q1[KH2PO4 Standards] --> Q2[Calibration Curve A vs c]
        Q2 --> Q3[Read Sample c from Curve]
        T5 --> Q3
        Q3 --> Q4[Apply Dilution Factor]
        Q4 --> Q5[Total Phosphorus mg per L as P]
    end
    D5 --> C1
    C5 --> T1
```

### Diagram 3 — Decision Logic for Selecting Phosphorus Test Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Phosphorus Test Required] --> Q{What form of P is of interest?}
    Q -->|Dissolved reactive P only| R1[Skip Digestion - Direct Ascorbic Acid Method 4500 P E]
    Q -->|Total dissolved P| R2[Filter then Persulfate Digest]
    Q -->|Total P including particulate| R3[Whole Sample Persulfate Digestion 4500 P B]
    R3 --> S[Ascorbic Acid Color Finish 4500 P E]
    R2 --> S
    R1 --> S
    S --> T[Measure Absorbance 880 nm]
    T --> U[Report in mg per L as P]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**"Differentiate between orthophosphate, polyphosphate, and organic phosphorus. Why must all three be converted to orthophosphate for total phosphorus analysis?"**

**Model Answer:**
- **Orthophosphate (PO₄³⁻):** The simplest, directly reactive form; no P–O–P bonds.
- **Polyphosphate (condensed P):** Linear or cyclic chains of P–O–P bonds (e.g., pyrophosphate, tripolyphosphate). Used in detergents; hydrolyzes slowly in water.
- **Organic phosphorus:** P covalently bonded to carbon in biomolecules such as phospholipids, nucleic acids, ATP, and pesticides.
- **Why conversion is essential:** Only orthophosphate reacts with ammonium molybdate to form the measurable molybdenum blue complex. Polyphosphates and organic P must be mineralized/hydrolyzed by acid-persulfate digestion into PO₄³⁻ before colorimetric quantification; otherwise the analytical signal would underestimate the true P content.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"Why is ammonium persulfate used in the digestion step instead of concentrated nitric acid alone?"**

**Model Answer:**
- Ammonium persulfate ((NH₄)₂S₂O₈) is a **strong oxidizing agent** that simultaneously hydrolyzes polyphosphates and oxidizes organic P to PO₄³⁻ at moderate temperatures (≤ 121 °C).
- It avoids the use of hazardous concentrated HNO₃–HClO₄ mixtures that can volatilize P at high temperatures and pose safety risks.
- Persulfate digestion is **reproducible, safe, and APHA-recommended** for routine water and wastewater analysis.
- It also minimizes silica and arsenic interference, giving a cleaner final matrix for color development.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **omit the dilution factor** in the final formula — full 2 marks lost. Always write $\text{TP} = \dfrac{C \times V_f}{V_s}$ explicitly.
> - Students report the result as PO₄³⁻ instead of as P. State the form every time.
> - Calibration curve plot is skipped in answers — losing 2 marks. Always show the slope and intercept.
> - Digestion temperature and time are forgotten. Always state **121 °C for 30 min** when citing the persulfate method.
> - Interferences (As, Si, F⁻, turbidity) are not mentioned — examiners allocate 2 marks for "awareness of limitations."

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
