**(a) Describe the principle and procedure for the determination of Total Phosphorus in a wastewater sample by the persulfate digestion–ascorbic acid method.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A 25 mL wastewater sample was digested with persulfate and made up to 50 mL. After color development, the absorbance was 0.412. The calibration equation obtained is $A = 0.182 \, c$ (where c is in mg/L). Calculate the Total Phosphorus in mg/L as P and as PO₄³⁻. State whether the value indicates compliance with the inland discharge norm of TP ≤ 2 mg/L.** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Principle (3 marks):**
- Total P = orthophosphate + acid-hydrolyzable polyphosphates + organic P.
- Persulfate digestion in acidic medium converts all forms to orthophosphate.
- Orthophosphate reacts with ammonium molybdate in the presence of antimony tartrate (catalyst) to form **molybdophosphoric acid**, which is reduced by **ascorbic acid** to intensely blue **molybdenum blue complex**.
- Absorbance measured at **880 nm** is proportional to P concentration (Beer–Lambert law).

**Reagents (2 marks):**
- Ammonium persulfate (solid) and 1:1 H₂SO₄
- Combined reagent: ammonium molybdate + antimony potassium tartrate + ascorbic acid
- Stock P standard: 50 mg/L from KH₂PO₄

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Pipette 25 mL well-mixed sample into 50 mL Erlenmeyer flask.
2. Add 1 mL H₂SO₄ (1+1) and 0.4 g ammonium persulfate.
3. Autoclave at 121 °C (≈ 1.1 kg/cm²) for 30 min; cool.
4. Add 1 drop phenolphthalein; neutralize with 1 N NaOH to faint pink.
5. Transfer to 50 mL volumetric flask; add 1 mL combined reagent; dilute to mark.
6. Wait 10–30 min; measure absorbance at 880 nm against reagent blank.
7. Read concentration from calibration curve; apply dilution factor.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Concentration from calibration:

$$
c = \frac{A}{0.182} = \frac{0.412}{0.182} = 2.264 \; \text{mg/L}
$$

This is the P concentration in the **diluted, digested** solution.

**Step 2:** Apply dilution factor $V_f / V_s = 50 / 25 = 2$:

$$
\text{TP as P} = 2.264 \times 2 = 4.53 \; \text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to PO₄³⁻ form:

$$
\text{TP as PO}_4 = 4.53 \times 2.29 = 10.37 \; \text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 4:** Compliance check:

$$
4.53 \; \text{mg/L} \; > \; 2 \; \text{mg/L (norm)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The wastewater **does NOT comply** with the CPCB inland discharge norm; tertiary chemical treatment (alum/ferric coagulation or enhanced biological P removal) is required.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a) Explain the chemistry of molybdenum blue formation. Why is antimony potassium tartrate added, and why is the absorbance measured at 880 nm rather than 690 nm?** **(7 marks)**

**(b) In a calibration run, the following absorbances were obtained for KH₂PO₄ standards at 880 nm using a 1 cm cuvette:**

| Standard (mg/L as P) | Absorbance |
|---|---|
| 0.0 | 0.000 |
| 0.5 | 0.094 |
| 1.0 | 0.186 |
| 2.0 | 0.371 |
| 4.0 | 0.745 |

**Plot the calibration curve, find the slope, and determine the Total Phosphorus (mg/L as P) of a sample whose absorbance is 0.310 (after applying dilution factor 2).** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Molybdenum Blue Chemistry (3 marks):**
- Orthophosphate condenses with molybdate ions in acidic solution:

$$
\text{PO}_4^{3-} + 12\,\text{MoO}_4^{2-} + 27\,\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{PMo}_{12}\text{O}_{40} + 12\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

- The 12-molybdophosphoric acid formed is reduced by ascorbic acid (Mo⁶⁺ → Mo⁵⁺), giving a mixed-valence blue complex:

$$
\text{H}_3\text{PMo}_{12}\text{O}_{40} \xrightarrow{\text{ascorbic acid}} \text{H}_3\text{PMo}_{12}\text{O}_{40}^* \; (\text{molybdenum blue})
$$

**Role of Antimony Potassium Tartrate (2 marks):**
- Acts as a **catalyst** that accelerates the formation of the 12-molybdo complex and stabilises the blue color.
- It shifts the reaction kinetics so that the Mo-blue develops rapidly at room temperature without requiring a heating step.
- Improves reproducibility and reduces the variability of color intensity between samples.

**Why 880 nm Preferred over 690 nm (2 marks):**
- At 880 nm, the molar absorptivity is higher, giving **greater sensitivity** for low-P samples (e.g., lake water).
- Interference from humic substances (yellow-brown organic color in natural waters) is **negligible at 880 nm** because humics absorb strongly in the 250–450 nm region and very little above 700 nm.
- Silica and arsenate form weaker heteropoly acids that absorb more strongly at 690 nm; measuring at 880 nm **minimises these interferences**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Linear regression:**

$$
\bar{c} = \frac{0 + 0.5 + 1.0 + 2.0 + 4.0}{5} = 1.5
$$

$$
\bar{A} = \frac{0 + 0.094 + 0.186 + 0.371 + 0.745}{5} = 0.2792
$$

$\sum (c_i - \bar{c})(A_i - \bar{A})$:

$$
(−1.5)(−0.2792) + (−1.0)(−0.1852) + (−0.5)(−0.0932) + (0.5)(0.0918) + (2.5)(0.4658)
$$

$$
= 0.4188 + 0.1852 + 0.0466 + 0.0459 + 1.1645 = 1.8610
$$

$\sum (c_i - \bar{c})^2$:

$$
2.25 + 1.00 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 6.25 = 10.00
$$

**Slope:**

$$
m = \frac{1.8610}{10.00} = 0.1861
$$

**Intercept:**

$$
b = 0.2792 - (0.1861 \times 1.5) = 0.2792 - 0.2792 = 0
$$

**Calibration equation:**

$$
A = 0.1861 \, c
$$

**Step 2 — Read sample concentration (already corrected for dilution, so direct):**

$$
c = \frac{0.310}{0.1861} = 1.666 \; \text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply dilution factor (since the problem states "after applying dilution factor 2" — this means the absorbance is of the diluted sample, hence the actual TP = c × 2):**

$$
\text{Total Phosphorus} = 1.666 \times 2 = 3.33 \; \text{mg/L as P}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Re-read the problem statement carefully:** If the problem says "absorbance 0.310 after applying dilution factor 2", the actual TP = c × 2. Always confirm whether the absorbance corresponds to the **original** or the **diluted** sample before final reporting.

**Conclusion:** TP = **3.33 mg/L as P**, which exceeds the 2 mg/L CPCB norm; treatment is required.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Total Phosphorus = ortho-P + poly-P + organic-P** — all converted to ortho-P via acid-persulfate digestion.
- **Reference method:** APHA 4500-P B (digestion) followed by 4500-P E (ascorbic acid).
- **Digestion conditions:** 121 °C, 30 min, with (NH₄)₂S₂O₈ + H₂SO₄.
- **Wavelength:** 880 nm (preferred) or 690 nm.
- **Standard:** Anhydrous KH₂PO₄ (1 mole = 1 mole P).
- **Reporting units:** mg/L **as P** (not as PO₄ unless asked).
- **Key formula:** $\text{TP} = \dfrac{C \times V_f}{V_s}$.
- **Conversion factor:** P to PO₄ → multiply by **2.29**.
- **Interferences:** As, F⁻, SiO₂, humic color, turbidity, glassware contamination.
- **Catalyst:** Antimony potassium tartrate (accelerates Mo-blue formation).
- **Reductant:** Ascorbic acid (replaces toxic stannous chloride).
- **Stability:** Mo-blue is stable for 30 min only — read absorbance within 10–30 min of color development.
- **Calibration range:** Typically 0 to 5 mg/L as P; dilute samples exceeding 5 mg/L.
- **Storage:** Persulfate reagent is hygroscopic — store desiccated.
- **Reagent blank:** Always run a blank through the full procedure (digestion + color) and subtract its absorbance.
- **Compliance check:** CPCB TP norm for STP discharge into inland surface water = **≤ 2 mg/L**; into marine coastal waters = **≤ 5 mg/L**.
- **Eutrophication threshold:** TP > 0.02 mg/L in fresh lakes triggers algal bloom risk.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of B.O.D
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of B.O.D

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (B.O.D.)** is defined as the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO), expressed in **milligrams per litre (mg/L)** or **parts per million (ppm)**, required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the decomposable organic matter present in a water sample at a specified temperature over a specified incubation period.

The universally accepted standard test condition, prescribed by **IS 3025 (Part 44) : 1993** and **Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater (APHA, 23rd Edition)**, is:

- **Incubation Temperature:** $20^\circ\text{C} \pm 1^\circ\text{C}$
- **Incubation Period:** **5 days**
- **Result Notation:** $\text{BOD}_5$ at $20^\circ\text{C}$

$$\text{BOD}_5^{20^\circ\text{C}} = \text{Quantity of O}_2 \text{ consumed (mg/L) in 5 days at } 20^\circ\text{C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 5 days and 20°C?** The standard was historically fixed because British rivers in the early 20th century had a maximum natural flow time of about 5 days from source to sea, and 20°C approximates a reasonable warm-season average. It is the *most reproducible* and globally comparable index of organic pollution load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you place a sealed aquarium full of river water in a dark cupboard at room temperature for 5 days. Inside are tiny bacteria, like microscopic "pacmen." The only food available to them is the dissolved organic pollution (rotting leaves, sewage, food waste). As they eat this food, they consume the dissolved oxygen in the water just as we consume oxygen while eating lunch.

- **BOD is essentially a "lunch meter"** for bacteria — it tells you how much oxygen the bacteria will eat in 5 days.
- A **high BOD** = lots of organic pollution = lots of bacterial feasting = oxygen depletion = fish suffocation.
- A **low BOD** (typically $< 2\;\text{mg/L}$) = clean, oligotrophic water.

### 1.3 Engineering Significance & Standard Metrics

| Water Quality Classification | BOD Range (mg/L) | Real-world Reference |
|------------------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Very Pure Water | $< 1$ | Mountain spring, glacier melt |
| Slightly Polluted | $1 - 2$ | Treated drinking water effluent |
| Moderately Polluted | $3 - 5$ | River downstream of small town |
| Polluted | $5 - 10$ | River downstream of urban discharge |
| Highly Polluted | $> 10$ | Raw municipal sewage ($100 - 400$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Untreated domestic sewage typically exhibits a BOD of **$200 - 400\;\text{mg/L}$**, while the CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) disposal standard for inland surface water discharge is **$\le 30\;\text{mg/L}$**. Therefore, municipal sewage must be reduced by ~85 – 92% before disposal — this is the *raison d'être* of every sewage treatment plant (STP).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal gas law constant:** $R = 0.0821\;\text{L·atm·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$
- **Molar mass of O$_2$:** $32\;\text{g/mol}$
- **Saturation DO at 20°C and 1 atm:** $DOsat = 9.17\;\text{mg/L}$ (pure water)
- **Minimum residual DO required in BOD bottle:** $\ge 1\;\text{mg/L}$ (and depletion $\ge 2\;\text{mg/L}$)
- **Maximum sample volume for valid BOD:** To ensure $\ge 2\;\text{mg/L}$ depletion
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why BOD Exists

When organic matter enters a water body, indigenous aerobic heterotrophic bacteria utilize the organic carbon as a substrate (food) and molecular oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor in their respiratory chain. The overall stoichiometry, for a generic carbohydrate (glucose, C$_6$H$_{12}$O$_6$), is:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{bacteria}} 6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O + \text{new bacterial cells (sludge)}$$

> **Engineering Translation:** Each gram of carbonaceous organic matter theoretically demands a stoichiometric oxygen demand. BOD is the practical, time-limited measurement of this demand *in vitro*.

## 2.2 The Two-Stage BOD Curve (Carbonaceous vs Nitrogenous)

BOD exertion is **not instantaneous**. It follows a characteristic two-stage oxygen uptake curve:

| Stage | Duration | Substrate Oxidized | Biochemical Process |
|-------|----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Stage 1 (Carbonaceous)** | $0 - 8$ days (peak at day 5) | Carbon-based organics (C$_6$H$_{12}$O$_6$, etc.) | Respiration by heterotrophs |
| **Stage 2 (Nitrogenous)** | $8 - 20$ days | Ammonia → Nitrite → Nitrate | Nitrification by *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter* |

$$C_{NH_4^+} + 2\,O_2 \rightarrow C_{NO_2^-} + 2\,H^+ + H_2O$$
$$2\,C_{NO_2^-} + O_2 \rightarrow 2\,C_{NO_3^-}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** $\text{BOD}_5$ at $20^\circ\text{C}$ deliberately measures *only the carbonaceous* demand. If nitrification is not suppressed (using **allyl thiourea, ATU**), the measured value can be inflated by $30 - 100\%$, giving false high readings.

## 2.3 The First-Order Kinetics Model (BOD Rate Equation)

The rate of oxygen uptake follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to remaining oxidizable substrate:

$$\frac{dL_t}{dt} = -k \cdot L_t$$

where:
- $L_t$ = remaining oxidizable organic load at time $t$ (mg/L)
- $k$ = deoxygenation rate constant (day$^{-1}$), typically $0.1 - 0.35$ for municipal sewage
- $L_0$ = ultimate BOD = $L_u$ (mg/L)

Integrating from $t = 0$ (where $L_0 = L_u$) to time $t$:

$$L_t = L_u \cdot e^{-k t}$$

The **oxygen exerted up to time $t$** is the difference between the initial and the remaining load:

$$y_t = L_u - L_t = L_u \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$$

where $y_t$ = BOD exerted in time $t$. For the standard 5-day test:

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = L_u \left(1 - e^{-5k}\right)}$$

> **Engineering Utility:** This equation allows us to forecast the oxygen sag curve in a receiving stream (Streeter-Phelps model) and is the foundation of every river water quality assessment.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f}{P}$ | Unseeded direct dilution BOD | mg/L |
| 2 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f}{P} \;\text{(seeded)}$ | Seeded sample (low-microbe water) | mg/L |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{V_s}{V_s + V_d}$ | Decimal volumetric fraction of sample (dimensionless) | — |
| 4 | $f = \dfrac{V_{seed,\;sample}}{V_{seed,\;blank}}$ | Seed correction factor | — |
| 5 | $y_t = L_u \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$ | BOD exerted in time $t$ (first-order) | mg/L |
| 6 | $L_u = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$ | Ultimate BOD back-calculation | mg/L |
| 7 | $k = -\dfrac{1}{t} \ln \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{BOD}_t}{\text{BOD}_u}\right)$ | Rate constant from BOD data | day$^{-1}$ |
| 8 | $DOsat(20^\circ C) = 9.17$ | Saturation DO at standard conditions | mg/L |

**Variable Legend (critical for KTU problems):**

- $D_1$ = DO of diluted sample at day 0 (immediately after preparation)
- $D_2$ = DO of diluted sample at day 5 (after incubation)
- $B_1$ = DO of dilution water blank at day 0
- $B_2$ = DO of dilution water blank at day 5
- $V_s$ = Volume of sample (mL)
- $V_d$ = Volume of dilution water (mL)
- $f$ = seed ratio; for unseeded samples, $f = 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Validation Check (worth 1 mark in viva):** The BOD test is valid only if:
> (i) $D_2 \ge 1\;\text{mg/L}$ (residual DO)
> (ii) $D_1 - D_2 \ge 2\;\text{mg/L}$ (depletion)
> (iii) No air bubbles visible in BOD bottle seal.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: The Standard Dilution-Formula from First Principles

### Background Setup
The BOD bottle (typically $300\;\text{mL}$ capacity) contains a mixture of:
- $V_s$ mL of wastewater sample
- $V_d$ mL of dilution water (nutrient-buffered, aerated, $20^\circ$C)
- Optional $V_{\text{seed}}$ mL of seed culture

The dissolved oxygen is initially saturated in dilution water ($D_1 \approx 9.17\;\text{mg/L}$). After 5 days of microbial respiration, the oxygen has dropped to $D_2$.

### Step 1 — Define Oxygen Consumed per Litre of Diluted Sample

The mass of oxygen consumed in 5 days within the BOD bottle, per litre of diluted mixture, is:

$$\Delta D_{\text{diluted}} = D_1 - D_2 \quad \text{(mg/L, in the diluted sample)}$$

$$\Delta B_{\text{blank}} = B_1 - B_2 \quad \text{(mg/L, correction for any DO drop in blank due to residual organics in dilution water or seed respiration)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Seed Correction

The blank depletion $B_1 - B_2$ is due to the seed culture's own respiration. We must correct the sample's apparent depletion by subtracting the seed contribution:

$$\Delta D_{\text{corrected}} = (D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f$$

where $f = \dfrac{\text{seed volume in sample bottle}}{\text{seed volume in blank bottle}}$.

> **Logic:** If the sample bottle has *more* seed than the blank, the correction is amplified proportionally.

### Step 3 — Convert "per litre of diluted mixture" to "per litre of original sample"

Only the fraction $P = \dfrac{V_s}{V_s + V_d}$ of the bottle is the original wastewater. The oxygen demand must be ascribed *only* to the original wastewater fraction:

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f}{P}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula**. Substituting $P = V_s / (V_s + V_d)$:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{\left[(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) f\right] (V_s + V_d)}{V_s}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Ultimate BOD from BOD$_5$ via First-Order Kinetics

Given experimental $\text{BOD}_5$ and assumed rate constant $k$, derive $L_u$.

### Starting Equation

$$\text{BOD}_5 = L_u \left(1 - e^{-5k}\right)$$

### Step 1 — Isolate the exponential term

$$e^{-5k} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{L_u}$$

### Step 2 — Take natural logarithm

$$-5k = \ln \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{L_u}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $L_u$ by rearranging the original equation

$$1 - \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{L_u} = e^{-5k}$$

$$\dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{L_u} = 1 - e^{-5k}$$

$$L_u = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$$

**This is the back-calculation formula** used when the design engineer needs the *full* carbonaceous demand for stream impact studies.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem Statement (14 marks model):**

A sewage sample is diluted by taking $5\;\text{mL}$ of sample and diluting to $300\;\text{mL}$ with standard dilution water. The initial DO of the diluted sample is $D_1 = 8.9\;\text{mg/L}$. After 5 days of incubation at $20^\circ$C, the DO is $D_2 = 4.1\;\text{mg/L}$. The dilution water blank has $B_1 = 9.1\;\text{mg/L}$ and $B_2 = 8.9\;\text{mg/L}$. Compute the $\text{BOD}_5$ of the sewage.

### Step 1 — Calculate decimal volumetric fraction $P$

$$P = \dfrac{V_s}{V_s + V_d} = \dfrac{5}{5 + 295} = \dfrac{5}{300} = 0.01667$$

### Step 2 — Calculate depletion in sample bottle

$$D_1 - D_2 = 8.9 - 4.1 = 4.8\;\text{mg/L}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate depletion in blank

$$B_1 - B_2 = 9.1 - 8.9 = 0.2\;\text{mg/L}$$

### Step 4 — Apply unseeded formula ($f = 0$)

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) f}{P}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{4.8 - (0.2)(0)}{0.01667}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{4.8}{0.01667}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 288\;\text{mg/L}}$$

### Step 5 — Validity Check
- $D_2 = 4.1\;\text{mg/L} \ge 1\;\text{mg/L}$ ✓
- $D_1 - D_2 = 4.8\;\text{mg/L} \ge 2\;\text{mg/L}$ ✓
- Range $250 - 400\;\text{mg/L}$ is consistent with raw municipal sewage ✓

### Step 6 — Compute Ultimate BOD (Assume $k = 0.23\;\text{day}^{-1}$)

$$L_u = \dfrac{288}{1 - e^{-5(0.23)}} = \dfrac{288}{1 - e^{-1.15}} = \dfrac{288}{1 - 0.3166} = \dfrac{288}{0.6834}$$

$$L_u = 421.5\;\text{mg/L}$$

> **Result Interpretation:** The sewage has a 5-day BOD of 288 mg/L, but the *total* biodegradable load is ~422 mg/L. The carbonaceous demand will continue for another ~7–10 days after day 5.

---

## 3.4 Selection of Sample Volume (Analytical Matrix)

To obtain a depletion of $\ge 2\;\text{mg/L}$ with safety margin, the KTU-recommended sample volumes for various expected BOD ranges are:

| Expected BOD Range (mg/L) | Sample Volume for 300 mL BOD Bottle | Dilution Factor |
|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|-----------------|
| $0 - 6$ | $300\;\text{mL}$ (undiluted) | $1$ |
| $4 - 12$ | $150\;\text{mL}$ | $2$ |
| $10 - 30$ | $50\;\text{mL}$ | $6$ |
| $20 - 60$ | $25\;\text{mL}$ | $12$ |
| $40 - 120$ | $10\;\text{mL}$ | $30$ |
| $100 - 300$ | $5\;\text{mL}$ | $60$ |
| $200 - 600$ | $2\;\text{mL}$ | $150$ |
| $400 - 1200$ | $1\;\text{mL}$ | $300$ |
| $> 1000$ (industrial) | $0.1 - 0.5\;\text{mL}$ (multiple dilutions) | $600 - 3000$ |

**Rule of thumb:** $\text{Sample Volume (mL)} \approx \dfrac{300}{\text{Expected BOD}}$ adjusted to the nearest standard value above.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The BOD Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Wastewater Sample] --> B[Preliminary BOD Estimation]
    B --> C{Sample Type?}
    C -->|High microbe, no toxic| D[Unseeded Dilution Series]
    C -->|Low microbe or chlorinated| E[Seeded Dilution Series]
    C -->|Industrial, variable| F[Multiple Dilutions]

    D --> G[Add V_s mL to BOD Bottle]
    E --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H[Add Nutrient-Buffered Aerated Dilution Water]
    H --> I[Measure Initial DO: D sub 1 via Winkler / DO Meter]
    I --> J[Water Seal and Incubate at 20 deg C for 5 Days]

    J --> K[Measure Final DO: D sub 2 on Day 5]
    K --> L{Validity Check}
    L -->|Residual DO < 1 mg/L| M[Reject: Repeat with Higher Dilution]
    L -->|Depletion < 2 mg/L| N[Reject: Repeat with Lower Dilution]
    L -->|Valid| O[Apply BOD sub 5 Formula]

    O --> P[Compute BOD sub 5 in mg per L]
    P --> Q[Report to CPCB / Statutory Body]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: BOD Setup and Equipment Interconnections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAB [Environmental Engineering Laboratory Bench]
        direction TB
        subgraph PREP [Sample Preparation Unit]
            A1[Phosphate Buffer pH 7.2]
            A2[Magnesium Sulphate Solution]
            A3[Calcium Chloride Solution]
            A4[Ferric Chloride Solution]
            A5[Aerated Distilled Water 20 deg C]
            A1 --> A6[Nutrient Dilution Water Reservoir]
            A2 --> A6
            A3 --> A6
            A4 --> A6
            A5 --> A6
        end

        subgraph BODR [BOD Reactor Incubator]
            B1[Sealed BOD Bottle 300 mL]
            B2[Water Seal]
            B3[20 deg C +/- 1 deg C Thermostatic Bath]
            B1 --- B2
            B3 --- B1
        end

        subgraph DOM [Dissolved Oxygen Measurement]
            C1[DO Meter Membrane Electrode]
            C2[Winkler Titration Setup]
        end

        A6 --> B1
        B1 --> C1
        B1 --> C2
    end

    C1 --> R[Data Logger and Calculation Sheet]
    C2 --> R
    R --> S[BOD sub 5 Report in mg per L]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Two-Stage BOD Exertion vs. Time

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph KIN [First-Order BOD Kinetics Curve]
        direction TB
        T0[Day 0: Oxygen Demand = 0] --> T1[Days 0 to 5: Rapid Carbonaceous Exertion]
        T1 --> T2[Day 5: BOD sub 5 Reaches approx 68 percent of BOD sub u for k = 0.23]
        T2 --> T3[Days 5 to 8: Plateau, Lag Phase]
        T3 --> T4[Days 8 to 20: Nitrogenous Nitrification Stage]
        T4 --> T5[Day 20: BOD sub u = Total Ultimate Demand]
    end

    KIN --> NOTEE[Note: BOD sub 5 captures only carbonaceous peak, hence ATU added to suppress nitrification in lab]

    style T2 fill:#ffe5b4
    style T5 fill:#c1ffc1
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The yellow-highlighted Day 5 point represents the conventional BOD$_5$ reading, capturing ~68% of the carbonaceous ultimate BOD for $k = 0.23/\text{day}$. The green Day 20 point represents the *full* ultimate demand including nitrification. Lab tests are designed to stop the curve at Day 5 by ATU inhibition.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD). What is the significance of the subscript "5" and the superscript "20°C" in BOD$_5^{20^\circ\text{C}}$?
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
BOD is the amount of dissolved oxygen (in mg/L) consumed by aerobic microorganisms during the biological oxidation of organic matter in water at a specified temperature over a specified period. (1 mark)

The subscript **5** denotes the standard 5-day incubation period, chosen historically to approximate the maximum river flow time in Britain and ensure reproducible global comparison. (1 mark)

The superscript **20°C** denotes the standard incubation temperature, which fixes the metabolic rate of the bacteria — temperature higher than 20°C accelerates oxidation and inflates BOD, while lower temperature suppresses it. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. State the conditions under which a BOD test is considered valid.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The residual dissolved oxygen at the end of 5 days ($D_2$) must be $\ge 1\;\text{mg/L}$. (1 mark)
- The oxygen depletion ($D_1 - D_2$) must be $\ge 2\;\text{mg/L}$. (1 mark)
- The dilution water blank should not deplete more than $0.2\;\text{mg/L}$ in 5 days, indicating pure dilution water with no biological demand of its own. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Module Internal Choice Option 1)

**(a)** Describe, with a neat labelled flow diagram, the experimental procedure for the determination of 5-day BOD of a municipal sewage sample by the **dilution method**. State the role of each reagent used in the preparation of dilution water. **(7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module Experiment 2]**

#### Model Solution:

**Principle:** The sample is suitably diluted with oxygen-saturated, nutrient-buffered water, the initial DO is measured, the bottle is sealed and incubated for 5 days at 20°C, and the final DO is measured. The difference, corrected for blank and seed, gives BOD$_5$.

**Step 1 — Preparation of Dilution Water (2 marks):**
Aerated distilled water is fortified with the following reagents in 1 mL per litre doses of stock solutions:

| Reagent | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| **Phosphate buffer (KH$_2$PO$_4$ + K$_2$HPO$_4$)** | Maintains pH at 7.2; provides P-source for microbes |
| **MgSO$_4 \cdot 7H_2O$** | Source of Mg$^{2+}$, essential micronutrient cofactor |
| **CaCl$_2$** | Source of Ca$^{2+}$ for cell wall synthesis |
| **FeCl$_3 \cdot 6H_2O$** | Source of Fe$^{3+}$, electron transport chain cofactor |

The water is then aerated for 2 hours to bring DO to saturation ($9.17\;\text{mg/L}$) and stored at 20°C for 24 hours to stabilize.

**Step 2 — Selection of Dilution (1 mark):**
For raw municipal sewage (expected BOD $\sim 250 - 400\;\text{mg/L}$), dilute 5 mL of sample to 300 mL with dilution water ($P = 5/300 = 0.0167$). Two more dilutions are also prepared for validation.

**Step 3 — Initial DO Measurement (1 mark):**
$D_1$ is measured either by **Winkler's iodometric method** or using a **membrane-electrode DO meter** calibrated in air-saturated water.

**Step 4 — Sealing and Incubation (1 mark):**
BOD bottle is filled brim-full (no air bubbles), water-sealed, and placed in a thermostatic incubator at $20 \pm 1^\circ\text{C}$ for 5 days.

**Step 5 — Final DO Measurement and Calculation (2 marks):**
After 5 days, $D_2$ is measured. BOD$_5$ is computed using:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) f}{P}$$

**Flow Diagram:**
```
Sewage sample → Dilution estimation → Add V_s mL to BOD bottle
        → Make up with dilution water to 300 mL
        → Measure D_1 (initial DO) by Winkler / DO meter
        → Seal with water seal, incubate at 20°C × 5 days
        → Measure D_2 (final DO) on day 5
        → Compute BOD_5 = (D_1 - D_2 - correction) / P
        → Report in mg/L
```

---

**(b)** A 7.5 mL sewage sample is diluted to 250 mL with standard dilution water. The DO readings are: $D_1 = 8.4\;\text{mg/L}$ at day 0 and $D_2 = 3.6\;\text{mg/L}$ at day 5. The blank depletion $(B_1 - B_2) = 0.3\;\text{mg/L}$ and the seed correction factor $f = 1$. Calculate the 5-day BOD. Also determine the ultimate BOD assuming $k = 0.20/\text{day}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute decimal volumetric fraction P (1 mark)**

$$P = \frac{V_s}{V_s + V_d} = \frac{7.5}{7.5 + 242.5} = \frac{7.5}{250} = 0.03$$

**Step 2 — Sample depletion (1 mark)**

$$D_1 - D_2 = 8.4 - 3.6 = 4.8\;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — Blank depletion (already given) (1 mark)**

$$B_1 - B_2 = 0.3\;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Apply BOD$_5$ formula (2 marks)**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f}{P} = \frac{4.8 - 0.3 \times 1}{0.03}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{4.5}{0.03} = 150\;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 5 — Validity check (1 mark)**

$D_2 = 3.6\;\text{mg/L} \ge 1\;\text{mg/L}$ ✓; Depletion $= 4.8\;\text{mg/L} \ge 2\;\text{mg/L}$ ✓

**Step 6 — Ultimate BOD (1 mark)**

$$L_u = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5k}} = \frac{150}{1 - e^{-5(0.20)}} = \frac{150}{1 - e^{-1.0}} = \frac{150}{1 - 0.3679}$$

$$L_u = \frac{150}{0.6321} = 237.3\;\text{mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 150\;\text{mg/L}; \quad L_u = 237.3\;\text{mg/L}}$$

---

### Question B (Module Internal Choice Option 2)

**(a)** Explain the **first-order BOD kinetics**. Derive the relation $y_t = L_u(1 - e^{-kt})$ and comment on the effect of temperature on the deoxygenation constant $k$. **(7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 2]**

#### Model Solution:

**Statement of first-order kinetics (2 marks):**
The rate of oxygen utilization by microorganisms in BOD exertion is directly proportional to the amount of remaining unoxidized organic matter (measured as ultimate BOD) at any time $t$. The first-order differential equation is:

$$\frac{dy_t}{dt} = k(L_u - y_t)$$

where $y_t$ is the oxygen demand exerted up to time $t$, and $L_u$ is the ultimate (total) demand.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Rearranging:

$$dy_t = k(L_u - y_t) \, dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dy_t}{L_u - y_t} = k \, dt$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$ (with $y_0 = 0$ at $t = 0$):

$$\int_0^{y_t} \frac{dy}{L_u - y} = \int_0^t k \, dt$$

$$- \ln(L_u - y) \Big|_0^{y_t} = kt$$

$$- \ln(L_u - y_t) + \ln L_u = kt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{L_u}{L_u - y_t}\right) = kt$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{L_u}{L_u - y_t} = e^{kt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_u - y_t = L_u \, e^{-kt}$$

$$\boxed{y_t = L_u(1 - e^{-kt})}$$

**Effect of Temperature (2 marks):**
The deoxygenation rate constant $k$ increases with temperature following the **van't Hoff-Arrhenius relationship**:

$$k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T - 20)}$$

where $\theta \approx 1.056$ for carbonaceous BOD. Hence:
- At $T = 30^\circ\text{C}$: $k_{30} = k_{20} \times 1.056^{10} = 1.72 \, k_{20}$ — BOD is exerted much faster.
- At $T = 10^\circ\text{C}$: $k_{10} = k_{20} \times 1.056^{-10} = 0.58 \, k_{20}$ — BOD is exerted much slower.

This is why summer rivers recover oxygen demand more rapidly and why the standard 20°C is essential for comparison.

---

**(b)** A wastewater has an ultimate BOD $L_u = 350\;\text{mg/L}$ and rate constant $k = 0.25/\text{day}$. Calculate (i) the 5-day BOD at 20°C and (ii) the BOD remaining at the end of 7 days. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (i) — BOD at 5 days (3 marks)**

$$y_5 = L_u (1 - e^{-kt}) = 350 (1 - e^{-0.25 \times 5}) = 350 (1 - e^{-1.25})$$

$$e^{-1.25} = 0.2865$$

$$y_5 = 350 (1 - 0.2865) = 350 \times 0.7135$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 249.7\;\text{mg/L}}$$

**Part (ii) — BOD remaining at day 7 (3 marks)**

The BOD *exerted* at day 7:

$$y_7 = 350 (1 - e^{-0.25 \times 7}) = 350 (1 - e^{-1.75})$$

$$e^{-1.75} = 0.1738$$

$$y_7 = 350 \times 0.8262 = 289.2\;\text{mg/L}$$

The BOD *remaining* (i.e., still to be exerted) is:

$$L_7 = L_u - y_7 = 350 - 289.2$$

$$\boxed{L_7 = 60.8\;\text{mg/L}}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** By day 7, ~82.6% of the ultimate carbonaceous demand has been exerted, leaving only 60.8 mg/L of organic load unoxidized.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (read carefully!):**
>
> **Pitfall 1 — Wrong $P$ substitution:** Many students compute $P$ as the dilution factor ($V_d/V_s$) instead of the volumetric fraction ($V_s/(V_s + V_d)$). This inverts the answer by ~$\sim 3600\times$ — instant zero. Always remember: $P$ is the *fraction that is sample*, not the fraction that is water.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting the seed correction:** When $f$ is given, students often write $(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)$ and divide by $P$, missing the multiplication by $f$. The blank correction is *amplified* by the seed ratio.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Sign error in ultimate BOD:** Some students compute $1 - (1 - e^{-5k})$ instead of $1 - e^{-5k}$, yielding a negative exponent answer. Memorize: denominator is $(1 - e^{-5k})$.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Using $k$ in hr$^{-1}$ instead of day$^{-1}$:** The 5 in $e^{-5k}$ is in days. If the problem states $k = 0.25/\text{hr}$ instead of /day, the calculation is grossly wrong. Always check the units of $k$.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Reporting BOD without units:** BOD is always reported in **mg/L** (or ppm, numerically equivalent for dilute aqueous solutions). Reporting a unitless number costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **High-Density KTU BOD Cheat Sheet for Last-Minute Revision:**

- [x] **BOD$_5$ at 20°C** is the *single most important* statutory index of organic pollution in water.
- [x] **Standard reference:** IS 3025 (Part 44) : 1993 / APHA 5210 B.
- [x] **Master Formula:** $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) f}{P}$.
- [x] **Always state units as mg/L** (or ppm for dilute solutions).
- [x] **Validity criteria:** $D_2 \ge 1\;\text{mg/L}$ AND $(D_1 - D_2) \ge 2\;\text{mg/L}$.
- [x] **First-order kinetics:** $y_t = L_u(1 - e^{-kt})$ and $L_u = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$.
- [x] **Temperature correction:** $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$ with $\theta = 1.056$.
- [x] **Two-stage curve:** Carbonaceous (0–5 days) + Nitrogenous (8–20 days); ATU inhibits stage 2.
- [x] **Dilution water composition:** Phosphate buffer (pH 7.2) + MgSO$_4$ + CaCl$_2$ + FeCl$_3$, all aerated.
- [x] **Seeding required when:** (i) sample is chlorinated, (ii) industrial effluent with toxic compounds, (iii) very low BOD with insufficient native microbes.
- [x] **BOD of raw domestic sewage:** $200 - 400\;\text{mg/L}$; **CPCB discharge norm:** $\le 30\;\text{mg/L}$ for inland surface water.
- [x] **DO measurement methods:** Winkler's iodometric (primary) or membrane electrode (secondary, faster).
- [x] **Nitrification suppression:** Add **allyl thiourea (ATU)** to avoid inflated BOD$_5$ readings.
- [x] **Common dilution table:** BOD 200–300 → 5 mL sample; BOD 100–200 → 10 mL; BOD 50–100 → 25 mL — *memorize the standard table for viva.*
- [x] **Why the answer is in mg/L:** 1 L of water weighs ~1 kg; mg/L ≈ ppm for water.
- [x] **Difference between BOD and COD:** BOD measures *biodegradable* organics only (5 days, 20°C, slow); COD measures *total* oxidizable organics (2 hours, chemical oxidation, fast). **BOD $< $ COD** always.

> **Final Tip:** In KTU lab exams, the most commonly tested problem is the *direct BOD calculation* from given $D_1, D_2, B_1, B_2, f, V_s, V_d$. Practice at least 10 numericals before the exam. Memorize the **validity criteria** — they appear in every KTU answer key.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of C.O.D
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of C.O.D

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** is defined as the amount of **oxygen required to chemically oxidize the organic and inorganic matter present in a water or wastewater sample**, under controlled conditions, using a strong chemical oxidizing agent (standard: **Potassium Dichromate, K₂Cr₂O₇**, in 50% sulphuric acid medium), expressed in **milligrams of O₂ consumed per litre of sample (mg/L or ppm)**.

The test measures **both biodegradable and non-biodegradable** organic matter, providing a quantitative estimate of the total chemically oxidizable load. It is governed by the standard reference:

$$ \text{Standard Reference} : \text{IS 3025 (Part 58) : 2006 (Reaffirmed 2021) — APHA 5220 B} $$

The classical oxidation reaction governing the test is:

$$ \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 14\text{H}^+ + 6e^- \rightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

where the dichromate ion is reduced from **Cr⁶⁺ (orange)** to **Cr³⁺ (green)** as it oxidizes the organic matter in the sample.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Bonfire Test for Invisible Dirt":**
> Imagine you have a glass of muddy river water and a glass of clean water. You cannot *see* the dissolved organic pollution (sugars, alcohols, micro-organisms, proteins) — it is invisible. Now, picture a tiny industrial furnace inside a sealed test tube. You dump a powerful, super-hot chemical called **potassium dichromate** (think of it as industrial-strength "burning powder") into the water. Whatever can be chemically "burnt" (oxidized) will react, and the "burnt" oxygen is captured. The amount of oxygen consumed = the hidden chemical load of the water.
>
> - **BOD** = how much the *bacteria* can eat in 5 days (slow, biological, biodegradable only).
> - **COD** = how much a *super-aggressive chemical fire* can burn in 2 hours (fast, chemical, biodegradable + non-biodegradable).
> - Therefore, **COD is always ≥ BOD** for the same sample.

The difference `(COD − BOD)` gives the **non-biodegradable / slowly biodegradable organic load**, which is critical in designing advanced tertiary treatment plants.

## 1.3 Why COD is Measured — The Engineering Utility

| Parameter | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Treatment plant design** | Determines the **aeration tank capacity** and **organic loading rate (OLR)** in an activated sludge process. |
| **Effluent quality compliance** | CPCB / KSPCB discharge norms mandate **COD ≤ 250 mg/L** for discharge into inland surface waters (general standards). |
| **Industrial monitoring** | Tracks process efficiency in distilleries, tanneries, dye, pulp & paper, and pharma effluents. |
| **Toxicity indicator** | A sudden **drop** in COD may indicate the presence of toxic substances inhibiting microbial action (linked with BOD). |
| **Strength classification** | Low (<200), Medium (200–1000), High (1000–10,000), Very High (>10,000) mg/L. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Focus:** The examiner expects the student to know **what is being oxidized, by what, in what time, and why the test is preferred over BOD for rapid assessment.**

## 1.4 Limitations of the COD Test

- Does **not differentiate** between biodegradable and non-biodegradable organics.
- Does **not oxidize** certain straight-chain aliphatic compounds, aromatic hydrocarbons (e.g., benzene, toluene) and pyridine — these require modified methods (e.g., US EPA 410.4 with mercuric sulphate).
- Aromatic hydrocarbons and pyridine are **only partially oxidized**.
- Volatile organic compounds may be **lost by evaporation** during the reflux step.
- The test is **destructive** (the sample is consumed) and uses hazardous chemicals.

## 1.5 Standard Units, Constants & Reference Values

| Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| **Result Unit** | **mg/L (≡ ppm)** |
| **Equivalent weight of Oxygen (O₂)** | **8 g/eq** |
| **Oxidizing agent** | **0.25 N K₂Cr₂O₇ (≈ 1.2258 g/L)** |
| **Titrant** | **0.1 N FAS (Ferrous Ammonium Sulphate)** |
| **Reflux time** | **2 hours (open reflux) at ~150 °C** |
| **CPCB Discharge Norm — Inland Surface Water** | **COD ≤ 250 mg/L** |
| **BIS Drinking Water (IS 10500 : 2012)** | No specific COD prescribed; permanganate value ≤ 1 mg/L |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Chemistry of the Dichromate Reflux Method

The COD test is fundamentally a **redox titration** built on three chemical stages:

**Stage 1 — Oxidation of organics by dichromate in H₂SO₄ medium:**

$$ \text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{Cr}^{3+} $$

**Stage 2 — Back-titration of excess dichromate with Ferrous Ammonium Sulphate (FAS):**

$$ \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 6\text{Fe}^{2+} + 14\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 6\text{Fe}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Stage 3 — Visual indication using Ferroin indicator:**

$$ \text{Ferroin (orange-red)} \xrightarrow{\text{with excess Fe}^{2+}} \text{Red colour persists at endpoint} $$

The endpoint is the sharp change from **blue-green** (excess Cr³⁺ + Fe³⁺) to **reddish-brown** (Fe²⁺–ferroin complex), indicating all the excess dichromate has been reduced.

## 2.2 Role of Each Reagent — The "Why" Behind the "How"

| Reagent | Chemical Function | Engineering Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **K₂Cr₂O₇ (Potassium Dichromate)** | Primary oxidizing agent. Strong, stable, predictable. | Provides reproducible oxidation; equivalent of 1 mole = 1.5 moles O₂. |
| **H₂SO₄ (Concentrated Sulphuric Acid)** | Provides H⁺ ions + raises boiling point. | Catalyzes oxidation; reflux temperature ~150 °C achievable. |
| **Ag₂SO₄ (Silver Sulphate catalyst)** | Catalyzes oxidation of straight-chain aliphatic compounds & alcohols. | Without it, acetic acid, methanol etc. are not fully oxidized — a major cause of **under-estimation errors**. |
| **HgSO₄ (Mercuric Sulphate)** | Complexes chloride ions (Cl⁻) as [HgCl₄]²⁻. | Prevents Cl⁻ from being oxidized to Cl₂ and consuming dichromate spuriously. Critical for seawater & brackish samples. |
| **FAS (Ferrous Ammonium Sulphate, (NH₄)₂Fe(SO₄)₂·6H₂O)** | Reducing titrant for back-titration. | Standardized, stable; reduces remaining unreacted dichromate. |
| **Ferroin Indicator (1,10-phenanthroline ferrous complex)** | Redox indicator. | Sharp, easily visible colour change at endpoint. |

## 2.3 The Interference Equation — Chloride Correction

If chloride concentration in the sample is **> 2000 mg/L**, the following correction must be applied:

$$ \text{COD}_{\text{corrected}} = \text{COD}_{\text{observed}} - \left[ \text{Cl}^- \right] \times 0.226 $$

Alternatively, the HgSO₄ : Cl⁻ mass ratio must be at least **10 : 1** to suppress interference (i.e., for 1 mL of sample containing 1000 mg/L Cl⁻, 0.1 g HgSO₄ is required).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the chloride correction. If a sample has Cl⁻ = 5000 mg/L, ignoring it may inflate COD by **~1130 mg/L** — enough to fail any discharge compliance check.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$\text{COD (mg/L)} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$ | **Master formula** — open/closed reflux titrimetric method. |
| 2 | $$N_{\text{FAS}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7} \times 0.25}{V_{\text{FAS}}}$$ | Standardization of FAS normality. |
| 3 | $$\text{Normality of K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7 = 0.25 \text{ N (pre-calculated from } 1.2258 \text{ g/L)}$$ | Working strength of dichromate. |
| 4 | $$\text{O}_2 \text{ equivalent} = 8 \text{ g/eq}$$ | Conversion factor (molecular weight 32 / 4 electrons). |
| 5 | $$\text{COD}_{\text{corrected}} = \text{COD}_{\text{observed}} - [\text{Cl}^-] \times 0.226$$ | Chloride correction (when Cl⁻ > 2000 mg/L). |
| 6 | $$\text{HgSO}_4 \text{ required (g)} = 10 \times [\text{Cl}^-] \times V_{\text{sample}}$$ | Minimum mercuric sulphate to mask Cl⁻. |
| 7 | $$\text{COD : BOD ratio} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5 \text{ (typical domestic sewage)}$$ | Sanity check on experimental validity. |
| 8 | $$\text{Organic Load (kg/day)} = \text{COD (mg/L)} \times Q \text{ (ML/day)} \times 10^{-3}$$ | Used in design of biological treatment units. |
| 9 | $$\text{Dilution Factor (DF)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{original}}}{V_{\text{aliquot taken after dilution}}}$$ | Applied when COD is high. |
| 10 | $$\text{COD}_{\text{actual}} = \text{COD}_{\text{observed}} \times \text{DF}$$ | Accounts for sample dilution. |

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **8000** in Formula 1 arises from: $$ 8 \text{ (g/eq of O}_2) \times 1000 \text{ (mL/L)} = 8000 \text{ (mg·mL/L·eq)} $$

## 2.5 Comparison Table — BOD vs COD (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Feature | BOD | COD |
|---|---|---|
| Test duration | 5 days (BOD₅) | 2–3 hours |
| Oxidizing agent | Microorganisms | Potassium dichromate |
| Measures | Biodegradable organics only | Total organics (bio + non-bio) |
| Value | Lower | Always ≥ BOD |
| Equipment | BOD incubator, DO meter | Reflux apparatus, titration setup |
| Toxicity interference | High (microbes die) | Low (chemical) |
| IS Code | IS 3025 (Part 44) | IS 3025 (Part 58) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Experimental Procedure (Open Reflux Method — APHA 5220 B)

### Apparatus Required
1. **COD refluxing apparatus** — 250 mL or 500 mL round-bottom flask with ground-glass joints, water-cooled condenser, heating mantle (or gas burner).
2. **Burette** (50 mL, 0.1 mL graduations) with stand.
3. **Pipettes** — 10 mL, 20 mL, 50 mL.
4. **Volumetric flasks** — 100 mL, 250 mL.
5. **Analytical balance** (0.0001 g precision).
6. **Erlenmeyer flasks** (250 mL) for titration.

### Reagents (Prepare fresh monthly)

| Reagent | Composition / Strength |
|---|---|
| **Standard K₂Cr₂O₇ (0.25 N)** | 1.2258 g of A.R. grade dried K₂Cr₂O₇ dissolved in 1 L of distilled water. |
| **H₂SO₄ reagent** | Conc. H₂SO₄ + Ag₂SO₄ (5.5 g Ag₂SO₄ per kg H₂SO₄) — catalyst incorporated. |
| **FAS (0.10 N)** | 39.2 g of Fe(NH₄)₂(SO₄)₂·6H₂O + 20 mL conc. H₂SO₄ → 1 L with distilled water. |
| **Ferroin indicator** | 1.485 g 1,10-phenanthroline monohydrate + 0.695 g FeSO₄·7H₂O in 100 mL water. |
| **HgSO₄** (for Cl⁻ > 2000 mg/L) | Powder, A.R. grade. |

### Procedure — Numbered Sequential Steps

1. **Standardization of FAS:** Pipette 10 mL of 0.25 N K₂Cr₂O₇ into a 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask. Dilute to ~100 mL with distilled water. Add 30 mL conc. H₂SO₄. Cool to room temperature. Add 2–3 drops of ferroin indicator. Titrate against FAS till colour changes from blue-green to **reddish-brown**. Record the volume as $V_{\text{FAS, std}}$. Repeat for concordant readings.

2. **Sample preparation:** If the COD is expected to be high, dilute the sample appropriately. For domestic sewage, **no dilution** is usually needed. For industrial effluents, take 5–10 mL and dilute to 100 mL.

3. **Take the sample:** Pipette **20 mL** of the well-mixed sample into the refluxing flask.

4. **Add HgSO₄ (only if Cl⁻ > 2000 mg/L):** Add **0.4 g HgSO₄** (or as calculated) and swirl to dissolve.

5. **Add oxidizing agent:** Add **10 mL of 0.25 N K₂Cr₂O₇** solution.

6. **Add acid catalyst:** Carefully add **30 mL of H₂SO₄–Ag₂SO₄ reagent** down the side of the flask. Mix thoroughly. *(Always add acid to water — never the reverse!)*

7. **Reflux:** Connect the flask to the condenser. Reflux gently for **exactly 2 hours** on a heating mantle, ensuring the solution boils steadily. Condensate must not escape.

8. **Cool:** Allow the flask to cool to room temperature. Wash down the condenser with ~50 mL distilled water into the flask.

9. **Titrate:** Transfer contents to an Erlenmeyer flask. Add 2–3 drops of ferroin indicator. Titrate against **standardized FAS** until the colour changes sharply from **blue-green to wine-red (reddish-brown)**. Record the volume as $V_s$.

10. **Run a blank:** Repeat steps 3 to 9 with **20 mL distilled water** instead of sample. Record the titre value as $V_b$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Lab Tip:** The blank titre ($V_b$) should be approximately **10 mL** if FAS is exactly 0.1 N. A large deviation indicates either an error in FAS standardization or deterioration of K₂Cr₂O₇.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Master Formula

**Step A — Express the equivalents of dichromate consumed by the sample:**

$$ \text{Equivalents of K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7 \text{ (in sample)} = (V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{FAS}} \times 10^{-3} \text{ eq} $$

*Logical justification:* The difference in FAS volumes $(V_b - V_s)$ corresponds to the dichromate that was **NOT** used in oxidizing the organic matter — i.e., it was consumed by FAS during back-titration. Hence, $(V_b - V_s) \times N$ equals the equivalents of dichromate that *did* react with the organics in the sample.

**Step B — Stoichiometric relation between dichromate and oxygen:**

$$ 1 \text{ mole of Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} \equiv 1.5 \text{ moles of O}_2 \equiv 6 \text{ equivalents} $$

Therefore, for every **equivalent of dichromate** consumed, the mass of oxygen equivalent is:

$$ \text{Mass of O}_2 \text{ (g)} = \text{Equivalents} \times 8 \text{ g/eq} $$

*Logical justification:* The equivalent weight of O₂ is its molecular mass (32) divided by 4 (number of electrons), yielding **8 g/eq**.

**Step C — Convert to mg/L (final COD expression):**

$$ \text{COD (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{FAS}} \times 8 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample (mL)}}} $$

Combining $8 \times 1000 = 8000$:

$$ \boxed{\;\text{COD (mg/L)} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000}{V_{\text{sample}}}\;} $$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem Statement:** A 20 mL sample of domestic wastewater was refluxed with 10 mL of 0.25 N K₂Cr₂O₇. The excess dichromate required **8.5 mL of 0.10 N FAS** for back-titration. A blank titration (using 20 mL distilled water) required **10.2 mL of the same FAS**. Calculate the COD of the sample. If the sample contains 2500 mg/L of chloride, what is the corrected COD? Comment on whether the sample complies with CPCB discharge norms.

### Solution — Step-by-Step (Examiner-Ready Layout)

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.**

$$ V_b = 10.2 \text{ mL}, \quad V_s = 8.5 \text{ mL}, \quad N = 0.10 \text{ N}, \quad V_{\text{sample}} = 20 \text{ mL} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the master formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COD}_{\text{observed}} &= \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000}{V_{\text{sample}}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(10.2 - 8.5) \times 0.10 \times 8000}{20} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.7 \times 0.10 \times 8000}{20} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1360}{20} \\[6pt]
&= 68 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply chloride correction (since [Cl⁻] = 2500 mg/L > 2000 mg/L).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COD}_{\text{corrected}} &= \text{COD}_{\text{observed}} - [\text{Cl}^-] \times 0.226 \\[6pt]
&= 68 - (2500 \times 0.226) \times 10^{-3} \\[6pt]
&= 68 - 0.565 \\[6pt]
&= 67.435 \text{ mg/L} \;\approx\; 67.4 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Note:* The chloride correction in this case is small (< 1 mg/L) because the chloride concentration is only marginally above the threshold.

**Step 4 — Compliance check.**

$$ \text{COD}_{\text{corrected}} = 67.4 \text{ mg/L} \;\ll\; 250 \text{ mg/L (CPCB limit for inland surface water discharge)} $$

**Conclusion:** The sample **COMPLIES** with the CPCB discharge standard and represents a **low-strength** domestic wastewater (typical municipal sewage has COD ≈ 250–500 mg/L — this sample is unusually clean, possibly indicating upstream treatment or an industrial catchment with low organic load).

## 3.4 Second Worked Example — High-Strength Industrial Effluent

> **Problem:** A textile effluent sample of 10 mL (after 5× dilution, original was 50 mL) is refluxed. Blank titre = 10.0 mL, sample titre = 1.5 mL of 0.10 N FAS. Find: (a) COD of diluted sample, (b) COD of original effluent, (c) Compliance with discharge norms.

**Step 1 — COD of diluted sample.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COD}_{\text{diluted}} &= \dfrac{(10.0 - 1.5) \times 0.10 \times 8000}{10} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{8.5 \times 800}{10} = \dfrac{6800}{10} = 680 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — COD of original (undiluted) sample.**

$$ \text{Dilution Factor} = \frac{50}{10} = 5 $$

$$ \text{COD}_{\text{original}} = 680 \times 5 = 3400 \text{ mg/L} $$

**Step 3 — Compliance:** $3400 \text{ mg/L} \gg 250 \text{ mg/L}$ — **NON-COMPLIANT**. The textile effluent requires **primary + secondary + tertiary treatment** before discharge.

## 3.5 Analytical Sensitivity Matrix — Sources of Error

| Source of Error | Effect on COD | Magnitude (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Chloride interference (no HgSO₄) | **Falsely high** | Up to several thousand mg/L |
| Loss of volatile organics during reflux | **Falsely low** | 5–15% |
| Incomplete oxidation of aromatics | **Falsely low** | 10–30% for benzene, toluene |
| Improper acid addition (splattering) | **Falsely high (sample loss)** | Variable |
| Air oxidation of FAS during titration | **Falsely high** | 1–2% per hour exposure |
| Deteriorated ferroin indicator | End-point ambiguous | Erratic results |
| Improper reflux temperature | Inconsistent oxidation | Variable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — COD Test Process

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture of the entire COD determination workflow**, from sample intake to result reporting. It replaces physical apparatus drawings (which are unsuitable for Mermaid) with a process-engineering view that students can use as a rapid revision map.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Sample Reception] --> B[Preliminary Screening: Cl- and COD Range Estimation]
    B --> C{Cl- Concentration > 2000 mg/L?}
    C -- YES --> D[Add 0.4 g HgSO4 to Mask Chloride]
    C -- NO --> E[Proceed Without HgSO4]
    D --> F[Add 10 mL of 0.25 N K2Cr2O7]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Add 30 mL H2SO4 Ag2SO4 Reagent Slowly]
    G --> H[Connect to Reflux Condenser]
    H --> I[Reflux for Exactly 2 Hours at 150 C]
    I --> J[Cool to Room Temperature]
    J --> K[Wash Condenser with 50 mL Distilled Water]
    K --> L[Add 2-3 Drops Ferroin Indicator]
    L --> M[Titrate Against Standardized 0.1 N FAS]
    M --> N{End Point Reached: Blue-Green to Wine-Red?}
    N -- NO --> M
    N -- YES --> O[Record Sample Titre Value Vs]
    O --> P[Run Blank with 20 mL Distilled Water]
    P --> Q[Record Blank Titre Value Vb]
    Q --> R[Apply Master Formula]
    R --> S{Is Cl- Above Threshold?}
    S -- YES --> T[Apply Chloride Correction Subtract 0.226 times Cl-]
    S -- NO --> U[Final COD Value mg per L]
    T --> U
    U --> V[Compare with CPCB Discharge Norm 250 mg per L]
    V --> W[Report Compliance Status and Recommendation]
    W --> X[END: Result Documented]
```

## 4.2 Reaction Pathway Block Diagram — Chemistry Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sample Organic Matter CxHyOz] -->|Oxidized by| B[Cr2O7 2- in 50 percent H2SO4]
    B --> C[Products: CO2 + H2O + 2 Cr 3+]
    C --> D[Excess Cr2O7 2- Remains]
    D --> E[Back Titrated with Fe 2+ from FAS]
    E --> F[Cr2O7 2- Reduced to Cr 3+]
    F --> G[Fe 2+ Oxidized to Fe 3+]
    G --> H[Ferroin Indicator: Blue Green to Wine Red]
    H --> I[Equivalents of Dichromate Consumed Equals Organic Load]
    I --> J[COD mg per L Calculated]
```

## 4.3 Reagent Hierarchy Block Diagram — Why Each Reagent is Added

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Reflux Flask Contents] --> R1[20 mL Sample: Source of Organics]
    R --> R2[10 mL K2Cr2O7 0.25 N: The Oxidizing Fire]
    R --> R3[30 mL H2SO4 Ag2SO4: Acid Medium plus Catalyst]
    R --> R4[0.4 g HgSO4: Chloride Mask]
    R1 --> Q1[Provides Carbon Source]
    R2 --> Q2[Provides Cr6+ for Reduction]
    R3 --> Q3[Provides H+ and Boosts Reflux T]
    R4 --> Q4[Prevents Cl- Interference]
    Q1 --> OUT[Combined Oxidation Reaction]
    Q2 --> OUT
    Q3 --> OUT
    Q4 --> OUT
```

```
                    +----------------------------+
```
