List any four commonly used chemical coagulants in water treatment.** **Model Answer:** The **Optimum Coagulant Dose** is the minimum concentration of a coagulant (in mg/L) that produces the **lowest residual turbidity** (typically ≤ 1 NTU) in the treated water, while operating within the **optimum pH range**, at a **specified temperature**, and at a **defined mixing intensity (G-value)**. It is determined experimentally using the **Standard Jar Test (IS 3025 Part 60)**. **Four commonly used chemical coagulants:** 1. **Aluminium Sulphate** — Al₂(SO₄)₃·18H₂O (Alum) — most common, cost-effective. 2. **Ferric Chloride** — FeCl₃·6H₂O — effective over wide pH range. 3. **Ferric Sulphate** — Fe₂(SO₄)₃ — produces dense flocs. 4. **Poly Aluminium Chloride (PAC)** — pre-hydrolysed; requires lower dose. *(Other acceptable answers: Lime Ca(OH)₂, Aluminium Chlorohydrate)* ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Differentiate between coagulation and flocculation. Why is the jar test called a "scaled-down simulation" of a water treatment plant?** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Coagulation | Flocculation | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Charge neutralization of colloids | Particle collisions to form large flocs | | Mechanism | Physico-chemical | Physical (hydrodynamic) | | Mixing intensity | High (G = 300–1000 s⁻¹) | Low (G = 20–75 s⁻¹) | | Duration | 1–2 minutes (Rapid Mix) | 20–30 minutes (Slow Mix) | | Energy input | High | Low | | Chemical involved | Yes (coagulant added) | No chemical (just gentle stirring) | **Why Jar Test is a "scaled-down simulation":** The Jar Test apparatus replicates all three sequential processes of a full-scale water treatment plant: (1) The **rapid mix** simulates the **flash mixer** at the plant inlet, (2) The **slow mix** simulates the **flocculation basin** with paddle stirrers, and (3) The **quiescent settling** simulates the **sedimentation tank / clarifier**. By varying the coagulant dose across 6 jars, operators can determine the exact dose to be used at the plant without expensive full-scale trials. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the **mechanism of coagulation** with a neat sketch of the **four modes** of destabilization of colloids. Discuss the role of **pH** in coagulation using Alum as an example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A jar test was performed on a raw water sample with turbidity **150 NTU** and pH **7.6**. Six jars of 1 L each were dosed with 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 mg/L of alum (stock solution 10 g/L). The residual turbidities after 30 min settling were: 150, 80, 30, 6, 3, and 9 NTU respectively. **(i)** Calculate the volume of stock solution added per jar. **(ii)** Determine the **optimum coagulant dose** and **percentage removal efficiency**. **(iii)** Calculate the **alkalinity consumed** at the optimum dose. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Mechanism of Coagulation:** The destabilization of colloidal particles in water occurs through **four principal mechanisms**: 1. **Double-Layer Compression:** Increase in ionic strength (especially with indifferent electrolytes) compresses the diffuse double layer, allowing particles to approach within van der Waals attraction range. 2. **Charge Neutralization (Adsorption-Destabilization):** Specific adsorption of coagulant hydrolysis products (e.g., Al(OH)²⁺, polynuclear Al species) onto the negatively charged colloid surface, reducing the **Zeta Potential** to near zero (isoelectric point). 3. **Interparticle Bridging:** Long-chain polymer molecules (e.g., PAC, polyacrylamides) adsorb simultaneously on multiple particles, forming physical bridges. 4. **Sweep Coagulation (Enmeshment / Precipitation):** At high coagulant doses, a voluminous precipitate of Al(OH)₃ forms that physically sweeps up colloidal particles as it settles. **Role of pH (Alum example):** - Below pH 5.5: Alum exists predominantly as Al³⁺ and AlSO₄⁺ — effective for charge neutralization but poor for sweep floc. - pH 5.5–7.5: Alum hydrolysizes to Al(OH)₃ precipitate — **OPTIMUM ZONE for sweep coagulation**. - Above pH 8.0: Soluble aluminate ion (AlO₂⁻) forms — **re-dissolution** of precipitate, coagulation fails. - Each mole of Alum releases 6 H⁺ ions → pH drops → may need **lime addition** to maintain optimum pH. **Diagram (mermaid for sketch):** ```mermaid graph LR A[Colloid negative] --> B[Mode 1
Double Layer Compression] A --> C[Mode 2
Charge Neutralization] A --> D[Mode 3
Bridging] A --> E[Mode 4
Sweep Floc] B --> F[Destabilized] C --> F D --> F E --> F style A fill:#ffe1e1 style F fill:#00cc66 ``` **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Stock Volume Calculation** (using $V_{stock} = \dfrac{D \times V_{jar}}{C_{stock}}$ with $C_{stock} = 10{,}000$ mg/L, $V_{jar} = 1000$ mL): $$\begin{aligned} V_1 &= \dfrac{0 \times 1000}{10{,}000} = 0.00 \text{ mL} \\ V_2 &= \dfrac{5 \times 1000}{10{,}000} = 0.50 \text{ mL} \\ V_3 &= \dfrac{10 \times 1000}{10{,}000} = 1.00 \text{ mL} \\ V_4 &= \dfrac{15 \times 1000}{10{,}000} = 1.50 \text{ mL} \\ V_5 &= \dfrac{20 \times 1000}{10{,}000} = 2.00 \text{ mL} \\ V_6 &= \dfrac{25 \times 1000}{10{,}000} = 2.50 \text{ mL} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Optimum Dose and Removal Efficiency:** | Jar | Dose (mg/L) | $V_{stock}$ (mL) | $T_r$ (NTU) | Removal (%) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 0.00 | 150 | 0.0 | | 2 | 5 | 0.50 | 80 | 46.7 | | 3 | 10 | 1.00 | 30 | 80.0 | | 4 | 15 | 1.50 | 6 | 96.0 | | **5** | **20** | **2.00** | **3** | **98.0** ← Optimum | | 6 | 25 | 2.50 | 9 | 94.0 | **Optimum Coagulant Dose = 20 mg/L** (lowest $T_r = 3$ NTU) $$\eta_{opt} = \dfrac{150 - 3}{150} \times 100 = 98.0\%$$ **(iii) Alkalinity Consumed:** $$\text{Alkalinity consumed} = 20 \times \dfrac{600}{666} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{666} = 18.02 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]` **(a)** Describe the **standard jar test procedure** in detail with a labelled diagram. List the **significance** of rapid mix and slow mix phases. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A water treatment plant treats 50 MLD of raw water. The optimum alum dose from the jar test is **18 mg/L**. The commercial alum is 60% pure. Calculate: **(i)** The **daily alum consumption** in kg/day. **(ii)** The **cost per day** if alum costs ₹20 per kg of pure alum. **(iii)** The **sludge production** if 1 kg of commercial alum produces 0.4 kg of dry chemical sludge. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Standard Jar Test Procedure:** 1. **Sample Collection:** Collect a representative sample of raw water (at least 8–10 L for 6 jars + reserve) in a clean container. Note time and location of collection. 2. **Initial Characterization:** Measure **Turbidity (T₀)**, **pH**, **Temperature**, **Total Alkalinity**, and **Total Hardness** of the raw water. 3. **Distribution:** Pour **1000 mL** of raw water into each of the 6 square/circular jars of the Jar Test apparatus. 4. **Coagulant Dosing:** Prepare a **stock solution** (typically 10 g/L). Add calculated volumes to jars to achieve doses of (typically) 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 mg/L. 5. **Rapid Mix:** Stir all jars simultaneously at **100–120 rpm for 1–2 minutes**. This disperses the coagulant and initiates hydrolysis + charge neutralization. 6. **Slow Mix (Flocculation):** Reduce speed to **20–40 rpm for 20–30 minutes**. This promotes particle collisions and floc growth without breaking flocs. 7. **Settling:** Switch off the stirrers. Allow **quiescent settling for 30–60 minutes**. 8. **Sampling Supernatant:** Using a pipette, withdraw supernatant from **2–3 cm below the water surface** (avoid disturbing settled sludge). 9. **Final Measurement:** Measure **residual turbidity (T_r)** and **final pH** of each supernatant. 10. **Plotting:** Plot a graph of **Dose (X-axis) vs T_r (Y-axis)**. The dose giving lowest T_r = Optimum. **Significance of Rapid Mix:** - Disperses coagulant uniformly throughout the water. - Initiates hydrolysis of coagulant and charge neutralization. - High G-value (300–1000 s⁻¹) provides intense turbulence to overcome diffusion limitations. - Short duration (1–2 min) prevents hydrolysis products from re-dissolving. **Significance of Slow Mix:** - Allows destabilized (neutralized) micro-flocs to **collide and aggregate** into macro-flocs. - Gentle stirring prevents floc breakage; G-value of 20–75 s⁻¹. - 20–30 min duration gives sufficient collision frequency. - Larger flocs → higher settling velocity → cleaner supernatant. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Daily Alum Consumption:** Purity of commercial alum = 60% = 0.60 Pure alum dose required = 18 mg/L (optimum dose) For 50 MLD (Million Litres per Day) = $50 \times 10^6$ L/day $$\text{Pure alum needed} = 18 \, \dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times 50 \times 10^6 \, \text{L/day}$$ $$= 9 \times 10^8 \, \text{mg/day} = 900 \, \text{kg/day (pure alum)}$$ Adjusting for 60% purity: $$\text{Commercial alum} = \dfrac{900}{0.60} = 1500 \, \text{kg/day}$$ **(ii) Cost per Day:** $$\text{Cost} = 900 \, \text{kg/day (pure)} \times ₹20/\text{kg} = ₹18{,}000 \text{ per day}$$ **(iii) Sludge Production:** Sludge factor = 0.4 kg dry sludge per kg commercial alum $$\text{Daily sludge} = 1500 \text{ kg/day} \times 0.4 = 600 \text{ kg dry sludge/day}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Students often write 50 MLD as 50,000 L/day instead of 50,000,000 L/day. **1 MLD = 10⁶ L = 10³ m³**. Always verify. > 2. **Purity Factor Ignored:** Forgetting to divide by 0.60 (purity) gives 900 kg/day as commercial alum — losing **2 marks**. > 3. **Confusing pure and commercial alum in cost:** If the rate is "per kg pure alum", do NOT multiply by 1500 (commercial). Use 900. > 4. **Sign convention on graph:** Always label **X-axis = Coagulant Dose (mg/L)** and **Y-axis = Residual Turbidity (NTU)**. Reversed axes lose 1 mark. > 5. **Writing "Alum dose = 20 mg/L" without stating "Optimum":** In KTU, you must explicitly state "The **optimum** coagulant dose is 20 mg/L because it gives the **minimum residual turbidity of 4 NTU**." ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:** - ⏺ **Jar Test** = Standard laboratory method to determine **Optimum Coagulant Dose** (IS 3025 Part 60). - ⏺ **Apparatus has 6 jars** of 1 L each, with a **common variable-speed motor**. - ⏺ **Rapid Mix:** 100–120 rpm, 1–2 min, G ≈ 700–1000 s⁻¹. - ⏺ **Slow Mix:** 20–40 rpm, 20–30 min, G ≈ 20–75 s⁻¹. - ⏺ **Settling Time:** 30–60 min (quiescent). - ⏺ **Optimum pH for Alum:** 6.5–7.5; **FeCl₃:** 5.0–9.0. - ⏺ **Coagulant stock solution:** Typically 10 g/L = 10,000 mg/L. - ⏺ **Volume of stock to add:** $V_{stock} = \dfrac{D \times V_{jar}}{C_{stock}}$. - ⏺ **Alkalinity consumed per mg/L of Alum:** $\dfrac{600}{666} \approx 0.45$ mg/L as CaCO₃. - ⏺ **% Removal:** $\dfrac{T_0 - T_r}{T_0} \times 100$. - ⏺ **G-value (Camp-Stein):** $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu V}}$. - ⏺ **Four mechanisms:** Double-layer compression, Charge neutralization, Bridging, Sweep coagulation. - ⏺ **Zeta Potential** is the key parameter — coagulation optimum occurs when Zeta Potential ≈ 0. - ⏺ **Sweep floc** is the dominant mechanism in most plants; requires high alum dose. - ⏺ **Pin-point floc** = a problem at sub-optimum dose in low-alkalinity water. - ⏺ **Over-dosing** causes **charge reversal** and re-stabilization of colloids (right limb of curve). - ⏺ **Residual turbidity target:** ≤ 1 NTU (IS 10500 drinking water). - ⏺ **1 mg/L = 1 ppm** for dilute aqueous solutions. - ⏺ **Jar test replicates:** Flash mixer → Flocculation basin → Clarifier in sequence. - ⏺ **PAC** (Poly Aluminium Chloride) is **pre-hydrolysed** → less pH drop → preferred for low-alkalinity water. - ⏺ **Always state "Optimum dose = X mg/L"** with justification (lowest $T_r$) in viva/lab records. - ⏺ **Lab Record Essentials:** Graph (Dose vs $T_r$), Observation table, Stock concentration, Calculations for $V_{stock}$, % removal, and final pH must all be present. - ⏺ **Plot the curve** — KTU evaluators specifically check for the "U-shaped" graph with three zones marked. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark Part B questions on this topic, structure your answer as **(i) Theory/Mechanism + Diagram = 7 marks**, **(ii) Numerical with all steps shown = 7 marks**. Use **box-style tables** for calculations, draw a **neatly labelled graph sketch**, and **circle the optimum point** on the curve to earn full credit.

topics.title: Determination of Chlorine Demand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Chlorine Demand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chlorine Demand** is defined as the difference between the quantity of chlorine (in **mg/L** or **ppm**) added to a water sample and the quantity of free available chlorine remaining at the end of a specified contact period (conventionally **30 minutes** at a standard temperature of **20 °C** and pH maintained near **7.0**).

Mathematically, the relationship is expressed as:

$$\text{Chlorine Demand} = \text{Chlorine Dose Applied} - \text{Residual Chlorine after contact time}$$

This parameter is a **design-grade empirical measurement** used in the operation of water treatment plants to determine the optimal chlorine dose for disinfection. The principle is rooted in the fact that chlorine added to water is consumed not only by pathogenic microorganisms but also by **inorganic reducing agents** (such as Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, H₂S, NO₂⁻), **organic matter**, **ammonia**, and **amino compounds** present in the raw water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Combined Available Chlorine** refers to chlorine that has reacted with ammonia to form chloramines (mono-, di-, and trichloramine). **Free Available Chlorine** refers to chlorine existing as hypochlorous acid (HOCl) and hypochlorite ion (OCl⁻). Both together constitute the **Total Residual Chlorine (TRC)**, which is the parameter measured experimentally in this lab.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Thirsty Sponge" Model

Imagine you are watering a **parched sponge** for the first time. The sponge absorbs a huge amount of water before any drops fall out. Similarly, raw water containing organic matter, ammonia, and reducing agents acts like a "sponge" — it greedily consumes chlorine before any **residual chlorine** is left to fight pathogens. The total amount of chlorine the water absorbs before leaving a protective residual is precisely its **chlorine demand**.

**Why does this matter to a KTU engineer?**
- Under-dosing → germs survive (public health risk)
- Over-dosing → bad taste, formation of **trihalomethanes (THMs)**, which are carcinogenic
- Correct dosing → safe water that retains a **residual of 0.2 to 0.5 mg/L** at the consumer's tap

This balancing act is what the laboratory experiment teaches you to determine empirically.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Molecular weight of Chlorine (Cl₂) = 70.91 g/mol**
- **Equivalent weight of Chlorine = 35.45 g/eq**
- **Standard contact time for demand = 30 minutes** (BIS 10500 / APHA 4500-Cl)
- **Recommended free residual at consumer end ≥ 0.2 mg/L**
- **Standard temperature = 20 °C ± 2 °C**
- **pH range for test = 7.0 ± 0.2**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Chlorine in Water

When chlorine gas (Cl₂) or sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) dissolves in water, it undergoes **hydrolysis** to form **hypochlorous acid (HOCl)**, the actual disinfecting agent.

$$\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longleftrightarrow \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl}$$

Hypochlorous acid is a **weak acid** and partially dissociates depending on pH:

$$\text{HOCl} \longleftrightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^-$$

The relative distribution of HOCl and OCl⁻ is governed by the pH:

- At **pH < 7.5** → predominantly **HOCl** (more effective disinfectant, ~80× stronger)
- At **pH > 7.5** → predominantly **OCl⁻** (weaker disinfectant)

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the experiment is conducted at **pH 7.0 ± 0.2** — to keep the disinfection efficiency in a controlled, comparable state.

## 2.2 Break-Point Chlorination — The Heart of the Experiment

This is the **single most important theoretical concept** you must master for the KTU viva and written exams.

When chlorine is added in **progressively increasing doses** to water containing natural ammonia (NH₃), a characteristic **break-point curve** emerges. Plotting **Residual Chlorine (y-axis) vs. Chlorine Dose (x-axis)** gives a curve with **three distinct zones**:

### Zone 1 — Initial Chlorine Demand (Rising Limb, Steep)
- Chlorine reacts with **reducing agents**: Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, H₂S, NO₂⁻, and organic matter
- Residual chlorine is **zero** (or near zero)
- The slope of this segment reflects the **oxidation demand**

### Zone 2 — Chloramine Formation (Dome-shaped)
- Chlorine reacts with ammonia to form **monochloramine (NH₂Cl)**, then dichloramine (NHCl₂)
- Residual chlorine **rises** as combined chlorine accumulates
- Peak of the dome represents **maximum combined residual**

### Zone 3 — Destruction of Chloramines (Falling Limb)
- Continued chlorination **oxidizes** chloramines to nitrogen gas and nitrate
- Residual chlorine **decreases sharply** to a minimum (often near zero)

### Zone 4 — Break-Point & Free Residual (Post-Break-Point)
- Beyond the **break-point**, all ammonia and chloramines have been destroyed
- **Free available chlorine (HOCl + OCl⁻)** appears
- Residual chlorine **rises linearly** with further dose increase

The dose corresponding to the **minimum point of the curve (just before free residual appears)** is called the **Break-Point Chlorine Dose**, and the chlorine consumed up to this point is the **Chlorine Demand** of the water.

## 2.3 The Three Sequential Reactions (Memorize These!)

**Reaction 1 — Formation of Monochloramine:**

$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{HOCl} \longrightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Reaction 2 — Formation of Dichloramine (with excess chlorine):**

$$\text{NH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{HOCl} \longrightarrow \text{NHCl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Reaction 3 — Destruction of Chloramines at Break-Point (yields N₂ gas):**

$$2\text{NHCl}_2 + \text{HOCl} \longrightarrow \text{N}_2\uparrow + 3\text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At pH ~7, the **break-point dose is approximately 10 mg/L of chlorine per 1 mg/L of ammonia-N** present in the water. This empirical ratio is often asked in viva questions.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chlorine Demand | $C_d = C_{applied} - C_{residual}$ | mg/L | Operational dose selection |
| Free Available Chlorine | $FAC = \vert \text{HOCl} \vert + \vert \text{OCl}^- \vert$ | mg/L | Disinfection effectiveness |
| Combined Available Chlorine | $CAC = \text{NH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{NHCl}_2$ | mg/L | Intermediate chlorination level |
| Total Residual Chlorine | $TRC = FAC + CAC$ | mg/L | Field monitoring parameter |
| Iodometric Calculation | $Cl_2 = \dfrac{(V_1 N_1 - V_2 N_2) \times 35.45}{V_{sample}} \times 1000$ | mg/L | Residual Cl₂ by titration |
| Break-Point Dose (Empirical) | $D_{bp} \approx 10 \times \text{NH}_3\text{-N}$ | mg/L | Quick field estimate |
| CT Concept (Disinfection) | $\text{Inactivation} = f(C \times T)$ | mg·min/L | EPA / WHO guideline compliance |

> [!NOTE]
> **CT value** (Concentration × Time) is the **product of free chlorine residual (mg/L) and contact time (minutes)**. WHO requires a CT of at least 15 mg·min/L at pH 8 for a 99.9% inactivation of *E. coli*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Laboratory Procedure (KTU Standard)

The following stepwise procedure is what you will record in your **laboratory record**. KTU examiners award marks for the **sequence, accuracy, and rationale** behind each step.

**Step 1 — Sample Collection and Preparation**
- Collect the water sample (raw water, settled water, or filtered water) in a clean, sterilized glass bottle.
- Measure and record the **pH** and **temperature** of the sample. Adjust pH to 7.0 ± 0.2 using phosphate buffer if required.

**Step 2 — Preparation of Chlorine Stock Solution**
- Prepare a stock chlorine solution by diluting laboratory-grade sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) or chlorine water.
- Standardize the stock solution by the **iodometric method** (using 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃ and starch indicator) to determine its exact concentration in **mg/mL**.

**Step 3 — Setting Up the Demand Series**
- Take **6 to 8 clean, glass-stoppered conical flasks** (250 mL each).
- Add **200 mL** of the water sample to each flask.
- Add **progressively increasing doses** of stock chlorine solution — for example: **0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0 mg/L**.

**Step 4 — Contact Time**
- Allow the flasks to stand undisturbed for **30 minutes** at **20 °C** in diffused light.
- This simulates the **detention time in a contact tank** of a real water treatment plant.

**Step 5 — Determination of Residual Chlorine**
- After 30 minutes, analyze each flask for **residual chlorine** using one of the following standard methods:
  - **Iodometric titration (APHA 4500-Cl B)**
  - **DPD method (APHA 4500-Cl G)** — ferrous ammonium sulfate titration
  - **Starch-iodide method** — simpler, colorimetric
- The most common in KTU labs is the **DPD Ferrous Titration method**.

**Step 6 — Plotting the Break-Point Curve**
- Plot **Residual Chlorine (mg/L) on the y-axis** vs **Chlorine Dose (mg/L) on the x-axis**.
- Connect the points to obtain the characteristic **break-point chlorination curve**.
- The **break-point** is identified as the point where residual chlorine first rises sharply after a minimum.

**Step 7 — Calculation of Chlorine Demand**
- **Chlorine Demand = Chlorine Dose at break-point (since residual is ~0 at the break-point minimum).**
- Alternatively, for any given dose, use: $C_d = C_{applied} - C_{residual}$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Iodometric Calculation

The Iodometric method is the **primary titration method** in KTU laboratories. Let us derive the formula used to compute residual chlorine.

**Principle:** Free chlorine liberates iodine from potassium iodide in an acidic medium. The liberated iodine is titrated against a standard sodium thiosulfate solution using starch as the indicator.

**Chemical Equations:**

$$\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{KI} \longrightarrow 2\text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$$

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \longrightarrow 2\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

**Stoichiometric Logic:**
- 1 mole of **Cl₂** liberates 1 mole of **I₂**
- 1 mole of **I₂** reacts with 2 moles of **Na₂S₂O₃**
- Therefore, **1 mole Cl₂ ≡ 2 moles Na₂S₂O₃**
- Equivalent weight of Cl₂ = 70.91 / 2 = **35.45 g/eq**

**Formula Derivation (every step shown):**

Let $V_1$ mL be the volume of standard Na₂S₂O₃ used for the **sample**, and $V_2$ mL be the **blank** (distilled water) titration. The difference $(V_1 - V_2)$ represents thiosulfate consumed by chlorine released from the sample. The normality of thiosulfate is $N$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Milliequivalents of Cl}_2 \text{ in sample} &= (V_1 - V_2) \times N \\
\text{Equivalent weight of Cl}_2 &= 35.45 \text{ g/eq} = 35.45 \text{ mg/meq} \\
\text{Weight of Cl}_2 \text{ in sample (mg)} &= (V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 35.45 \\
\text{Concentration in mg/L} &= \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 35.45 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final boxed expression (the KTU favourite):**

$$\boxed{C_{Cl_2} = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 35.45 \times 1000}{V_{sample}} \;\; \text{mg/L}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A 200 mL water sample with 2.5 mg/L chlorine dose gave a residual. In iodometric titration, 200 mL sample consumed 4.8 mL of 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃. The blank consumed 0.2 mL. Find the residual chlorine.

**Step 1:** Volume difference
$$V_1 - V_2 = 4.8 - 0.2 = 4.6 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Cl_2} &= \dfrac{4.6 \times 0.025 \times 35.45 \times 1000}{200} \\
&= \dfrac{4.6 \times 0.025 \times 35.45 \times 1000}{200} \\
&= \dfrac{4076.75}{200} \\
&= 20.38 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This example value is unusually high because the dose and titration are arbitrarily chosen for illustration. In real KTU experiments, residuals typically range from **0.1 to 1.5 mg/L**. Always check your arithmetic and units **twice** before recording the result in your lab record.

**Step 3 — Chlorine Demand**
$$C_d = C_{applied} - C_{residual} = 2.5 - 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ mg/L (hypothetical realistic case)}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (DPD vs Iodometric Method)

| **Feature** | **DPD Method** | **Iodometric Method** |
|---|---|---|
| Specificity for free Cl₂ | Yes (distinguishes free & combined) | No (measures total residual) |
| Sensitivity | **0.01 mg/L** (high) | 0.1 mg/L (moderate) |
| Interferences | MnO₄⁻, oxidized Mn | Nitrite, Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺ |
| Reaction speed | Fast | Moderate |
| Equipment cost | Moderate (colorimeter) | Low (burette only) |
| KTU lab preference | High (preferred for accuracy) | High (preferred for simplicity) |
| APHA standard | 4500-Cl G | 4500-Cl B |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Break-Point Chlorination Curve — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **conceptual process logic** of chlorination and the appearance of residual species as the dose increases. This directly supports the KTU viva question *"Explain the break-point chlorination curve."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Raw Water Sample]) --> B[Analyze Ammonia N and pH]
    B --> C{Add Chlorine Dose D mg/L}
    C --> D1[Zone 1: Initial Oxidation Demand]
    D1 --> E1[Reactions with Fe2+ Mn2+ H2S NO2- organic matter]
    E1 --> F1[Residual Cl2 approx 0 mg/L]
    F1 --> G1{Dose Increasing?}
    G1 -->|Yes| D2[Zone 2: Chloramine Formation]
    D2 --> E2[HOCl + NH3 gives NH2Cl mono]
    D2 --> E3[HOCl + NH2Cl gives NHCl2 di]
    E2 --> F2[Combined Residual Cl2 RISES]
    E3 --> F2
    F2 --> G2{Dose Increasing?}
    G2 -->|Yes| D3[Zone 3: Chloramine Destruction]
    D3 --> E4[2NHCl2 + HOCl gives N2 gas + HCl]
    E4 --> F3[Combined Residual Cl2 FALLS to MINIMUM]
    F3 --> G3{Residual approx 0?}
    G3 -->|Yes| D4[ZONE 4: BREAK-POINT REACHED]
    D4 --> E5[Free Available Chlorine HOCl OCl- appears]
    E5 --> F4[Residual Cl2 RISES linearly with dose]
    F4 --> H([End: Operating Dose selected beyond break-point])
    G3 -->|No| D3
```

## 4.2 Laboratory Procedure Flow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1([Step 1: Collect Water Sample]) --> S2[Step 2: Measure pH and Temperature]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Standardize Chlorine Stock Solution]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Prepare 6 to 8 Flasks with 200 mL each]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Add graded chlorine doses 0.5 to 5.0 mg/L]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Wait 30 minutes contact at 20 C]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Titrate residual Cl2 in each flask]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Record Volume of Na2S2O3 used]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Calculate residual Cl2 using formula]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Plot Residual versus Dose curve]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Identify break-point from curve]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12: Compute Chlorine Demand]
    S12 --> E([End: Report Chlorine Demand in mg/L])
```

## 4.3 Free vs Combined Chlorine — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Residual Chlorine TRC] --> B[Free Available Chlorine FAC]
    A --> C[Combined Available Chlorine CAC]
    B --> B1[HOCl Hypochlorous Acid]
    B --> B2[OCl- Hypochlorite Ion]
    C --> C1[NH2Cl Monochloramine]
    C --> C2[NHCl2 Dichloramine]
    C --> C3[NCl3 Nitrogen Trichloride]
    B1 --> D[Active disinfectant at pH 7]
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> E[Weak disinfectant, persistent taste]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define chlorine demand. How is it different from chlorine dose?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Chlorine Dose (1 mark):** The quantity of chlorine (in mg/L) added to the water at the treatment plant to achieve disinfection.
> - **Chlorine Demand (2 marks):** It is the difference between the chlorine dose applied to the water and the residual chlorine remaining after a specified contact time (usually 30 minutes). It accounts for the chlorine consumed by organic matter, ammonia, and reducing agents present in the water.
> - **Key relation:** $C_d = C_{applied} - C_{residual}$

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is break-point chlorination? Why is it preferred in municipal water treatment?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (2 marks):** Break-point chlorination is the process of adding chlorine to water in a dose sufficient to oxidize all ammonia, chloramines, and reducing impurities, leaving a free available chlorine (HOCl + OCl⁻) residual.
> - **Reasons for preference (1 mark):** It ensures complete destruction of ammonia-nitrogen and provides a stable, measurable free chlorine residual that protects the water throughout the distribution system, ensuring safe water at the consumer's tap.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **break-point chlorination curve** with a neat sketch. Discuss the chemical reactions occurring at each zone. **(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Part (a) — 7 marks breakdown:**
>
> 1. **Definition of break-point chlorination (1 mark):** The point at which chlorine dose is just sufficient to destroy all combined chlorine (chloramines) and yield a free available chlorine residual.
>
> 2. **Sketch (2 marks):** A properly labelled curve with axes — x-axis: chlorine dose, y-axis: residual chlorine, showing the four zones (initial rise, dome of chloramines, dip to minimum, and post-break-point linear rise).
>
> 3. **Zone-wise chemical reactions (3 marks):**
>
>    - **Zone 1:** Chlorine reacts with reducing agents like Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, H₂S, and organic matter — residual Cl₂ is zero.
>    - **Zone 2:** $\text{NH}_3 + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (formation of mono/di chloramine — combined residual rises).
>    - **Zone 3:** $2\text{NHCl}_2 + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow + 3\text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (destruction of chloramines — combined residual falls).
>    - **Zone 4 (Beyond break-point):** Free chlorine residual (HOCl + OCl⁻) appears and rises linearly.
>
> 4. **Break-point identification (1 mark):** The dose at the lowest point of the dip, just before the linear rise of free residual begins, is the break-point dose.

**(b)** In a chlorine demand test, **200 mL** water samples were treated with varying chlorine doses. After a **30-minute** contact period, residual chlorine was determined iodometrically. The titration data is tabulated below. **(7 marks)**

| **Flask No.** | **Chlorine Dose (mg/L)** | **Na₂S₂O₃ Used (mL) of 0.025 N** | **Blank (mL)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 0.6 | 0.2 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 1.4 | 0.2 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 2.2 | 0.2 |
| 4 | 2.0 | 2.8 | 0.2 |
| 5 | 2.5 | 3.6 | 0.2 |
| 6 | 3.0 | 6.5 | 0.2 |
| 7 | 4.0 | 12.8 | 0.2 |
| 8 | 5.0 | 20.0 | 0.2 |

**Plot the break-point curve, identify the break-point, and compute the chlorine demand.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Calculate residual chlorine (C) using the formula (1 mark per row, but write the first one fully):**
> $$C = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 35.45 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$$
>
> For Flask 1:
> $$C_1 = \dfrac{(0.6 - 0.2) \times 0.025 \times 35.45 \times 1000}{200} = \dfrac{0.4 \times 0.025 \times 35450}{200} = 1.77 \text{ mg/L}$$
>
> Similarly, computing all rows:
>
> | **Flask** | **Dose (mg/L)** | **Residual (mg/L)** |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 0.5 | 1.77 |
> | 2 | 1.0 | 5.32 |
> | 3 | 1.5 | 8.86 |
> | 4 | 2.0 | 11.51 |
> | 5 | 2.5 | 15.05 |
> | 6 | 3.0 | 27.92 |
> | 7 | 4.0 | 55.83 |
> | 8 | 5.0 | 87.82 |
>
> **Step 2 — Plot the curve (1 mark):** Residual (y-axis) vs Dose (x-axis).
>
> **Step 3 — Identify the break-point (1 mark):** From the curve, the residual rises through Zone 2 (combined chlorine), then falls in Zone 3. The break-point dose is the minimum after which free chlorine appears. **For a clear break-point, plot only residual values; if combined and free are not distinguished, the minimum point near the dip is the break-point.**
>
> **Step 4 — State the chlorine demand (1 mark):**
> $$C_d = C_{applied} - C_{residual} \text{ at break-point dose}$$
> $$\boxed{C_d \approx 2.5 \text{ mg/L (based on interpretation of curve)}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **laboratory procedure** for the determination of chlorine demand of a water sample. List the apparatus and reagents required. **(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Apparatus required (1.5 marks):**
> - 6–8 glass-stoppered conical flasks (250 mL)
> - Burette and pipettes (10 mL, 200 mL)
> - Volumetric flasks
> - Iodometric titration setup
> - pH meter and thermometer
>
> **Reagents required (1.5 marks):**
> - Standardized chlorine stock solution
> - Potassium iodide (KI) crystals, glacial acetic acid
> - Standard sodium thiosulfate (0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃)
> - Starch indicator solution (1% soluble starch)
> - Buffer solution (pH 7)
>
> **Procedure (4 marks):**
> 1. Standardize the chlorine stock solution iodometrically.
> 2. Take 6–8 clean conical flasks; add 200 mL water sample to each.
> 3. Add graded doses of chlorine (0.5 to 5.0 mg/L) and note exact doses.
> 4. Maintain at 20 °C for 30 minutes contact time.
> 5. Add 1 g KI + 5 mL glacial acetic acid to each flask; titrate liberated I₂ with 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃ until pale yellow; add starch indicator; titrate until blue color disappears.
> 6. Record titration volumes and compute residual chlorine.
> 7. Plot residual vs dose; identify break-point; compute chlorine demand.

**(b)** A water treatment plant uses **chlorination for disinfection**. The raw water contains **0.4 mg/L of ammonia-nitrogen** and the pH is **7.2**. Estimate the **break-point chlorine dose** and the **chlorine demand**, and recommend the **operating dose** for a free residual of **0.5 mg/L**. **(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Given:** NH₃-N = 0.4 mg/L; pH = 7.2; required free residual = 0.5 mg/L
>
> **Step 1 — Break-point dose (2 marks):**
> The empirical rule at pH ~7:
> $$D_{bp} \approx 10 \times \text{NH}_3\text{-N} = 10 \times 0.4 = 4.0 \text{ mg/L}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Chlorine demand (2 marks):**
> At the break-point, residual chlorine ≈ 0, so:
> $$C_d \approx D_{bp} = 4.0 \text{ mg/L}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Operating dose (2 marks):**
> For a free residual of 0.5 mg/L at the consumer end:
> $$\text{Operating Dose} = C_d + C_{residual} = 4.0 + 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ mg/L}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Recommendation (1 mark):**
> Apply **4.5 mg/L** of chlorine at the plant inlet. Allow minimum **30 minutes** contact in a baffled contact tank. Verify residual of **≥ 0.2 mg/L** at the farthest point in the distribution network.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the Lab Record and Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the blank titration.** Many students report only the sample titration, ignoring the 0.2 mL blank. The blank value **must be subtracted** — examiners specifically look for this.
> 2. **Unit confusion.** Always use **mg/L** (or ppm, equivalent for dilute aqueous solutions). Writing "mL" or "g" in the result column is a guaranteed **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Wrong contact time.** The standard contact time is **30 minutes**, not 1 hour. Some texts allow 60 minutes for "long-term" demand — be consistent.
> 4. **No plot, no marks.** In Part B numerical questions, KTU examiners **require the curve to be drawn**. Even a rough sketch with axes labelled earns partial marks.
> 5. **Mixing up "residual" and "demand".** A common error: students state the dose itself as the demand. Always show the **arithmetic** $C_d = C_{dose} - C_{residual}$.
> 6. **Temperature and pH not recorded.** In the record, always report **pH = 7.0 ± 0.2** and **T = 20 °C ± 2 °C** at the time of test. Missing this entry is a 1-mark loss.
> 7. **Starch indicator overdose.** Adding starch too early in the iodometric titration causes a deep blue complex that is hard to discharge. Add it only when the solution turns **pale straw yellow**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Chlorine Demand** = Chlorine applied − Chlorine residual after 30 min contact at 20 °C and pH 7.0.
- **Break-point chlorination** is the cornerstone concept; the curve has **four zones** (initial demand → chloramine rise → chloramine destruction → free residual).
- The empirical break-point dose is **~10× the ammonia-N** concentration in mg/L.
- The iodometric formula is the **most-tested** equation in KTU exams:
  $$C_{Cl_2} = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 35.45 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$$
- **Free Available Chlorine (FAC) = HOCl + OCl⁻** — the actual disinfecting species.
- **Combined Available Chlorine (CAC) = NH₂Cl + NHCl₂ + NCl₃** — weak disinfectants, cause taste/odor.
- **Total Residual Chlorine (TRC) = FAC + CAC** — the operational monitoring parameter.
- Operating dose = Chlorine demand + Desired free residual (typically 0.2–0.5 mg/L at consumer end).
- Standard methods: **Iodometric (APHA 4500-Cl B)**, **DPD (APHA 4500-Cl G)**, **Starch-iodide**, **Amperometric titration**.
- Always **plot the curve** — even in written exams — to get full marks.
- Standard contact time: **30 minutes**; standard temperature: **20 °C**; standard pH: **7.0 ± 0.2**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Sulphate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Sulphate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Sulphate (SO₄²⁻):**
> Sulphate is a naturally occurring divalent anion (SO₄²⁻) formed by the oxidation of sulphites, thiosulphates, and elemental sulphur. In environmental engineering, sulphate is a major dissolved ion found in natural waters, industrial effluents (especially from mining, fertilizer, and pulp-paper industries), and domestic wastewater. Its determination is essential for assessing **potability, corrosivity, scaling tendency, and anaerobic digestion toxicity** in water and wastewater systems.

**The Gravimetric Determination of Sulphate (Barium Chloride Method)** is a standard analytical procedure in which sulphate ions in a water sample are precipitated as **Barium Sulphate (BaSO₄)** by the addition of Barium Chloride (BaCl₂) in an acidic medium. The precipitate is filtered, washed, dried (or ignited), and weighed. The mass of BaSO₄ is then stoichiometrically related back to the concentration of SO₄²⁻ in the original sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2:**
> Sulphate determination is performed using the **gravimetric method (turbidimetric/precipitation with BaCl₂)**, which is the prescribed standard method endorsed by *Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater (APHA, 23rd Edition, Method 4500-SO₄²⁻ E)*. The experiment is typically conducted for **drinking water, municipal wastewater, and industrial effluent samples**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Invisible Salt Detective":**
> Imagine a glass of clear water. To your eyes, the water looks perfectly clean. But hidden inside it are billions of tiny, invisible sulphate particles dissolved like sugar in tea. How do you "catch" them so you can weigh and count them? You throw in a chemical bait — **Barium Chloride**. Barium is a "magnet" for sulphate. The moment the two meet, they instantly lock together and form a heavy, white, sandy solid (Barium Sulphate) that sinks to the bottom. This solid is your "captured" sulphate. You simply filter the solid out, dry it, weigh it, and use chemistry math to figure out exactly how much sulphate was hiding in the water. **More white powder = more sulphate = more pollution**.

This is the essence of **gravimetric analysis** — converting a dissolved, invisible ion into a weighable, tangible solid.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numerical Reference Values (Bolded for Memory):**
> - **Molecular Weight of SO₄²⁻ (M_SO4) = 96.06 g/mol**
> - **Molecular Weight of BaSO₄ (M_BaSO4) = 233.40 g/mol**
> - **Gravimetric Factor (F) = M_SO4 / M_BaSO4 = 96.06 / 233.40 = 0.4116**
> - **Permissible Limit of Sulphate in Drinking Water (BIS IS 10500:2012) = 200 mg/L**
> - **Permissible Limit (in absence of alternate source) = 400 mg/L**
> - **Acceptable Limit (WHO Guidelines) = 250 mg/L**
> - **Standard Test Temperature = 25 ± 2 °C**
> - **Acidity Condition = pH maintained between 4.5 – 5.0 (using HCl)**

---

## 1.4 Significance of Sulphate in Environmental Engineering

| Domain | Role of Sulphate Monitoring |
|---|---|
| **Drinking Water** | High SO₄²⁻ (>250 mg/L) causes laxative effects and scales in pipes |
| **Boiler Feed Water** | CaSO₄ scaling reduces heat-transfer efficiency |
| **Concrete / Construction** | Sulphate attack degrades structural concrete via ettringite formation |
| **Wastewater (Anaerobic Digesters)** | >200 mg/L inhibits methanogenic bacteria |
| **Industrial Effluents** | TDS, salinity, and corrosion assessment |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Principle of the Gravimetric Method

The method relies on the **quantitative precipitation** of sulphate ions as Barium Sulphate (BaSO₄) under controlled conditions.

$$SO_4^{2-}_{(aq)} + BaCl_{2(aq)} \longrightarrow BaSO_{4(s)} \downarrow + 2Cl^-_{(aq)}$$

### Key Chemical Requirements for a Successful Gravimetric Determination:

1. **Insolubility of the Precipitate:** BaSO₄ has a solubility product (K_sp) of **1.1 × 10⁻¹⁰** at 25 °C, making it virtually insoluble in water.
2. **Stoichiometric Purity:** No other anions (carbonate, phosphate, chromate) should co-precipitate. Hence, the solution is **acidified with HCl (pH 4.5–5.0)** to suppress interference from CO₃²⁻ and PO₄³⁻.
3. **Filterable Particle Size:** Precipitation is done near boiling temperature in a **slightly acidic medium** to ensure the formation of large, easily filterable crystals (avoiding colloidal suspension that passes through the filter).
4. **Quantitative Recovery:** Excess BaCl₂ is added (slight excess of ~5–10%) to drive the reaction to completion via the **Common Ion Effect** (excess Ba²⁺ suppresses BaSO₄ solubility).

---

## 2.2 Reagents Required (Standard APHA 4500-SO₄²⁻ E Protocol)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Reagent List (KTU Board Exam Frequently Asked):**
> 1. **Barium Chloride (BaCl₂·2H₂O) crystals** — Analytical Reagent (AR) grade, 20–30 mesh size.
> 2. **Hydrochloric Acid (HCl), 1:1 dilution** — For sample acidification.
> 3. **Silver Nitrate (AgNO₃) test solution (0.1 M)** — For washing-water chloride test.
> 4. **Methyl Orange Indicator** — For pH monitoring during acidification.
> 5. **Distilled Water (Sulphate-free)** — For rinsing and dilution.

---

## 2.3 Apparatus Required

| S. No. | Apparatus | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Porcelain Crucible (Gooch / sintered glass / ashless filter paper)** | Collection and weighing of BaSO₄ precipitate |
| 2 | **Drying Oven (103–105 °C)** OR **Muffle Furnace (800 ± 25 °C)** | Drying / ignition of precipitate |
| 3 | **Analytical Balance (precision 0.0001 g)** | Weighing of empty and loaded crucible |
| 4 | **Hot Plate with Magnetic Stirrer** | Digestion of precipitate (aging) |
| 5 | **Vacuum Filtration Setup** (if using sintered glass) | Rapid filtration |
| 6 | **Filter Paper — Whatman No. 42 (ashless)** | Filtration medium |
| 7 | **Beakers, Pipettes, Measuring Cylinder, Glass Rod, Wash Bottle** | Standard labware |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE MASTER FORMULA (KGU / KTU Board "Most Repeated" Formula):**

$$\boxed{\,SO_4^{2-}\ (mg/L) \;=\; \frac{W \times 0.4116 \times 1000}{V}\,}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Mass of BaSO₄ precipitate (in grams)
- $0.4116$ = **Gravimetric Conversion Factor** (ratio of molecular weights: 96.06/233.40)
- $1000$ = Conversion from grams to milligrams
- $V$ = Volume of sample taken (in mL)

### Alternative Expression (also accepted by KTU examiners):

$$SO_4^{2-}\ (mg/L) \;=\; \frac{W \times 96.06 \times 1000}{233.40 \times V}$$

### Subsidiary Computations:

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Mass of BaSO₄ (W) | $W_2 - W_1$ (crucible+precipitate − empty crucible) | g |
| Gravimetric Factor (F) | $\frac{M_{SO_4}}{M_{BaSO_4}} = \frac{96.06}{233.40}$ | dimensionless |
| Concentration Factor | $\frac{1000}{V}$ | mL⁻¹ (or L⁻¹ when V in L) |
| Total SO₄²⁻ in sample | $C \times V_{total}$ | mg |
| Interference Check | Co-precipitation of SiO₂, Fe³⁺, PO₄³⁻ | qualitative |

---

## 2.5 Sources of Error & Interferences (Real-World Utility)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Frequently Tests These Interferences:**
> 1. **Co-precipitation of Silica (SiO₂):** Forms at low pH, addressed by acidifying to pH 4.5–5.0.
> 2. **Suspended Matter / Turbidity:** Removed by pre-filtration through 0.45 µm membrane.
> 3. **Organic Matter:** Destroyed by acid digestion (HNO₃–HClO₄) before precipitation.
> 4. **Fe³⁺, Al³⁺ interference:** Masked by boiling at low pH.
> 5. **Loss of precipitate during filtration / transfer:** Use ashless filter paper and careful washing.

### Engineering Utility of the Method:
- **Water Treatment Plant Design:** Determines the type of ion-exchange / lime-soda softening required.
- **Industrial Effluent Compliance:** Verifies discharge norms under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.
- **Soil & Groundwater Studies:** Sulphate leaching assessment in agricultural land.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Laboratory Procedure

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Procedure (APHA 4500-SO₄²⁻ E — Gravimetric Method with Drying of Residue)**

### Step 1: Sample Preparation
- Collect the water sample in a clean, pre-rinsed polyethylene/glass bottle.
- If the sample is turbid, filter through a 0.45 µm membrane filter.
- If the sample contains organic matter, digest with HNO₃ + HClO₄ to remove colour/organics.

### Step 2: Acidification
- Measure **250 mL** (or 100 mL for wastewater with high SO₄) of the clarified sample into a 500 mL beaker.
- Add **2–3 drops of methyl orange indicator**.
- Add **HCl (1:1) dropwise** until the solution just turns pink (pH ≈ 4.5–5.0).
- This step eliminates interference from CO₃²⁻, PO₄³⁻, and SiO₃²⁻.

### Step 3: Boiling
- Heat the acidified sample on a hot plate **just below boiling** (~95–98 °C) for a few minutes.
- **Why?** Hot conditions produce **larger BaSO₄ crystals** that are easier to filter and wash. Cold precipitation produces a fine, colloidal precipitate that clogs the filter.

### Step 4: Precipitation with Barium Chloride
- Add **~0.5 g of dry BaCl₂·2H₂O crystals** slowly to the hot solution **while stirring with a glass rod**.
- Stir gently for 1–2 minutes after the addition to ensure complete precipitation.
- The white, milky precipitate (BaSO₄) forms immediately.

$$Na_2SO_4 + BaCl_2 \longrightarrow BaSO_4 \downarrow\ (white) + 2NaCl$$

### Step 5: Digestion (Aging)
- Keep the beaker with the precipitate on a **hot plate near boiling** for **2 hours** (or digest in a steam bath).
- **Why?** Digestion **coarsens the precipitate particles**, reducing surface area and minimising co-precipitation of impurities. This is called **Ostwald Ripening**.

### Step 6: Filtration
- Filter the hot solution through a **pre-weighed sintered glass crucible (Gooch) or Whatman No. 42 ashless filter paper**.
- Use vacuum filtration for faster processing.
- Transfer all precipitate using a wash bottle and a rubber-tipped glass rod ("policeman").

### Step 7: Washing
- Wash the precipitate repeatedly with **hot distilled water** (3–5 times).
- Test the final washing for **chloride ions** using a few drops of **AgNO₃ solution**.
- **End point of washing:** When the filtrate shows no white AgCl turbidity (i.e., AgNO₃ test gives clear solution).
- **Why?** Removes adsorbed BaCl₂ and NaCl, which would otherwise add false mass to the precipitate.

### Step 8: Drying / Ignition
- Dry the crucible with precipitate in a **drying oven at 103–105 °C for 1 hour** (or ignite in a muffle furnace at **800 ± 25 °C for 30–60 minutes**).
- Cool in a **desiccator** to room temperature (15–20 minutes) before weighing.
- **Weigh to constant mass** (repeat drying/weighing cycle until two consecutive weighings differ by ≤ 0.0005 g).

### Step 9: Weighing
- Let the mass of the **empty crucible** = $W_1$ (g).
- Let the mass of the **crucible + BaSO₄ precipitate** = $W_2$ (g).
- Therefore, **Mass of BaSO₄ precipitate** = $W = W_2 - W_1$ (g).

### Step 10: Calculation

---

## 3.2 Complete Worked-Out Numerical Solution (KTU Standard)

> [!TIP]
> **Sample Problem — KTU Frequently Repeated Numerical:**

**Q: A 200 mL water sample was analysed for sulphate content. After gravimetric analysis, the mass of the empty crucible was 24.3826 g, and the mass of the crucible + BaSO₄ precipitate was 24.4612 g. Calculate the concentration of sulphate (as SO₄²⁻) in mg/L. Comment on its potability as per IS 10500:2012.**

### Given Data:
- Volume of sample, $V = 200$ mL
- Mass of empty crucible, $W_1 = 24.3826$ g
- Mass of crucible + BaSO₄, $W_2 = 24.4612$ g
- Molecular weight of SO₄²⁻ = 96.06 g/mol
- Molecular weight of BaSO₄ = 233.40 g/mol

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the mass of BaSO₄ precipitate**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= W_2 - W_1 \\
W &= 24.4612\ g - 24.3826\ g \\
W &= 0.0786\ g
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* The difference in mass gives the actual mass of pure BaSO₄ captured from the sample.

**Step 2: Calculate the gravimetric factor**

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{M_{SO_4}}{M_{BaSO_4}} \\
F &= \frac{96.06}{233.40} \\
F &= 0.4116
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* This factor represents the mass fraction of SO₄²⁻ in BaSO₄. One gram of BaSO₄ contains 0.4116 g of SO₄²⁻.

**Step 3: Apply the master KTU formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
SO_4^{2-}\ (mg/L) &= \frac{W \times 0.4116 \times 1000}{V} \\
SO_4^{2-}\ (mg/L) &= \frac{0.0786 \times 0.4116 \times 1000}{200} \\
SO_4^{2-}\ (mg/L) &= \frac{32.35}{200} \\
SO_4^{2-}\ (mg/L) &= 0.1617 \times 1000 / 200
\end{aligned}$$

Let me recompute carefully:

$$\begin{aligned}
0.0786 \times 0.4116 &= 0.03235 \\
0.03235 \times 1000 &= 32.35 \\
32.35 / 200 &= 0.16175\ g/L
\end{aligned}$$

Now convert **g/L to mg/L** by multiplying by 1000:

$$\begin{aligned}
0.16175\ g/L \times 1000 &= 161.75\ mg/L
\end{aligned}$$

Or in a single compact calculation:

$$\begin{aligned}
SO_4^{2-}\ (mg/L) &= \frac{0.0786 \times 0.4116 \times 1000 \times 1000}{200}
\end{aligned}$$

Wait, let me clarify the units cleanly:

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 0.0786\ g \\
W \times F &= 0.0786 \times 0.4116 = 0.03235\ g\ \text{of SO}_4 \\
\text{Concentration in g/mL} &= \frac{0.03235}{200} = 1.6175 \times 10^{-4}\ g/mL \\
\text{Convert to mg/L} &= 1.6175 \times 10^{-4}\ g/mL \times 10^6\ (mg/g) \times (1\ L / 1000\ mL)^{-1} \\
&= 1.6175 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 \times 1000 / 1 \\
&= 161.75\ mg/L
\end{aligned}$$

Simplified direct formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
SO_4^{2-}\ (mg/L) &= \frac{W \times 96.06 \times 1000}{233.40 \times V} \\
&= \frac{0.0786 \times 96.06 \times 1000}{233.40 \times 200} \\
&= \frac{7550.32}{46680} \\
&= 161.78\ mg/L \approx 162\ mg/L
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $SO_4^{2-} = 161.78$ mg/L $\approx$ **162 mg/L**

### Step 4: Potability Assessment (as per BIS IS 10500:2012)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Limits:**
> - Acceptable limit = 200 mg/L
> - Permissible limit (in absence of alternate source) = 400 mg/L
> - **Computed value = 162 mg/L < 200 mg/L** ✓

**Conclusion:** The water sample is **POTABLE** and within the **acceptable limit** as per IS 10500:2012. The sulphate concentration does not exceed the prescribed threshold, so the water is safe for drinking from a sulphate perspective.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Exam Scenarios

| Scenario | Expected Variation | Formula Variant |
|---|---|---|
| Sample size ≠ 250 mL | Re-substitute $V$ in master formula | $\frac{W \times 0.4116 \times 10^6}{V\ (mL)}$ |
| Reporting as BaSO₄ instead of SO₄²⁻ | Omit the factor 0.4116 | $\frac{W \times 1000 \times 1000}{V}$ |
| Reporting as SO₃ (sulphur trioxide equivalent) | Use factor = 80.06/233.40 = 0.3430 | $\frac{W \times 0.3430 \times 10^6}{V}$ |
| Reporting as S (elemental sulphur) | Use factor = 32.06/233.40 = 0.1374 | $\frac{W \times 0.1374 \times 10^6}{V}$ |
| Ignition in muffle furnace at 800 °C | Same calculation (BaSO₄ is stable up to 1500 °C) | No change |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Many students forget to multiply by **10⁶ (or 1000 × 1000)** when expressing in **mg/L**, mixing up grams and milligrams. Always double-check unit consistency:
> $W$ (g) × $10^6$ (mg/g conversion through mL → L) / $V$ (mL) = mg/L.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Water Sample] --> B{Sample Clear?}
    B -- No --> C[Filter Through 0.45 micron Membrane]
    C --> D[Measure 250 mL in Beaker]
    B -- Yes --> D
    D --> E[Add 2-3 drops Methyl Orange Indicator]
    E --> F[Add HCl 1:1 Dropwise]
    F --> G{pH 4.5 to 5.0?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Heat Just Below Boiling 95 to 98 C]
    H --> I[Add 0.5 g BaCl2 Crystals Slowly with Stirring]
    I --> J[White BaSO4 Precipitate Forms]
    J --> K[Digest on Hot Plate for 2 Hours]
    K --> L[Filter Through Pre-weighed Sintered Crucible]
    L --> M[Wash with Hot Distilled Water]
    M --> N[Test Washings with AgNO3]
    N --> O{Turbidity Observed?}
    O -- Yes --> M
    O -- No --> P[Dry at 105 C in Oven for 1 Hour]
    P --> Q[Cool in Desiccator 20 min]
    Q --> R[Weigh to Constant Mass W2]
    R --> S[Subtract Empty Crucible W1]
    S --> T[Calculate SO4 Concentration using Master Formula]
    T --> U[Report Result in mg/L]
    U --> V[End: Compare with IS 10500 Standard]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mass-to-Result Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"]
        A1[Water Sample Volume V mL] --> A2[BaCl2 Reagent]
    end

    subgraph REACTION["CHEMICAL REACTION BLOCK"]
        B1[SO4 in Sample] --> B2[BaCl2 Addition]
        B2 --> B3[BaSO4 White Precipitate Forms]
        B3 --> B4[Digestion and Aging]
    end

    subgraph SEPARATION["SEPARATION BLOCK"]
        C1[Vacuum Filtration] --> C2[Wash to Remove Cl-]
        C2 --> C3[Drying at 105 C]
        C3 --> C4[Weighing on Analytical Balance]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE["COMPUTATION BLOCK"]
        D1[W equals W2 minus W1] --> D2[Apply Gravimetric Factor 0.4116]
        D2 --> D3[Multiply by 1000 over V]
        D3 --> D4[Final SO4 in mg per L]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
        E1[Numerical Concentration] --> E2[Compare with IS 10500]
        E2 --> E3[Potability Verdict]
    end

    INPUT --> REACTION
    REACTION --> SEPARATION
    SEPARATION --> COMPUTE
    COMPUTE --> OUTPUT
```

## 4.3 Interference-Mitigation Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[BaSO4 Precipitate] --> I1{Interferences Present?}
    I1 --> CO3[Carbonate CO3 2 minus]
    I1 --> PO4[Phosphate PO4 3 minus]
    I1 --> SiO2[Silica SiO2]
    I1 --> ORG[Organic Matter]
    I1 --> FE[Iron Fe 3 plus]

    CO3 --> M1[Mitigation: Acidify with HCl pH 4.5]
    PO4 --> M1
    SiO2 --> M1
    ORG --> M2[Mitigation: Acid Digestion HNO3 HClO4]
    FE --> M3[Mitigation: Hot Acidic Conditions]

    M1 --> PURE[Pure BaSO4 Crystals Ready for Filtration]
    M2 --> PURE
    M3 --> PURE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define sulphate and state the standard method prescribed for its determination in water.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Sulphate (SO₄²⁻) is a divalent, naturally occurring anion commonly found in natural and industrial waters, arising from the oxidation of sulphide minerals, dissolution of gypsum, and industrial discharges.

The standard prescribed method for its determination is the **Gravimetric Method using Barium Chloride (BaCl₂)**, endorsed by APHA (Standard Methods, 23rd Edition, Method 4500-SO₄²⁻ E). In this method, sulphate ions are precipitated as Barium Sulphate (BaSO₄) in an acidic medium, filtered, dried, and weighed. The mass of BaSO₄ is stoichiometrically converted to the equivalent sulphate concentration using the factor **0.4116**.

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for method description)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Why is the sample acidified with HCl (pH 4.5–5.0) before precipitation with BaCl₂? List two consequences of skipping this step.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Acidification with HCl to maintain a pH between 4.5 and 5.0 is a **critical control step** in the gravimetric determination of sulphate for the following reasons:

1. **Eliminates interference from co-precipitating anions** such as carbonate (CO₃²⁻), phosphate (PO₄³⁻), and silicate (SiO₃²⁻), which would otherwise form insoluble barium salts and cause **positive errors** in the result.

2. **Promotes the formation of large, filterable BaSO₄ crystals** by suppressing colloidal dispersion. Acidic conditions ensure Ostwald ripening and prevent peptisation of the precipitate.

**Consequences of skipping acidification:**
- (a) **Positive error** due to co-precipitation of BaCO₃ / Ba₃(PO₄)₂ — falsely elevated sulphate reading.
- (b) **Loss of precipitate** through colloidal dispersion (colloidal BaSO₄ passes through filter paper), causing **negative error** in the result.

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 for rationale, 1.5 for consequences)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the principle and procedure for the gravimetric determination of sulphate in a water sample using the Barium Chloride method. Add a note on the chemical reaction involved and the role of acidification.

**(b)** [7 Marks] In a gravimetric analysis, 250 mL of a water sample yielded 0.0920 g of BaSO₄ precipitate. Calculate the concentration of sulphate in mg/L. State whether the water is potable as per IS 10500:2012. (Given: M_SO4 = 96.06 g/mol; M_BaSO4 = 233.40 g/mol)

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The method is based on the quantitative precipitation of sulphate ions as Barium Sulphate (BaSO₄) upon addition of Barium Chloride in an acidic medium. BaSO₄ is highly insoluble (K_sp = 1.1 × 10⁻¹⁰), ensuring complete recovery. The mass of BaSO₄ is gravimetrically measured and stoichiometrically related to SO₄²⁻ concentration.

**Chemical Reaction (1 mark):**

$$SO_4^{2-}_{(aq)} + BaCl_{2(aq)} \longrightarrow BaSO_{4(s)} \downarrow + 2Cl^-_{(aq)}$$

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Take 250 mL of clear, filtered sample in a 500 mL beaker.
2. Acidify with HCl (1:1) using methyl orange as indicator until pH = 4.5–5.0.
3. Heat to just below boiling (~95 °C).
4. Add ~0.5 g BaCl₂·2H₂O crystals slowly with stirring.
5. Digest on a hot plate for 2 hours (aging).
6. Filter through pre-weighed sintered-glass Gooch crucible or ashless Whatman No. 42 filter paper.
7. Wash precipitate with hot distilled water until washings give negative AgNO₃ test (no Cl⁻).
8. Dry in oven at 103–105 °C for 1 hour, cool in desiccator, weigh to constant mass.

**Role of Acidification (1 mark):**
Acidification (pH 4.5–5.0) prevents co-precipitation of interfering anions (CO₃²⁻, PO₄³⁻, SiO₃²⁻) and ensures formation of large, filterable BaSO₄ crystals, preventing colloidal losses.

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Volume of sample, $V = 250$ mL
- Mass of BaSO₄ precipitate, $W = 0.0920$ g
- $M_{SO_4} = 96.06$ g/mol
- $M_{BaSO_4} = 233.40$ g/mol

**Step 1: Gravimetric Factor (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{M_{SO_4}}{M_{BaSO_4}} \\
F &= \frac{96.06}{233.40} \\
F &= 0.4116
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply Master Formula (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SO_4^{2-}\ (mg/L) &= \frac{W \times F \times 10^6}{V} \\
&= \frac{0.0920 \times 0.4116 \times 10^6}{250} \\
&= \frac{37867.2}{250} \\
&= 151.47\ mg/L
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Potability Assessment (2 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **As per BIS IS 10500:2012:**
> - Acceptable limit of SO₄²⁻ = **200 mg/L**
> - Computed value = **151.47 mg/L < 200 mg/L**

**Conclusion:** The water sample **IS POTABLE** with respect to sulphate content. The concentration of 151.47 mg/L lies within the acceptable limit prescribed by IS 10500:2012.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] List the reagents and apparatus required for the gravimetric determination of sulphate. Describe in detail the precautions to be observed during the precipitation and filtration stages to obtain an accurate result.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A wastewater sample (300 mL) was analysed for sulphate. The empty crucible weighed 32.1456 g, and the crucible with BaSO₄ precipitate weighed 32.2201 g. Calculate the sulphate concentration in mg/L and assess its suitability for discharge into inland surface waters as per CPCB norms (sulphate limit = 1000 mg/L for industrial effluents).

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks Model Answer:**

**Reagents Required (2 marks):**
1. Barium Chloride (BaCl₂·2H₂O) — AR grade crystals
2. Hydrochloric Acid (HCl) — 1:1 dilution
3. Methyl Orange indicator
4. Silver Nitrate (AgNO₃) — 0.1 M (washing test)
5. Distilled water (sulphate-free)

**Apparatus Required (2 marks):**
1. Analytical balance (precision 0.0001 g)
2. Drying oven (103–105 °C) or muffle furnace
3. Sintered glass Gooch crucible / Whatman No. 42 ashless filter paper
4. Vacuum filtration setup
5. Hot plate, beakers (500 mL), pipettes, glass rod with rubber tip, desiccator

**Precautions during Precipitation and Filtration (3 marks):**

| Stage | Precaution | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Precipitation** | Heat solution to just below boiling | Produces large, filterable crystals |
| **Precipitation** | Add BaCl₂ slowly with constant stirring | Avoids localised supersaturation |
| **Precipitation** | Maintain pH 4.5–5.0 with HCl | Prevents co-precipitation of CO₃²⁻, PO₄³⁻ |
| **Digestion** | Age the precipitate for 2 hours | Ostwald ripening removes impurities |
| **Filtration** | Use ashless filter paper / pre-weighed crucible | Prevents mass error from filter decomposition |
| **Washing** | Wash until AgNO₃ test is negative (no Cl⁻) | Removes adsorbed BaCl₂ and NaCl |
| **Drying** | Cool in desiccator before weighing | Prevents moisture absorption error |

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Volume of sample, $V = 300$ mL
- Mass of empty crucible, $W_1 = 32.1456$ g
- Mass of crucible + BaSO₄, $W_2 = 32.2201$ g

**Step 1: Mass of BaSO₄ (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= W_2 - W_1 \\
W &= 32.2201 - 32.1456 \\
W &= 0.0745\ g
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply Master Formula (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SO_4^{2-}\ (mg/L) &= \frac{W \times M_{SO_4} \times 10^6}{M_{BaSO_4} \times V} \\
&= \frac{0.0745 \times 96.06 \times 10^6}{233.40 \times 300} \\
&= \frac{7156470}{70020} \\
&= 102.21\ mg/L
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Discharge Suitability Assessment (2 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **CPCB Norms for Industrial Effluents (Schedule VI, The Environment (Protection) Rules, 1986):**
> - Standard limit of SO₄²⁻ in industrial effluents for discharge into **inland surface waters** = **1000 mg/L**
> - Computed value = **102.21 mg/L ≪ 1000 mg/L**

**Conclusion:** The wastewater sample **COMPLIES with CPCB discharge norms**. The sulphate concentration (102.21 mg/L) is well within the prescribed limit of 1000 mg/L and is suitable for discharge into inland surface waters from a sulphate perspective.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ CRITICAL EXAM PITFALLS — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (Most Common — 2 mark loss):** Students write the answer in g/L or mg/mL instead of mg/L. **Always remember the 10⁶ multiplier** (g → mg = ×10⁶ when sample is in mL).
>
> 2. **Missing Gravimetric Factor (3-mark loss):** Reporting the result in terms of BaSO₄ mass instead of SO₄²⁻. Always convert using **0.4116** unless explicitly told otherwise.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Mention Acidification Role (1.5 mark loss):** Procedure questions test whether you understand *why* HCl is added. Just saying "to acidify" is not enough — mention **interference suppression** and **crystal size control**.
>
> 4. **Not Stating Constant Mass / Desiccator Use (1 mark loss):** A common valuation point — the precipitate must be **weighed to constant mass** after **cooling in a desiccator**.
>
> 5. **Potability Conclusion Without Standard Reference (1.5 mark loss):** Always cite the **specific limit value** (e.g., "as per BIS IS 10500:2012, acceptable limit = 200 mg/L") and compare the **computed numerical value** explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Determination of Sulphate**

- ✅ **Method:** Gravimetric Barium Chloride Method (APHA 4500-SO₄²⁻ E).
- ✅ **Principle:** Precipitation of SO₄²⁻ as insoluble BaSO₄ (K_sp = 1.1 × 10⁻¹⁰).
- ✅ **Reaction:** $SO_4^{2-} + BaCl_2 \to BaSO_4 \downarrow + 2Cl^-$.
- ✅ **Reagent Key:** Barium Chloride dihydrate (BaCl₂·2H₂O) crystals, AR grade.
- ✅ **Critical pH:** 4.5 – 5.0 (acidified with HCl, methyl orange indicator).
- ✅ **Precipitation Temperature:** Just below boiling (~95–98 °C) for crystal growth.
- ✅ **Digestion Time:** 2 hours on hot plate / steam bath.
- ✅ **Filter Medium:** Sintered Gooch crucible OR Whatman No. 42 ashless paper.
- ✅ **Wash Test:** AgNO₃ for Cl⁻ (negative = washing complete).
- ✅ **Drying:** 103–105 °C (oven) OR 800 °C (muffle furnace ignition).
- ✅ **Cooling:** Desiccator (15–20 min) before weighing.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $SO_4^{2-}\ (mg/L) = (W \times 0.4116 \times 10^6) / V$.
- ✅ **Gravimetric Factor:** $0.4116 = 96.06 / 233.40$.
- ✅ **Drinking Water Limit (IS 10500:2012):** Acceptable = **200 mg/L**; Permissible = **400 mg/L**.
- ✅ **WHO Limit:** **250 mg/L**.
- ✅ **CPCB Discharge Norm (Industrial):** **1000 mg/L** into inland surface waters.
- ✅ **Interferences:** CO₃²⁻, PO₄³⁻, SiO₃²⁻, Fe³⁺, organic matter, turbidity.
- ✅ **Mitigations:** Acidify, pre-filter, digest with HNO₃–HClO₄, hot precipitation.
- ✅ **Common Pitfalls:** Unit errors, missing factor 0.4116, vague potability statements.
- ✅ **Result Reporting:** Always state the value, units (mg/L), and reference standard.

---

**END OF NOTES — DETERMINATION OF SULPHATE (Module Experiment 2)**
