topics.title: Determination of Hardness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Hardness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardness of water** is defined as the **capacity of water to precipitate soap**, caused primarily by the presence of dissolved **divalent metallic cations**, predominantly **Calcium (Ca²⁺)** and **Magnesium (Mg²⁺)** ions, along with trace amounts of **Strontium (Sr²⁺)**, **Barium (Ba²⁺)**, and **Ferrous iron (Fe²⁺)**.

Hardness is conventionally expressed as the **equivalent concentration of Calcium Carbonate (CaCO₃)** in **milligrams per litre (mg/L)** or **parts per million (ppm)**. Mathematically, since 1 mg/L = 1 ppm for dilute aqueous solutions, both units are numerically identical for water analysis.

$$\text{Hardness (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \text{Multiplier} \times \text{EDTA titre value}$$

## 1.2 Classification of Hardness

| Type | Definition | Key Cations |
|------|------------|-------------|
| **Total Hardness (TH)** | Sum of all Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ (and other divalent cation) concentrations | Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ + others |
| **Calcium Hardness (CH)** | Hardness contributed solely by Ca²⁺ ions | Ca²⁺ only |
| **Magnesium Hardness (MH)** | Hardness attributable to Mg²⁺ ions | Mg²⁺ only (derived) |

### 1.2.1 Sub-Categorization (Carbonate vs. Non-Carbonate)

> [!NOTE]
> **Carbonate Hardness (Temporary Hardness):** Caused by bicarbonates of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ — removable by boiling.

$$\text{Ca(HCO}_3\text{)}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Carbonate Hardness (Permanent Hardness):** Caused by chlorides, sulphates, and nitrates of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ — *not* removed by boiling.

$$\text{NCH} = \text{TH} - \text{CH} \quad \text{(if TH > CH)}$$

## 1.3 The "Lather vs. Scum" Analogy

Imagine you are trying to **wash oily hands** with soap in a bucket. If the bucket contains **soft water**, the soap lathers freely and slides across your skin, dissolving the grime. If the bucket contains **hard water**, the soap forms a **curdy, greyish-white scum** that floats uselessly on top — your skin stays oily and you waste half a bar.

**Why?** The Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions act like "rogue magnets" that **steal the soap's cleaning agent** (sodium stearate) and convert it into insoluble calcium stearate — this useless precipitate is the "scum."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Rule of Thumb:** Hardness above **200 mg/L as CaCO₃** causes severe scaling in boilers, while water below **60 mg/L** is considered soft and ideal for textile and pharmaceutical processes.

## 1.4 Permissible Limits (IS 10500:2012 / WHO)

| Parameter | Acceptable Limit | Permissible Limit (No Alternation) |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Total Hardness | **200 mg/L** | 600 mg/L |
| Calcium Hardness | **75 mg/L** | 200 mg/L |
| Magnesium Hardness | **30 mg/L** | 100 mg/L |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of EDTA Complexometric Titration

The most reliable, standard, and KTU-prescribed method for hardness determination is the **EDTA (Ethylenediamine Tetraacetic Acid) Titrimetric Method** — codified under **IS 3025 (Part 21) : 2009 (Reaffirmed 2014)** and **APHA Standard Methods 23rd Edition, Method 2340 C**.

### 2.1.1 Why EDTA?

EDTA is a **hexadentate ligand** that can wrap around a single metal cation through **six donor sites** (two nitrogen atoms and four oxygen atoms from carboxyl groups), forming a remarkably stable **1:1 chelate complex** regardless of the cation's charge.

$$\text{M}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{Y}^{2-} \longrightarrow \text{[MY]}^{2-} + 2\text{H}^{+}$$

Where:
- **M²⁺** = Divalent metal ion (Ca²⁺ or Mg²⁺)
- **H₂Y²⁻** = Disodium EDTA dihydrate salt (the titrant in its ionized form)
- **[MY]²⁻** = Stable metal-EDTA chelate complex

### 2.1.2 The Log K Stability Constant

The thermodynamic stability of the Ca-EDTA complex is exceptionally high:

$$\log K_{\text{CaY}^{2-}} = 10.7$$

This high value means the reaction proceeds **quantitatively to completion**, which is essential for sharp, accurate titration end-points.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Reaction Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Sample Preparation:** A measured aliquot of the water sample is buffered to **pH 10.0 ± 0.1** using an **NH₄Cl–NH₄OH buffer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 2 — Indicator Addition:** A few drops of **Eriochrome Black T (EBT)** indicator are added. EBT itself is a weak ligand (a triphenylmethane dye) that initially forms a **wine-red complex** with Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺:
> $$\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{EBT} \longrightarrow \text{[Ca-EBT]}_{\text{wine red}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 3 — Titration with EDTA:** Standardized EDTA (0.01 M) is added dropwise. EDTA, being a **stronger ligand**, displaces EBT from the metal complex:
> $$\text{[Ca-EBT]}_{\text{wine red}} + \text{H}_2\text{Y}^{2-} \longrightarrow \text{[CaY]}^{2-} + \text{EBT}_{\text{free (blue)}}$$
> At the **end-point**, the **last trace of red disappears** and the solution turns **clear sky blue**.

## 2.3 Two-Stage Procedure: Separating Calcium from Total Hardness

| Stage | Indicator | pH | What is Measured | Interference |
|-------|-----------|-----|------------------|--------------|
| **Stage 1** | **Murexide (Ammonium Purpurate)** | pH > 12 (NaOH added) | **Calcium Hardness** only | Mg²⁺ precipitates as Mg(OH)₂ |
| **Stage 2** | **Eriochrome Black T** | pH 10 (NH₄Cl–NH₄OH buffer) | **Total Hardness** (Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺) | All Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ titrated |

**Magnesium Hardness** is **derived by subtraction**:
$$\text{MH} = \text{TH} - \text{CH}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\text{Hardness} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_s}$ | General hardness (N = normality of EDTA) | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 2 | $\text{Hardness} = \dfrac{V \times 1000}{V_s}$ | Simplified (when 1 mL of 0.01 N EDTA ≡ 1.0 mg CaCO₃) | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 3 | $\text{MH} = \text{TH} - \text{CH}$ | Magnesium Hardness | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 4 | $\text{NCH} = \text{TH} - \text{CH}_{\text{carbonate}}$ | Non-Carbonate Hardness | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 5 | $\text{N}_{\text{EDTA}} = \dfrac{W}{V \times 53}$ | Standardization of EDTA against CaCO₃ (W = mg CaCO₃; V = mL EDTA; 53 = eq. wt. of CaCO₃ in N scale) | N (Normal) |
| 6 | $1 \text{ mL of 0.01 M EDTA} \equiv 1.0 \text{ mg CaCO}_3$ | Fundamental equivalence | — |

### 2.4.1 Why the Magic Number "50,000"?

The constant 50,000 in the master formula is derived as:

$$\text{Equivalent weight of CaCO}_3 = \frac{\text{Molecular weight}}{2} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ g/eq}$$

Then:
$$1 \text{ mL of 1 N EDTA} = 50 \text{ mg CaCO}_3$$

Therefore:
$$1 \text{ mL of 0.01 N EDTA} = 50{,}000 \times 0.01 = 0.5 \text{ mg CaCO}_3$$

Multiplying by 1000 (for mL → L) and 1000 (for mg → g) collapses to the constant **50,000** when normalizing per litre.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler Feed Water:** Hardness must be < 1 mg/L to prevent *limescale* formation, which causes tube ruptures.
- **Cooling Towers:** Hardness causes biofouling and reduced heat-transfer efficiency.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Hardness interferes with drug formulation and pH control.
- **Textile Dyeing:** Ca²⁺ ions precipitate dyes, ruining fabric coloration.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Stoichiometry to the Master Hardness Formula

We will derive the fundamental equation used in the KTU lab report from **first principles** of volumetric stoichiometry.

### 3.1.1 Given Data

- Let $V_s$ = Volume of water sample taken for titration (in mL)
- Let $V_e$ = Volume of standard EDTA solution used (in mL)
- Let $N_e$ = Normality of EDTA solution (in eq/L)
- Hardness is to be reported in **mg/L as CaCO₃**

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the Equivalence Principle**

At the end-point of a complexometric titration, the milliequivalents of EDTA delivered exactly equal the milliequivalents of Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ (expressed as CaCO₃) present in the sample aliquot.

$$\text{meq of EDTA} = \text{meq of hardness (as CaCO}_3\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Express Milliequivalents of EDTA**

By definition, milliequivalents = Normality × Volume (in mL):

$$\text{meq of EDTA} = N_e \times V_e$$

**Step 3 — Express Milliequivalents of Hardness**

If $W$ represents the mass of CaCO₃ equivalent in the sample (in mg), then:

$$\text{meq of hardness} = \frac{W}{\text{Equivalent weight of CaCO}_3} = \frac{W}{50}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the Two Sides**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_e \times V_e &= \frac{W}{50} \\
W &= 50 \times N_e \times V_e \quad \text{(in mg)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize to "per Litre" of the Original Sample**

To express the hardness as mg per litre of the water sample, divide the mass $W$ by the sample volume $V_s$ (in mL) and multiply by 1000 (to convert mL → L):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardness (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)} &= \frac{W}{V_s} \times 1000 \\
&= \frac{50 \times N_e \times V_e}{V_s} \times 1000 \\
&= \frac{V_e \times N_e \times 50{,}000}{V_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is the MASTER FORMULA** required for all KTU hardness calculations.

### 3.1.3 Simplified Special Case

For routine lab work, KTU students use a **0.01 N EDTA solution** where 1 mL of EDTA ≡ 1.0 mg of CaCO₃ (because 50,000 × 0.01 = 500; per 1000 mL = 0.5; per single mL of sample = 1.0 mg CaCO₃ when $V_s$ = 50 mL).

A more practical working equation often adopted is:

$$\boxed{\text{Total Hardness (mg/L)} = \frac{(\text{EDTA titre in mL}) \times 1000}{\text{Volume of sample in mL}}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Style)

> **[Sample Question Pattern]**
> 50 mL of a water sample requires 18.4 mL of 0.01 N EDTA solution for titration using EBT indicator. A separate 50 mL aliquot, titrated with 0.01 N EDTA using murexide indicator at pH 12, consumes 12.2 mL. Calculate **Total Hardness**, **Calcium Hardness**, and **Magnesium Hardness** in mg/L as CaCO₃.

### 3.2.1 Solution

**Given:**
- Sample volume $V_s$ = 50 mL
- EDTA Normality $N_e$ = 0.01 N
- EDTA volume for **Total Hardness** (with EBT) = 18.4 mL
- EDTA volume for **Calcium Hardness** (with murexide) = 12.2 mL

**Step 1 — Total Hardness (TH)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TH} &= \frac{V_e \times N_e \times 50{,}000}{V_s} \\
&= \frac{18.4 \times 0.01 \times 50{,}000}{50} \\
&= \frac{9200}{50} \\
&= 184 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calcium Hardness (CH)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH} &= \frac{12.2 \times 0.01 \times 50{,}000}{50} \\
&= \frac{6100}{50} \\
&= 122 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Magnesium Hardness (MH)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MH} &= \text{TH} - \text{CH} \\
&= 184 - 122 \\
&= 62 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Final Result Table

| Parameter | Result | Inference |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| Total Hardness | **184 mg/L** | Within permissible limit (IS 10500) |
| Calcium Hardness | **122 mg/L** | Slightly elevated; above acceptable 75 mg/L |
| Magnesium Hardness | **62 mg/L** | Above acceptable 30 mg/L — Mg-rich source |

## 3.3 EDTA Standardization Derivation

EDTA is a **secondary standard** — it must be standardized against a **primary standard** of **anhydrous CaCO₃ (A.R. grade)** before use.

**Procedure Summary:**
1. Weigh exactly 1.0 g of A.R. CaCO₃ → dissolve in minimum 1:1 HCl → dilute to 1 L → this gives **1000 mg/L as CaCO₃** stock.
2. Pipette 50 mL of this stock → dilute to 1000 mL → this gives **50 mg/L CaCO₃** working standard.
3. Titrate 50 mL of working standard with prepared EDTA using EBT indicator.

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{EDTA}} &= \frac{\text{mass of CaCO}_3 \text{ in aliquot (mg)}}{\text{Volume of EDTA} \times 50} \\
&= \frac{50 \text{ mg}}{V_e \times 50} \\
&= \frac{1}{V_e} \text{ (where } V_e \text{ is in mL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For example, if 50 mL of 50 mg/L CaCO₃ requires 50 mL of EDTA, then $N_{\text{EDTA}}$ = 0.01 N exactly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Titration Workflow — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Water Sample Collection"] --> nodeB["Measure 50 mL Aliquot in Conical Flask"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Add 1 to 2 mL NH4Cl-NH4OH Buffer pH 10"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Add 2 to 3 Drops Eriochrome Black T Indicator"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Solution Turns Wine Red Color"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Titrate with 0.01 N EDTA from Burette"]
    nodeF --> nodeG{"End Point Reached?"}
    nodeG -- No --> nodeF
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH["Color Changes to Clear Sky Blue"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Record Titre Volume V1 mL"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Repeat for Concordant Reading"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Calculate Total Hardness using Master Formula"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["END: Report TH in mg/L as CaCO3"]

    nodeM["PARALLEL: Second 50 mL Aliquot"] --> nodeN["Add 1 to 2 mL NaOH Solution pH above 12"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Add Murexide Indicator Powder"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["Solution Turns Pink Orange Color"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["Titrate with 0.01 N EDTA"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR{"End Point Reached?"}
    nodeR -- No --> nodeQ
    nodeR -- Yes --> nodeS["Color Changes to Purple Violet"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["Record Titre Volume V2 mL"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["Calculate Calcium Hardness"]
    nodeU --> nodeV["Derive Magnesium Hardness equals TH minus CH"]
    nodeV --> nodeW["END: Report CH and MH in mg/L as CaCO3"]
```

## 4.2 Chemical Reaction Schematic — Visualizing the Chelation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ion1["Ca 2+ or Mg 2+ in Sample"] --> complex1["Wine Red Metal EBT Complex"]
    complex1 --> step1["Add EDTA Drop by Drop"]
    step1 --> step2["EDTA Displaces EBT and Binds the Metal"]
    step2 --> complex2["Stable Ca EDTA or Mg EDTA Chelate"]
    step2 --> free1["Free EBT Indicator Blue Color"]
    free1 --> final["Final Solution: Clear Sky Blue"]
    complex2 --> final
```

## 4.3 Reagent & Apparatus Inventory Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph reagents["REAGENTS REQUIRED"]
        r1["0.01 M EDTA Solution"]
        r2["NH4Cl NH4OH Buffer pH 10"]
        r3["NaOH Solution 1 N for Calcium Stage"]
        r4["Eriochrome Black T Indicator Powder or Solution"]
        r5["Murexide Indicator Powder"]
    end

    subgraph apparatus["GLASSWARE AND APPARATUS"]
        a1["50 mL Burette with Stand"]
        a2["250 mL Conical Flasks x 2"]
        a3["50 mL Pipette"]
        a4["Measuring Cylinder 10 mL"]
        a5["White Tile for Background"]
    end

    subgraph samples["SAMPLE HANDLING"]
        s1["Tap Water or Borewell Water Sample"]
        s2["Distilled Water for Dilution and Rinsing"]
    end

    reagents --> process["Analytical Workbench: EDTA Titration Procedure"]
    apparatus --> process
    samples --> process
    process --> output["Result: TH CH MH in mg/L as CaCO3"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Indicator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["What do you want to measure?"] --> q1{"Hardness Component"}
    q1 -- Total Hardness --> path1["Use EBT Indicator and pH 10 Buffer"]
    q1 -- Calcium Hardness Only --> path2["Use Murexide Indicator and pH above 12 NaOH"]
    path1 --> formula1["TH equals V EDTA x N x 50000 over V sample"]
    path2 --> formula2["CH equals V EDTA x N x 50000 over V sample"]
    formula1 --> derive["MH equals TH minus CH"]
    formula2 --> derive
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Hardness. Mention the different types of hardness. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Hardness of water is defined as the **capacity of water to precipitate soap**, due to the presence of dissolved **divalent metallic cations** like Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Sr²⁺, Ba²⁺, and Fe²⁺. It is expressed in **mg/L as CaCO₃**.

**Types of Hardness:**

1. **Total Hardness (TH)** — Sum of calcium and magnesium hardness.
2. **Calcium Hardness (CH)** — Hardness due to Ca²⁺ ions only.
3. **Magnesium Hardness (MH)** — Hardness due to Mg²⁺ ions only.
4. **Carbonate Hardness / Temporary Hardness** — Caused by bicarbonates; removable by boiling.
5. **Non-Carbonate Hardness / Permanent Hardness** — Caused by chlorides, sulphates, and nitrates; not removable by boiling.

---

### Question 2: What is the principle of EDTA titration? Name the indicators used. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** EDTA (Ethylenediamine Tetraacetic Acid) is a hexadentate ligand that forms stable, water-soluble **1:1 chelate complexes** with Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions. The reaction is quantitative and stoichiometric, allowing precise titrimetric determination of hardness at a controlled pH.

**Indicators Used:**

| Stage | Indicator | pH Condition | Colour Change |
|-------|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| Total Hardness | **Eriochrome Black T (EBT)** | 10.0 ± 0.1 (NH₄Cl–NH₄OH buffer) | **Wine red → Sky blue** |
| Calcium Hardness | **Murexide (Ammonium Purpurate)** | > 12 (NaOH) | **Pink/orange → Purple/Violet** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students **commonly lose 2 marks** by forgetting to report the hardness in **mg/L as CaCO₃** — they report raw titre values, which is a fatal error.
> 2. Another frequent mistake is using the **molecular weight of CaCO₃ (100)** instead of the **equivalent weight (50)** in calculations.
> 3. **Do not** confuse EBT and murexide indicators — students often swap the two and report Ca²⁺ as Mg²⁺ values, leading to full-mark deductions.
> 4. Always **mention the buffer pH** in the procedure — examiners award 1 mark specifically for this.

### Question A (14 Marks): Part (a) + Part (b) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Explain the EDTA method for the determination of **Total Hardness** of a water sample. List the reagents, apparatus, procedure, and the formula used.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Reagents Required:**
- Standard **0.01 M EDTA solution** (disodium salt dihydrate)
- **NH₄Cl–NH₄OH buffer** of pH 10.0 ± 0.1
- **Eriochrome Black T (EBT)** indicator

**2. Apparatus:** 50 mL burette, 250 mL conical flask, 50 mL pipette, measuring cylinder, white tile.

**3. Procedure:**

1. Rinse and fill the **50 mL burette** with 0.01 M EDTA solution.
2. Pipette **50 mL** of the water sample into a clean **250 mL conical flask**.
3. Add **1–2 mL of NH₄Cl–NH₄OH buffer** to bring the pH to 10.0 ± 0.1.
4. Add **2–3 drops of EBT indicator** — solution turns **wine red**.
5. Titrate against EDTA **slowly** with continuous swirling until the colour changes sharply from **wine red to clear sky blue**.
6. Note the **titre value** (V mL). Repeat for **concordant reading** (≤ 0.1 mL variation).

**4. Formula:**

$$\text{Total Hardness (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \frac{V \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_s}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A **100 mL** water sample required **22.5 mL of 0.02 N EDTA** for total hardness titration. A separate **100 mL** aliquot required **14.0 mL** of the same EDTA for calcium hardness (using murexide at pH 12). Calculate TH, CH, and MH. State whether the water is suitable for drinking as per **IS 10500:2012**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_s$ = 100 mL, $N_e$ = 0.02 N
- $V_{TH}$ = 22.5 mL, $V_{CH}$ = 14.0 mL

**Step 1 — Total Hardness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TH} &= \frac{22.5 \times 0.02 \times 50{,}000}{100} \\
&= \frac{22{,}500}{100} \\
&= 225 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calcium Hardness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH} &= \frac{14.0 \times 0.02 \times 50{,}000}{100} \\
&= \frac{14{,}000}{100} \\
&= 140 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Magnesium Hardness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MH} &= 225 - 140 = 85 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — IS 10500:2012 Comparison:**

| Parameter | Result | Permissible Limit | Status |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|--------|
| TH | 225 mg/L | 200 mg/L (A) / 600 mg/L (P) | **Exceeds acceptable limit** |
| CH | 140 mg/L | 75 mg/L (A) / 200 mg/L (P) | **Exceeds acceptable limit** |
| MH | 85 mg/L | 30 mg/L (A) / 100 mg/L (P) | **Exceeds acceptable limit** |

**Conclusion:** The water is **not suitable for drinking** without treatment (lime-soda softening or ion-exchange). It lies **within permissible limits in the absence of an alternative source** but exceeds the desirable limit.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Part (a) + Part (b) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Describe the procedure for the determination of **Calcium Hardness** separately. Why is **NaOH** used instead of the NH₄Cl–NH₄OH buffer in this titration?

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** At pH > 12 (highly alkaline), magnesium ions are precipitated as **Mg(OH)₂**, so only **Ca²⁺ ions remain in solution** to react with EDTA. **Murexide indicator** selectively forms a complex only with Ca²⁺ at this pH.

**Procedure:**

1. Pipette **50 mL** of water sample into a 250 mL conical flask.
2. Add **2 mL of 1 N NaOH solution** to raise pH above 12.
3. Add a small quantity (≈ 0.1 g) of **murexide indicator powder** — the solution turns **pink/orange**.
4. Titrate with **0.01 M EDTA** until the colour changes sharply to **purple/violet**.
5. Note the titre value ($V_{CH}$ mL).

**Calculation:**

$$\text{CH (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \frac{V_{CH} \times N_e \times 50{,}000}{V_s}$$

**Why NaOH and not NH₄Cl–NH₄OH buffer?**
- The buffer maintains pH at 10, where **both Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ remain soluble** — not selective.
- At **pH > 12**, Mg²⁺ quantitatively precipitates as **Mg(OH)₂** (Ksp = 1.5 × 10⁻¹¹), removing it from the reaction zone.
- This ensures EDTA titrates **only Ca²⁺** in the sample.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** **25 mL** of standard **CaCO₃ solution (0.45 g CaCO₃/L)** required **30 mL** of EDTA solution for standardization. **50 mL** of a water sample consumed **25 mL** of this EDTA. Calculate the **Total Hardness** in mg/L as CaCO₃.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concentration of Standard CaCO₃ Solution:**

$$0.45 \text{ g/L} = 450 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 2 — Mass of CaCO₃ in 25 mL Aliquot:**

$$\text{Mass} = \frac{450 \times 25}{1000} = 11.25 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 3 — Normality of EDTA:**

Since the equivalent weight of CaCO₃ is 50 g/eq:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{EDTA}} &= \frac{W}{V \times 50} \\
&= \frac{11.25}{30 \times 50} \\
&= \frac{11.25}{1500} \\
&= 0.0075 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Hardness of Sample:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TH} &= \frac{V \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_s} \\
&= \frac{25 \times 0.0075 \times 50{,}000}{50} \\
&= \frac{9375}{50} \\
&= 187.5 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Total Hardness of the given water sample is **187.5 mg/L as CaCO₃** — within the acceptable limit of IS 10500:2012.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Determination of Hardness (KTU 2024)**

- [x] **Definition:** Hardness = soap-precipitating capacity; expressed in **mg/L as CaCO₃**.
- [x] **Primary Cause:** Dissolved **Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺** ions.
- [x] **Method Prescribed by KTU:** **EDTA Complexometric Titration** (IS 3025 Part 21).
- [x] **Total Hardness** → EBT indicator → pH **10.0 ± 0.1** (NH₄Cl–NH₄OH buffer) → colour change **wine red → sky blue**.
- [x] **Calcium Hardness** → Murexide indicator → pH **> 12** (NaOH added) → colour change **pink/orange → purple/violet**.
- [x] **Magnesium Hardness** is **always derived**: **MH = TH − CH**.
- [x] **Master Formula:** $\text{Hardness} = \dfrac{V_e \times N_e \times 50{,}000}{V_s}$
- [x] **Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = 50** (NOT 100 — this is the molecular weight).
- [x] **1 mL of 0.01 M EDTA ≡ 1.0 mg CaCO₃** (working equivalence).
- [x] **EDTA is a secondary standard** — must be standardized against primary standard A.R.-grade CaCO₃.
- [x] **EDTA is a hexadentate ligand** forming **1:1 chelate** with metal ions.
- [x] **Acceptable limits (IS 10500):** TH = 200, CH = 75, MH = 30 mg/L as CaCO₃.
- [x] **Permissible limits (no alternative source):** TH = 600, CH = 200, MH = 100 mg/L as CaCO₃.
- [x] **Temporary Hardness** = removable by boiling; **Permanent Hardness** = not removable by boiling.
- [x] Always report results in **mg/L as CaCO₃** — examiners will not award full marks for raw mL values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Presumptive Coliform Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL609 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Presumptive Coliform Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
The **Presumptive Coliform Test** is the first stage of the standard **Multiple Tube Fermentation Technique (MTFT)** used in water and wastewater microbiology to obtain a presumptive enumeration of total coliform bacteria. The test is based on the principle that **coliform organisms ferment lactose** with the production of **acid and gas (CO₂ and H₂)** within a defined incubation period of **24 ± 2 hours to 48 ± 3 hours** at a temperature of **35 °C ± 0.5 °C**.

A positive presumptive test is indicated by the appearance of **gas (≥ 10% of the inverted Durham tube volume)** inside a small inverted fermentation tube (Durham tube) submerged in lactose broth. The numerical estimate of coliform density is reported as the **Most Probable Number (MPN) per 100 mL of sample**, statistically derived from the number of positive tubes across serial dilutions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are trying to estimate how many fish live in a vast, murky lake, but you cannot see them directly. Your strategy is to cast multiple fishing nets of different mesh sizes and count the catches. By analyzing how many nets caught fish, you can *statistically infer* the population density without counting every single fish.

The **Presumptive Coliform Test** works the same way: instead of netting fish, we are "netting" bacteria. We prepare several tubes of nutrient broth (lactose broth) at different sample dilutions (e.g., 10 mL, 1 mL, 0.1 mL). Bacteria that happen to land in a tube will ferment the lactose sugar, producing **gas bubbles** as a visible signal of their presence. By counting how many tubes show gas at each dilution, a statistical table (MPN table) gives us a smart estimate of the bacterial population in the original water sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Presumptive Test is **NOT a confirmation** of coliforms. It is a *presumptive* (educated guess) result. False positives can occur from anaerobic spore-formers, lactic acid bacteria, or yeasts. Confirmation is done in the subsequent *Confirmed* and *Completed* tests of the MTFT series.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Indicator Organism:** Total coliforms include genera *Escherichia*, *Citrobacter*, *Klebsiella*, and *Enterobacter*. Fecal coliforms (a subset) specifically grow at **44.5 °C ± 0.2 °C** in the confirmatory step using **E. coli (EC) broth**.

### 1.3 Core Physical Parameters & Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Incubation Temperature** | **35 °C ± 0.5 °C** | APHA Standard Methods 9221 |
| **Incubation Duration** | **24 ± 2 h** (first read) and **48 ± 3 h** (final) | APHA Standard Methods 9221 |
| **Media Used** | **Lactose Broth** (single-strength or double-strength) | APHA Standard Methods 9221 |
| **Indicator** | **Gas in Durham tube** (CO₂ + H₂) | APHA Standard Methods 9221 |
| **Reporting Unit** | **MPN Index per 100 mL** | Standard Microbiology |
| **Typical Dilution Series** | **10 mL, 1 mL, 0.1 mL** (5 tubes each) | APHA Standard Methods 9221 |
| **BOD of Dilution Water** | **< 0.2 mg/L** (seed water) | APHA Standard Methods |
| **Statistical Confidence** | **95% confidence limits** | Statistical Basis of MPN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drinking Water Standard (BIS 10500:2012 / WHO Guidelines):** Potable water must show **0 MPN per 100 mL** of total coliforms, and **0 MPN per 100 mL** of fecal coliforms (E. coli). Any detectable coliform in 100 mL is grounds for rejection of the supply.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Does the Test Work?
The biochemistry of the presumptive test rests on the following microbial pathway:

1. **Coliform bacteria possess the enzyme β-galactosidase**, which cleaves the disaccharide **lactose** into its monomers, **glucose** and **galactose**.
2. The resulting monosaccharides are metabolized through **glycolysis** (Embden-Meyerhof pathway), producing pyruvate.
3. Under anaerobic or partially anaerobic conditions in the fermentation tube, pyruvate is split by **formate hydrogen-lyase** into **formic acid (HCOOH)**, which is further cleaved into **carbon dioxide (CO₂) and hydrogen (H₂)** gases.
4. These gases accumulate in the inverted **Durham tube**, producing the visible bubble that signals a positive presumptive reaction.

The overall simplified reaction is:

$$C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{β-galactosidase}} 4\,C_3H_4O_3 + CO_2\uparrow + H_2\uparrow$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Lactose Broth (LB)?** LB is selective for lactose-fermenting bacteria. It contains beef extract, peptone, and lactose. A fermentation vessel with an inverted vial (Durham tube) traps the gas. Some protocols use **Brilliant Green Lactose Bile (BGLB) broth** for the confirmed test to suppress non-coliforms.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Multi-Tube Technique

The MTFT is a **limiting-dilution assay**. The logic is:

- **Step 1 — Series Dilution:** A water sample is diluted in decimal steps (10-fold: 10 mL → 1 mL → 0.1 mL) so that, statistically, the lowest dilution tubes contain many coliforms (most will turn positive) and the highest dilution tubes contain few or none (most will remain negative).
- **Step 2 — Replicate Inoculation:** **5 tubes per dilution** are inoculated to give statistical validity. Replicates are essential because bacterial distribution follows the **Poisson probability law**; a single tube would give no confidence interval.
- **Step 3 — Incubation:** Tubes are held at 35 °C for 24–48 hours. The 24-hour read is preliminary; the **48-hour read is the official endpoint** (some slow lactose-fermenters need extra time).
- **Step 4 — Observation:** Each tube is recorded as **+ (gas present)** or **− (no gas)**.
- **Step 5 — MPN Lookup:** The three-digit code (e.g., 5-3-2) is read against the **MPN Standard Table** to obtain the MPN per 100 mL with upper and lower 95% confidence limits.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Purpose & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **MPN Density (Thomas Formula Approximation)** | $MPN/100\,mL = \dfrac{P \times 100}{\sqrt{N \times T}}$ | Quick estimate when table unavailable (per 100 mL) |
| **Variables of Thomas Formula** | $P$ = # positive tubes; $N$ = volume in *negative* tubes; $T$ = total volume in *all* tubes | All volumes in mL |
| **Membrane Filtration Equivalent** | $CFU/100\,mL = \dfrac{Colonies \times 100}{Volume\,filtered\,(mL)}$ | Used in alternate MF method (per 100 mL) |
| **Bacterial Distribution Law** | $P(r) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{r}}{r!}$ | **Poisson** distribution — basis of MPN statistics |
| **95% Confidence Factor** | $CF_{95\%} \approx 3.3$ (lower) to $0.30$ (upper) of MPN | Approximate KTU-style confidence range |
| **Incubation Endpoint** | $t_{inc} = 48 \pm 3$ hours | KTU/APHA standard read time |
| **Sample Dilution Factor** | $DF = 10^{(n)}$ where $n$ = number of 1:10 dilutions | For adjusting MPN at higher dilutions |
| **Volume per Tube** | $V = 10$ mL, $1$ mL, $0.1$ mL | Standard series in 5-tube MPN |
| **Total Volume Assayed** | $T = 5V_{10} + 5V_{1} + 5V_{0.1} = 55.5$ mL | For standard 5-5-5 series |
| **Reporting Limit of Detection** | $\geq 2$ MPN per 100 mL | Lowest reliable MPN for 5-tube method |
| **MUG Fluorescence (E. coli)** | $4-Methylumbelliferyl\text{-}\beta\text{-}D\text{-}glucuronide$ cleavage → fluorescent | Used in modern confirmation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** When asked for the **MPN index**, the *table value* (e.g., 110 per 100 mL for a 5-3-1 result) is the official answer. The Thomas formula is an **approximation** useful for derivation-type questions. Always cite the **APHA Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater, 23rd Edition (2017)** methodology if asked for the source.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Environmental Engineering
- **Municipal Water Treatment:** Every batch of treated drinking water undergoes the MTFT series (Presumptive → Confirmed → Completed) before distribution.
- **Wastewater Compliance:** Treatment plants report **fecal coliform MPN** in effluent under the **EPA Clean Water Act NPDES** permits (typically < 200 MPN/100 mL for secondary effluent).
- **Swimming Pool & Recreational Water Safety:** Beach and pool monitoring uses MPN to enforce **recreational water quality guidelines**.
- **Bottled Water & Food Industry:** Routine microbiological QA/QC relies on the same MTFT logic for product release.
- **Disaster Response & Field Testing:** Field-deployable **Colilert® (Defined Substrate Technology)** is a modern rapid MPN alternative using the same statistical foundation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Laboratory Procedure (Board Exam Standard)

**Materials & Glassware Required:**
- **Lactose Broth (LB)** — single strength (for 1 mL and 0.1 mL inocula) and double strength (for 10 mL inocula, to maintain nutrient balance).
- **Inoculating tubes** (15 mm × 150 mm borosilicate) with **inverted Durham tubes** (10 mm × 75 mm).
- **Pipettes:** 1 mL and 10 mL sterile serological pipettes.
- **Autoclave** (sterilization at 121 °C, 15 psi, 15 minutes).
- **Bacteriological incubator** preset to 35 °C ± 0.5 °C.
- **Sample bottles**, **sterile dilution water** (phosphate-buffered, pH 7.2).
- **Test tube racks**, **markers**, **incubation logbook**.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Preparation of Media:** Dispense **10 mL of double-strength LB** into 5 tubes (for 10 mL inocula) and **10 mL of single-strength LB** into 10 tubes (for 1 mL and 0.1 mL inocula). Each tube must contain an inverted Durham tube filled with broth, with no air bubbles trapped.
2. **Sterilization:** Autoclave all tubes at **121 °C for 15 minutes at 15 psi**. After autoclaving, allow tubes to cool to room temperature. Durham tubes must still contain broth (no air bubble) — discard any with trapped air.
3. **Sample Collection:** Collect the water sample in a **sterile, glass-stoppered, autoclaved bottle**. For chlorinated samples, add **0.1 mL of 3% sodium thiosulfate** per 100 mL to neutralize chlorine. Transport on ice and process within **6 hours** (24 hours max if refrigerated ≤ 10 °C).
4. **Inoculation:**
   - 5 tubes of double-strength LB ← **10 mL of sample** each (final volume ≈ 20 mL)
   - 5 tubes of single-strength LB ← **1 mL of sample** each
   - 5 tubes of single-strength LB ← **0.1 mL of sample** each
5. **Incubation:** Place all 15 tubes in the incubator at **35 °C ± 0.5 °C** for **24 ± 2 hours**.
6. **First Read (24 h):** Examine each tube. Record tubes showing **gas bubble (≥ 10% of Durham tube volume)** as **POSITIVE (+)**. Return tubes to incubator.
7. **Final Read (48 h):** Re-examine at **48 ± 3 hours**. Any gas now present is recorded. **No further gas after 48 h counts as negative.** This is the **official presumptive result**.
8. **Recording:** Form a 3-digit code: number of positives at 10 mL dilution, 1 mL dilution, 0.1 mL dilution (e.g., **5-3-1**).
9. **MPN Determination:** Use the **MPN Standard Table** (APHA 9221) to read the MPN index per 100 mL along with **95% confidence limits**.
10. **Reporting:** Report as **"Presumptive MPN = X per 100 mL of sample"** along with the dilution scheme used.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Thomas' Approximation Formula

The **Thomas formula** (1942) is a simplified statistical estimate of MPN based on the assumption that positive tubes follow a **Poisson distribution** in the region of the limiting dilution.

**Derivation:**

Let:
- $P$ = total number of positive tubes (out of 15)
- $N$ = total volume (mL) of sample in *all negative tubes*
- $T$ = total volume (mL) of sample in *all 15 tubes*

The probability of a tube being negative is $P_0 = e^{-\lambda}$ where $\lambda$ is the expected number of bacteria per tube volume.

The proportion of negative tubes in the total is $\dfrac{N_{neg}}{N_{total}} = e^{-\lambda \cdot v}$, where $v$ is the average sample volume per tube.

Solving for the bacterial density $\rho$ (per mL) using Thomas' empirical derivation:

$$\rho \approx \dfrac{\ln(N_{total}/N_{neg})}{v_{eff}} = \dfrac{\ln(T/N)}{\text{avg tube volume}}$$

After empirical calibration, this simplifies to the working form:

$$MPN \text{ per } 100\,mL = \dfrac{P \times 100}{\sqrt{N \times T}}$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A water sample is tested using the 5-tube, 3-dilution MTFT. After 48 hours of incubation at 35 °C, the following results are obtained:

- **10 mL tubes:** All 5 tubes show gas (POSITIVE)
- **1 mL tubes:** 3 tubes show gas, 2 tubes show no gas
- **0.1 mL tubes:** 1 tube shows gas, 4 tubes show no gas

**Find:** (i) The MPN index per 100 mL using the standard table. (ii) Verify using Thomas' formula. (iii) State whether the water is fit for drinking.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Record the dilution series result:**

The result code is **5-3-1** (5 positives at 10 mL, 3 at 1 mL, 1 at 0.1 mL).

**Step 2 — Look up the MPN Table (5-5-5 series, 10 mL / 1 mL / 0.1 mL):**

From APHA Standard Methods 9221, the code **5-3-1** corresponds to:

$$MPN = 110 \text{ per } 100 \text{ mL sample}$$

with **95% confidence limits** of approximately **30 to 360 per 100 mL**.

**Step 3 — Verification by Thomas' Formula:**

Calculate the three variables:

$$P = 5 + 3 + 1 = 9 \text{ positive tubes}$$

$$N = (5-5)\times 10 + (5-3)\times 1 + (5-1)\times 0.1$$

$$N = (0)(10) + (2)(1) + (4)(0.1)$$

$$N = 0 + 2 + 0.4 = 2.4 \text{ mL}$$

$$T = (5)(10) + (5)(1) + (5)(0.1)$$

$$T = 50 + 5 + 0.5 = 55.5 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Thomas' Formula:**

$$MPN/100\,mL = \dfrac{P \times 100}{\sqrt{N \times T}}$$

$$MPN/100\,mL = \dfrac{9 \times 100}{\sqrt{2.4 \times 55.5}}$$

$$MPN/100\,mL = \dfrac{900}{\sqrt{133.2}}$$

$$MPN/100\,mL = \dfrac{900}{11.541}$$

$$MPN/100\,mL \approx 78 \text{ per } 100 \text{ mL}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Thomas formula gives **≈ 78 MPN/100 mL**, while the table value is **110 MPN/100 mL**. The table is the official, statistically refined value. Use the table in the exam unless the question specifically asks for Thomas' approximation.

**Step 5 — Drinking Water Suitability:**

As per **BIS 10500:2012 (Indian Standard Drinking Water Specification)** and **WHO Guidelines**, the coliform count in any 100 mL sample of drinking water must be **zero (0 MPN/100 mL)**.

Since **MPN = 110 per 100 mL ≫ 0**, the water sample **IS NOT FIT FOR DRINKING** and is heavily contaminated. It must be rejected or subjected to disinfection (chlorination, UV, or ozonation) and retested.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of MPN Results (BIS 10500:2012)

| MPN per 100 mL | Interpretation | Regulatory Action |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | **Safe / Potable** | Approved for distribution |
| **1 to 3** | **Doubtful quality** | Re-sample; investigate source |
| **3 to 10** | **Polluted** | Disinfection + re-test |
| **> 10** | **Heavily polluted** | Reject the source / supply |
| **> 100** | **Grossly contaminated (sewage-like)** | Immediate closure & emergency chlorination |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (Marks Deduction Alert):** Students often forget to mention that the MPN value must be reported **per 100 mL** (not per mL). Always write the unit. Also, the MPN is an **estimate, not a precise count** — words like "indication," "statistical estimate," or "probable number" are mandatory in answers to earn full marks.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Procedural Flowchart — Presumptive Coliform Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Water Sample Collection] --> B[Sterile bottle, 100 mL]
    B --> C[Add 0.1 mL 3% Na2S2O3 if chlorinated]
    C --> D[Transport on ice to lab within 6 h]
    D --> E[Prepare 15 LB tubes with Durham tubes]
    E --> F[Autoclave 121 C, 15 psi, 15 min]
    F --> G[Inoculate 5 tubes with 10 mL sample]
    G --> H[Inoculate 5 tubes with 1 mL sample]
    H --> I[Inoculate 5 tubes with 0.1 mL sample]
    I --> J[Incubate at 35 C plus minus 0.5 C]
    J --> K{Read at 24 h}
    K -->|Gas observed| L1[Mark as POSITIVE plus]
    K -->|No gas| L2[Mark as NEGATIVE minus]
    L1 --> M{Read at 48 h}
    L2 --> M
    M -->|Gas observed| N1[POSITIVE plus]
    M -->|No gas| N2[NEGATIVE minus]
    N1 --> O[Form 3-digit code: 10mL, 1mL, 0.1mL]
    N2 --> O
    O --> P[Look up APHA MPN Table]
    P --> Q[Report MPN per 100 mL]
    Q --> R[Compare with BIS 10500 Standard 0 per 100 mL]
    R --> S{MPN equals 0?}
    S -->|Yes| T1[POTABLE: Approved]
    S -->|No| T2[NOT POTABLE: Disinfect and retest]
    T1 --> U[End of Presumptive Test]
    T2 --> U
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MTFT Series Position

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1: PRESUMPTIVE Test]
        S1A[Sample in Lactose Broth] --> S1B[35 C, 24-48 h]
        S1B --> S1C{Gas?}
        S1C -->|Yes| S1D[Presumptive Positive]
        S1C -->|No| S1E[Presumptive Negative]
    end
    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2: CONFIRMED Test]
        S1D --> S2A[Streak on EMB Agar]
        S2A --> S2B[35 C, 24 h]
        S2B --> S2C{Metallic Sheen?}
        S2C -->|Yes| S2D[Confirmed Coliform]
    end
    subgraph Stage3[Stage 3: COMPLETED Test]
        S2D --> S3A[Inoculate LB Brilliant Green]
        S3A --> S3B[35 C, 48 h]
        S3B --> S3C{Gas plus Gram neg rod?}
        S3C -->|Yes| S3D[COMPLETED: Coliform Confirmed]
    end
    Stage1 --> Report[Final MPN Report per 100 mL]
    Stage2 --> Report
    Stage3 --> Report
```

### 4.3 Schematic of a Fermentation Tube (Physical Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Tube[Fermentation Tube Setup]
        T1[Lactose Broth Media - 10 mL]
        T2[Inverted Durham Tube - 10 mm x 75 mm]
        T3[Trapped Gas Bubble - CO2 plus H2]
        T1 --> T2
        T2 --> T3
    end
    Note1[Positive: Gas bubble fills 10 percent plus of Durham tube] -.-> T3
    Note2[Negative: No gas, broth remains clear] -.-> T1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** In a **positive tube**, the inverted Durham tube shows a clearly visible gas pocket (at least 10% of the small tube's volume). In a **negative tube**, the Durham tube is completely filled with broth with no air pocket. Always invert the fermentation tube gently before reading to dislodge any trapped air that may mimic a gas bubble.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "Presumptive Coliform Test." What medium and indicator are used in this test?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Presumptive Coliform Test is the first step of the **Multiple Tube Fermentation Technique (MTFT)** used to obtain a **presumptive Most Probable Number (MPN)** of coliform bacteria in a water sample. The medium used is **Lactose Broth (LB)**, dispensed in fermentation tubes fitted with an **inverted Durham tube**. The indicator is the **production of gas (CO₂ + H₂)** by coliforms fermenting lactose at **35 °C ± 0.5 °C** within **24–48 hours**. A positive result is a gas bubble filling **≥ 10%** of the Durham tube volume. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the Most Probable Number (MPN)? Why is the MPN value reported per 100 mL and not per mL?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Most Probable Number (MPN)** is a **statistical estimate** of the density of viable coliform bacteria in a water sample, derived from the **number of positive fermentation tubes** across serial dilutions, based on the **Poisson probability distribution** of bacterial occurrence. The MPN is reported per **100 mL** (not per mL) because **drinking water standards (BIS 10500:2012, WHO)** specify the permissible limit as **"0 MPN per 100 mL"** — this volume (100 mL) represents a reasonable sample size for detecting low-level contamination and is the standard unit in the APHA MPN table. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Answer **either** the full Q-A or the full Q-B.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **principle, medium, apparatus, procedure, and interpretation** of the Presumptive Coliform Test as per APHA Standard Methods. Include a labelled sketch of the fermentation tube setup. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):** The test is based on the ability of coliform bacteria to ferment **lactose** with the production of **acid and gas (CO₂ + H₂)**. The enzyme **β-galactosidase** cleaves lactose into glucose and galactose, which are metabolized through glycolysis into pyruvate, and finally split into gases by **formate hydrogen-lyase**.

**Medium (1 mark):** **Lactose Broth (LB)** — single strength for 1 mL and 0.1 mL inocula; **double strength** for 10 mL inocula. Each tube contains an **inverted Durham tube** to trap gas.

**Apparatus (1 mark):** 15 borosilicate fermentation tubes (15 mm × 150 mm) with Durham tubes, 5 mL and 10 mL sterile pipettes, autoclave, bacteriological incubator preset to 35 °C ± 0.5 °C, sterile sample bottles, and phosphate buffer dilution water.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Dispense LB into 15 tubes (5 of double strength for 10 mL sample, 10 of single strength for 1 mL and 0.1 mL samples).
2. Autoclave at 121 °C for 15 min.
3. Inoculate 5 tubes with 10 mL sample, 5 with 1 mL, 5 with 0.1 mL.
4. Incubate at 35 °C ± 0.5 °C.
5. Read at 24 h and 48 h; record gas-positive tubes.
6. Form 3-digit code and look up MPN table.

**Interpretation (1 mark):** MPN index from table is compared to the **BIS 10500 standard of 0 MPN/100 mL** to decide potability.

**(b)** A water sample is inoculated in the 5-5-5 MTFT series. After 48 h, the results are: **10 mL tubes — 4 positive, 1 negative; 1 mL tubes — 2 positive, 3 negative; 0.1 mL tubes — 0 positive, 5 negative.** Determine the MPN index per 100 mL. State whether the water is potable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Result Code:** 4-2-0

**Step 2 — MPN Table Lookup (5-5-5 series):** From APHA Standard Methods 9221, the code **4-2-0** corresponds to:

$$MPN = 22 \text{ per } 100 \text{ mL sample}$$

with 95% confidence limits of approximately **7 to 89 per 100 mL**.

**Step 3 — Verification using Thomas' Formula (extra credit):**

$$P = 4 + 2 + 0 = 6$$

$$N = (5-4)(10) + (5-2)(1) + (5-0)(0.1) = 10 + 3 + 0.5 = 13.5 \text{ mL}$$

$$T = (5)(10) + (5)(1) + (5)(0.1) = 50 + 5 + 0.5 = 55.5 \text{ mL}$$

$$MPN/100\,mL = \dfrac{6 \times 100}{\sqrt{13.5 \times 55.5}} = \dfrac{600}{\sqrt{749.25}} = \dfrac{600}{27.37} \approx 22 \text{ per } 100 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** MPN = 22 per 100 mL is **far greater than the BIS 10500:2012 limit of 0 MPN/100 mL**. The water is **NOT POTABLE** and is moderately contaminated, requiring **disinfection and re-testing** before any domestic use. (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **statistical basis of the MPN method**. Why are **5 replicate tubes** used per dilution? What is the role of the **Poisson distribution**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statistical Basis (3 marks):** The MPN method is based on the **Poisson probability distribution**, which models the random distribution of discrete particles (bacteria) in a liquid medium. When a small volume of sample is added to a tube, the number of bacteria transferred follows a Poisson distribution, where the probability of a tube receiving *k* bacteria is:

$$P(k) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{k!}$$

where $\lambda$ is the mean (expected) number of bacteria per tube volume. The probability of a tube receiving **zero bacteria** (and thus remaining negative) is $P(0) = e^{-\lambda}$. By measuring the *proportion of negative tubes* across a dilution series, one can statistically estimate $\lambda$ and back-calculate the bacterial density in the original sample.

**Why 5 Replicate Tubes? (2 marks):** Five replicates per dilution provide a **balance between statistical precision and practical workload**. With 5 tubes, the 95% confidence limits are reasonably narrow (factor of ~3.3 above and ~0.30 below the MPN). Fewer tubes (e.g., 3) yield wider confidence intervals; more tubes (e.g., 10) marginally improve precision but double the laboratory effort. The standard 5-5-5 series (15 tubes total) is the APHA-recommended compromise.

**Role of Poisson Distribution (2 marks):** The Poisson distribution accounts for the **stochastic (random) nature of bacterial dispersion** in liquid samples. Even in a well-mixed sample, the actual number of bacteria in any given aliquot is a random variable. The MPN method *embraces* this randomness and uses it constructively — the more tubes that turn negative, the lower the bacterial density. The MPN table is essentially a pre-computed inverse of the Poisson probability for the standard 5-5-5 series.

**(b)** Differentiate between the **Presumptive, Confirmed, and Completed** tests of the MTFT. State the medium, temperature, and positive indicator used in each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Stage | Medium Used | Temperature | Duration | Positive Indicator | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Presumptive** | **Lactose Broth (LB)** | **35 °C ± 0.5 °C** | **24–48 h** | **Gas in Durham tube** | Initial screening for coliforms |
| **Confirmed** | **Brilliant Green Lactose Bile (BGLB) Broth** | **35 °C ± 0.5 °C** | **24–48 h** | **Gas in Durham tube** | Confirms coliforms; suppresses non-coliforms |
| **Confirmed (Fecal)** | **E. coli (EC) Broth** | **44.5 °C ± 0.2 °C** | **24 h** | **Gas in Durham tube** | Specifically detects **fecal coliforms / E. coli** |
| **Completed** | **Nutrient Agar slant + Lactose Broth** | **35 °C ± 0.5 °C** | **24–48 h** | **Gram-negative non-spore-forming rod + gas from lactose** | Final confirmation of coliform identity |

**Key Points (full marks):**
- The **Presumptive** test is a *screening* step with possible false positives (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Streptococcus*).
- The **Confirmed** test uses **bile salts and brilliant green dye** to inhibit non-coliform Gram-positives and anaerobic spore-formers.
- The **Fecal Coliform** test uses a higher temperature (**44.5 °C**) which only **E. coli and related fecal species** can tolerate.
- The **Completed** test verifies the **morphology (Gram-negative rod), spore status, and re-confirmed gas production** — fulfilling **Koch's postulates** for the isolated organism.
- Only the **Completed test** is legally admissible as **definitive proof** of coliform presence. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always write **"MPN per 100 mL"**, never just "MPN." Marks are deducted for unit omission.
> 2. **Confusing Presumptive with Confirmed:** Many students mix up media. Presumptive uses **Lactose Broth**; Confirmed uses **BGLB** or **EC Broth**.
> 3. **Wrong Temperature for Fecal Test:** Fecal coliforms grow at **44.5 °C**, NOT 35 °C. Mixing these is a 1-mark deduction error.
> 4. **Thomas Formula vs Table Value:** Use the **MPN table value** (e.g., 110/100 mL) for the final answer. Thomas is an *approximation* — only quote it if the question explicitly asks.
> 5. **Not Mentioning Statistical Nature:** MPN is an *estimate*, not a precise count. Failing to mention "statistical estimate" or "based on Poisson distribution" costs a key 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Presumptive Coliform Test**

- ✅ **Test Purpose:** Presumptive enumeration of **total coliforms** in water using the **Multiple Tube Fermentation Technique (MTFT)**.
- ✅ **Medium:** **Lactose Broth (LB)** — double strength for 10 mL inocula, single strength for 1 mL and 0.1 mL.
- ✅ **Indicator:** **Gas (CO₂ + H₂)** trapped in an **inverted Durham tube**; positive = ≥ 10% gas volume.
- ✅ **Incubation:** **35 °C ± 0.5 °C** for **24 ± 2 h (first read)** and **48 ± 3 h (final official read)**.
- ✅ **Dilution Series:** Standard **5-5-5** arrangement — 5 tubes of 10 mL, 5 tubes of 1 mL, 5 tubes of 0.1 mL sample.
- ✅ **Result Code:** 3-digit code = (positives at 10 mL)(positives at 1 mL)(positives at 0.1 mL), e.g., **5-3-1**.
- ✅ **MPN Reporting Unit:** **MPN per 100 mL** (from APHA Standard Methods 9221 MPN table).
- ✅ **Thomas Formula:** $\text{MPN/100 mL} = (P \times 100) / \sqrt{N \times T}$ — approximation only.
- ✅ **Statistical Basis:** **Poisson distribution** — probability of zero bacteria in a tube: $P(0) = e^{-\lambda}$.
- ✅ **Drinking Water Standard (BIS 10500:2012):** **0 MPN per 100 mL** of total and fecal coliforms.
- ✅ **False Positives:** Anaerobic spore-formers (*Clostridium*), lactic acid bacteria, yeasts may produce gas.
- ✅ **Next Steps:** Presumptive (+) → **Confirmed test (BGLB broth, 35 °C)** → **Completed test** for full MTFT confirmation.
- ✅ **Fecal Coliform Indicator:** Growth at **44.5 °C ± 0.2 °C** in **EC broth** confirms *E. coli*.
- ✅ **Sample Preservation:** Use **3% Na₂S₂O₃** for chlorinated samples; analyze within **6 h** (or 24 h if ≤ 10 °C).
- ✅ **Equipment:** **Autoclave at 121 °C, 15 psi, 15 min** for sterilization of media and glassware.
- ✅ **Confidence Interval:** 95% confidence limits are reported alongside the MPN value.
